name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that orients users to equipment, services, resources, tools, and contact options, while exposing featured equipment categories, key tools, upcoming webinars, and live counts for quote, comparison, reading, and service inquiry lists.

core_features: Load header context via getSiteHeaderContext to display live counts and totals for the user's quote list, product comparison list, reading list, and service inquiry items.
Load homepage content via getHomePageContent to retrieve featured equipment categories, featured tools, and upcoming webinars.

Expose direct navigation from the homepage to the Equipment listing (including a specific route for DAF), Services, Resources (case studies and webinars), Tools (including the equalization tank calculator), Quote list, Reading list, Service inquiry, and Contact pages.
Allow users to drill into a featured equipment category (with explicit support for DAF) to pre-filter the equipment listing page using URL parameters.
Promote key tools and calculators, including a direct link to the Equalization Tank Sizing calculator when present in featured_tools.
Highlight upcoming webinars and provide direct navigation to their detail pages using the webinar resourceId, as well as a generic navigation to the webinars list.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Scan header context and open quote/reading/service lists:
1. User lands on index.html.
2. On page initialization, the client calls getSiteHeaderContext.
3. The header utility bar displays counts and totals for quote list items, comparison items, reading list items, and service inquiry items.
4. User clicks the quote list icon; the browser navigates to quote_list.html (no additional parameters) where the current quote list is shown.
5. Alternatively, user can click the reading list icon to navigate to reading_list.html or the service inquiry icon/link to navigate to service_inquiry.html.

Workflow 2 – Navigate directly to equipment and pre-filter for DAF:
1. User scrolls to the featured equipment categories section.
2. The client has already called getHomePageContent and rendered featured_equipment_categories.
3. User identifies the card whose category.slug corresponds to a DAF category.
4. User clicks this DAF category card.
5. The browser navigates to equipment.html with URL parameter categoryId set to the category.slug (for DAF, this is "daf"), enabling the equipment page to display DAF items by default.

Workflow 3 – Navigate to chemical treatment equipment from main navigation:
1. User locates the main navigation or a dedicated teaser for chemical treatment equipment on the homepage.
2. User clicks the Chemical Treatment teaser link.
3. The browser navigates to equipment.html with URL parameter section=chemical_treatment so the equipment page can pre-filter to chemical_treatment products.

Workflow 4 – Explore services and maintenance plans:
1. User locates the Services overview teaser on the homepage and clicks it.
2. Browser navigates to services.html (no extra parameters), where all services are shown.
3. Alternatively, user clicks a Maintenance Plans promo tile on the homepage.
4. Browser navigates to services.html with URL parameter serviceType=maintenance_plans so the services page can filter its view to maintenance plans.

Workflow 5 – Discover resources, case studies, and webinars:
1. User scrolls to a Resources navigation area on the homepage.
2. User clicks the Case Studies promo tile; the browser navigates to resource_list.html?resourceType=case_studies, pre-filtering the resource list to case studies.
3. Alternatively, user clicks the Webinars & Events promo tile; the browser navigates to resource_list.html?resourceType=webinars, pre-filtering the list to webinars.

Workflow 6 – Open a specific upcoming webinar from the homepage:
1. On initialization, getHomePageContent loads upcoming_webinars and the component renders a list of webinar cards.
2. User inspects webinar titles, dates, and brief metadata (all from ResourceItem fields).
3. User clicks a specific webinar card.
4. Browser navigates to resource_detail.html with URL parameter resourceId set to the clicked webinar's ResourceItem.id so that page can load the full webinar details and registration options.

Workflow 7 – Access tools and the equalization tank calculator:
1. On initialization, getHomePageContent loads featured_tools and the component renders them.
2. User clicks the generic Tools & Calculators promo tile; browser navigates to tools.html to show all available tools.
3. If one of the featured tools has Tool.slug equal to equalization_tank_sizing, it is rendered as a dedicated featured tool entry.
4. User clicks this Equalization Tank Sizing featured tool.
5. Browser navigates to calculator_equalization_tank.html (no extra parameters), where the calculator will handle its own internal logic.

Workflow 8 – Contact and service inquiry from the homepage:
1. User sees the Service inquiry link in the header and may click it to go to service_inquiry.html to assemble and submit a service inquiry.
2. User sees a Contact link in the header or footer and may click it to navigate to contact.html.
3. On contact.html, the user can submit a ContactRequest, but that functionality is outside the scope of the home page.

Workflow 9 – Return visits and updated header counts:
1. User previously added products to a quote list or resources to a reading list on other pages.
2. On a subsequent visit to index.html, getSiteHeaderContext is called again on initialization.
3. The header utility bar immediately reflects current counts and totals according to the current session/identity managed by the backend.
4. User can click through to the respective detailed list pages to continue their workflow.
interactions: On page load (index.html without required URL parameters), the client triggers getSiteHeaderContext and getHomePageContent and stores their responses into local page state (e.g., in-memory variables or a state store).

Clicking the quote list icon in the header navigates the user to quote_list.html, where the existing QuoteList and QuoteListItems for the user are managed; no URL parameters are required because the server associates the list with the user/session.

Clicking the comparison icon (if present in the header context) navigates the user to a comparison page (not explicitly defined in outgoing connections but implied by comparison_item_count); the home page simply exposes the count without directly mutating comparison data.
Clicking the reading list icon in the header navigates to reading_list.html, allowing the user to view ReadingList and ReadingListItem records summarized by getSiteHeaderContext.

Clicking the service inquiry link/icon in the header navigates to service_inquiry.html, where ServiceInquiry and ServiceInquiryItem details are managed; the home page only displays the current item count from getSiteHeaderContext.

Clicking a featured DAF equipment category card triggers navigation to equipment.html with a query parameter categoryId set to the category's slug, which for DAF will match the string "daf" as defined by the EquipmentCategory.slug enum.
Clicking the Chemical Treatment equipment teaser triggers navigation to equipment.html with URL parameter section=chemical_treatment, enabling the equipment listing page to filter by Product.section.
Clicking the Services overview teaser on the homepage navigates to services.html with no additional parameters so that all services, including MaintenancePlan entities, can be shown.

Clicking the Maintenance Plans promo tile navigates to services.html?serviceType=maintenance_plans to allow the Services page to filter its view to MaintenancePlan entries with service_type=maintenance_plans.
Clicking the Case Studies promo tile navigates to resource_list.html?resourceType=case_studies, ensuring that ResourceItem entries are filtered on resource_type=case_studies by the target page.
Clicking the Webinars & Events promo tile navigates to resource_list.html?resourceType=webinars, ensuring that ResourceItem entries are filtered on resource_type=webinars by the target page.

Clicking any upcoming webinar card navigates to resource_detail.html?resourceId=<ResourceItem.id>, where the resource detail page can use that identifier to load the full ResourceItem and support actions like WebinarRegistration.
Clicking the Tools & Calculators promo tile navigates to tools.html, where a list of Tool entities is displayed and can be further filtered or explored.

Clicking the Equalization Tank Sizing featured tool (identified by Tool.slug equalization_tank_sizing) navigates directly to calculator_equalization_tank.html, which will load and store EqualizationTankCalculation records as the user performs calculations.
Clicking Contact in the header or footer navigates to contact.html, where a ContactRequest can be submitted; the home page itself does not display or edit ContactRequest data.
page_functionality.state_logic: The home page (index.html) does not require or consume URL parameters for its own internal state; it always initializes in a default state based on the dynamic data returned by getSiteHeaderContext and getHomePageContent. State on this page is therefore derived solely from these two interfaces and is not persisted via URL parameters or localStorage. All cross-page navigation from the home page uses URL query parameters to define state for the destination pages, in line with the site-wide convention: equipment.html?categoryId=<slug> (for featured category navigation such as DAF), equipment.html?section=chemical_treatment (for the Chemical Treatment teaser), services.html?serviceType=maintenance_plans (for Maintenance Plans promo navigation), resource_list.html?resourceType=case_studies or resource_list.html?resourceType=webinars (for resource list filtering), and resource_detail.html?resourceId=<ResourceItem.id> (for specific webinar/resource detail). The user's working quote list, reading list, comparison session, and service inquiry are not stored or modified on the home page; instead, the page relies on getSiteHeaderContext to retrieve a snapshot summary of those lists, which is refreshed on each page load. Any changes to those underlying entities occur on their respective detailed pages and will be reflected on the home page when it is reloaded.

components.functionality: Calls getSiteHeaderContext when the home page initializes and exposes the result to header-related components so they can display live counts and totals for quote list items, comparison items, reading list items, and service inquiry items.

Displays header-level icons/links for Quote list, Comparison, Reading list, and Service inquiry using the data returned by header-context-loader. Shows item counts and, where applicable, total quote value and currency. Provides navigation to the corresponding detailed pages when icons or labels are clicked.
Calls getHomePageContent when the home page initializes and stores the resulting featured_equipment_categories, featured_tools, and upcoming_webinars for use by downstream components.

Renders a list/grid of featured equipment categories from homepage-content-loader. Each item is clickable and routes the user to the equipment listing page. For a category whose slug is 'daf', clicking navigates specifically to equipment.html?categoryId=daf as defined in the outgoing navigation rules. Implementations may also use the category slug or section for other categories, but the DAF case is explicitly supported.

Provides core navigation tiles or links for high-level sections: Equipment, Services, Resources (Case Studies and Webinars), Tools, and Contact. Uses explicit URL parameters for links that must pre-filter destination pages (e.g., resourceType, serviceType, section). Does not depend on dynamic content; instead, it defines the primary routing paths from the home page.

Displays the tools returned in featured_tools from homepage-content-loader. Each tool entry is clickable. If a tool has slug equal to 'equalization_tank_sizing', clicking that entry navigates to calculator_equalization_tank.html as required. Other tools navigate to the URL defined in Tool.url.

Displays upcoming webinars from the upcoming_webinars array returned by homepage-content-loader. For each ResourceItem (where resource_type is webinars), shows summary metadata such as title, time, and industry. Clicking a webinar card navigates to resource_detail.html with resourceId set to that ResourceItem.id. Optionally exposes a secondary "view all webinars" link that navigates to resource_list.html?resourceType=webinars.

Provides dedicated, always-available navigation links for resources: one to view all case studies and one to view all webinars/events. These links do not rely on dynamic data and always navigate with the appropriate resourceType query parameter.

Provides quick entry points into the Services section. One link navigates to services.html with no parameters (services overview). Another specifically navigates to services.html?serviceType=maintenance_plans so the Services page can pre-filter for MaintenancePlan entries.
Displays footer-level navigation links, including a Contact link that routes to contact.html. Ensures that users reaching the bottom of the home page still have immediate access to contact options.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width grid, approximately 2-column layout within a centered 900–980px container. Not a modern 12-column system, but conceptually a main-content column (~70–75%) and a left sidebar (~25–30%) with occasional full-width header and navigation rows.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a classic, boxed, fixed‑width layout centered on a patterned full‑width background. Inside the white container, content stacks in horizontal bands: (1) a top header band with the utility bar (and implied logo/context), (2) a full‑width navigation band composed of navigation tiles, (3) a two‑column content band where the left sidebar stacks functional lists (tools, webinars) and the right main column presents the primary homepage content (equipment category feature grid followed by narrow promotional strips), and (4) a full‑width footer band with contact links. Data loader components are aligned to the bands they serve and occupy the same spatial regions as their eventual content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a classic, boxed, fixed‑width layout centered on a patterned full‑width background. Inside the white container, content stacks in horizontal bands: (1) a top header band with the utility bar (and implied logo/context), (2) a full‑width navigation band composed of navigation tiles, (3) a two‑column content band where the left sidebar stacks functional lists (tools, webinars) and the right main column presents the primary homepage content (equipment category feature grid followed by narrow promotional strips), and (4) a full‑width footer band with contact links. Data loader components are aligned to the bands they serve and occupy the same spatial regions as their eventual content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header (header-context-loader + site-header-utility-bar) takes roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height, the navigation tiles band about 5–8%, and the main two‑column content band consumes the remaining 70–80%. Within this band, about 70–75% of the container width is assigned to the right main content column and 25–30% to the left sidebar. The footer-contact-links band spans the full container width and occupies a moderate height, similar to the navigation or slightly taller. Vertical gaps between bands and modules are modest (≈8–16px) to maintain a compact, information‑dense feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The design is intentionally compact and utilitarian. Elements sit fairly close together: headings are tight to the content beneath them, lists have limited line spacing, and sidebar modules pack multiple links in close vertical succession. The main equipment grid shows several categories in one view with modest internal padding, while the resources and services promo strips are slim, single‑row bands. White space is present mainly as narrow margins and paddings (≈8–12px) around cards and sections rather than large empty areas.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F‑pattern. The eye starts at the top left of the header band (company identity and status context), scans right across the utility bar, then drops to the full‑width primary navigation tiles and scans left‑to‑right again. From there, the gaze moves diagonally down to the top‑left of the main content column where the equipment-category-feature-grid begins, while the left sidebar’s featured tools and upcoming webinars are noticed peripherally. Reading then continues vertically down the main column through the equipment categories and into the horizontal resources and services promo strips, before eventually reaching the footer contact links. Red highlights on the active navigation tile and key list items in the sidebar serve as secondary attractors along this path.

layout.overall_description: On a patterned, full‑width background, a white, boxed container (around 900–980px wide) is centered on the page. At its very top, a relatively shallow header band stretches the full container width, containing the utility/status elements for the site; combined with the implied logo/context, this area visually anchors the page. Immediately beneath it, a horizontal bar of primary navigation tiles runs edge‑to‑edge inside the container like a classic corporate nav strip, each tile tightly spaced with a subtle gradient or beveled effect. Below the navigation, the container opens into a two‑column band: a narrower left sidebar and a wider right main column. The sidebar is a vertical stack of compact, boxy modules that surface featured tools and upcoming webinars, each module separated by a modest gap. The main column starts high on the right with an equipment category feature grid that visually dominates this area, listing several key equipment categories in a dense, card‑like fashion. Beneath this grid, two narrow but full‑width strips—one for resources and one for services—run horizontally across the main column, resembling slim promo bands with icons and concise text. Finally, at the bottom of the container, a full‑width footer band presents contact links in a compact, multi‑column arrangement. Everything scrolls together in a linear flow, with tight vertical spacing and conservative styling that emphasize clarity and information density over visual drama.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-context-loader occupies the uppermost content region inside the centered white container, directly beneath the patterned page background’s top edge. It shares the same horizontal bounds as the container (spanning nearly the full 900–980px width) and aligns flush with the left and right inner margins. Vertically, it matches the combined height reserved for header contextual elements (logo, company name, and status context), effectively forming an invisible scaffold behind the site-header-utility-bar. While loading, it can display a slim, full‑width loading indicator or placeholder within this header zone, but it does not extend beyond the header band or overlap the navigation tiles below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, located at the very top of the content stack within the container, occupying the same footprint as the header band; scrolls away with the page and does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The site-header-utility-bar is the topmost visible band inside the white container, sitting directly over the header-context-loader and running full width from the left inner edge to the right inner edge. It forms a shallow horizontal strip (roughly 10–15% of the container’s initial visible height) with a slightly beveled or gradient background in a conservative blue or grey, echoing the old‑generation corporate style. On the left side of this strip, aligned to the container’s left padding, there is room for the company name/logo and page context; on the right side, aligned to the right padding, live status indicators for quote, comparison, reading, and service inquiry lists are arranged horizontally with minimal gaps between them. Vertical padding is modest (~8px), keeping the bar compact. A narrow margin of about 8–12px separates the bottom of this bar from the navigation tiles band below, maintaining the stacked‑band pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non‑sticky header bar that scrolls away as the user scrolls down; always spans the full width of the central container and maintains a fixed height relative to its content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-content-loader occupies the entire two‑column content band beneath the primary navigation tiles. It stretches horizontally from the left inner edge of the container to the right inner edge, and vertically from the bottom edge of the navigation bar down to just above the footer-contact-links band. Within this region, it conceptually splits into two columns, with the left sidebar taking about 25–30% of the width and the right main content column taking the remaining 70–75%. While content is loading, it can display column‑aligned placeholders that mirror this structure—narrow stacked boxes on the left and a wider set of blocks on the right—without breaking the classic two‑column layout. The loader never overlaps the navigation above or the footer below; it merely fills the space between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, fills the main content area between navigation and footer; respects the same two‑column structure as the loaded content and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The equipment-category-feature-grid is the primary content block in the right main column of the two‑column band. It begins just below the navigation tiles, offset downward by a modest top margin of about 12–16px from the bottom of the nav bar. Horizontally, it is aligned to the right column, starting at roughly 30% of the container width (just to the right of the sidebar) and extending to the right inner edge, occupying about 70–75% of the container width. The grid’s title or heading sits flush left within this column, with minimal space above and below. Under the heading, equipment categories are arranged in a compact grid or stacked tiles—typically 2–3 columns per row within this right column—each card having small internal padding (≈8–12px) and sitting close to neighboring cards with narrow gutters. The overall height of this block can be substantial, filling much of the above‑the‑fold area on the right side and extending downward as necessary, with only a small vertical gap (≈12–16px) before the resources-promo-strip starts immediately beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored to the top of the main right column under the nav bar; expands vertically as items are added but does not affect the left sidebar’s fixed width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-navigation-tiles component forms a full‑width navigation band immediately below the site-header-utility-bar. It spans the entire width of the white container from left to right, with a height sufficient for a single row of tiles plus minimal vertical padding (~4–6px). Each tile represents a key area of the site (such as Equipment, Services, Resources, Tools, Contact) and is arranged horizontally in a tight row, with small, consistent gaps or border separators between items. The band’s background is a beveled or gradient bar, primarily blue with subtle shading, and the currently active tile uses a red highlight for its label or underline to echo the mockup’s accent color. Tiles are center‑aligned vertically within the bar, with moderate horizontal padding (~8–12px) inside each tile but little extra whitespace above or below the row. A slight drop shadow or top/bottom border gives the bar a subtle skeuomorphic feel. A narrow margin (~8–12px) separates this navigation band from the main two‑column content area below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full‑width navigation bar that scrolls offscreen as users move down; it is not sticky and remains in its position in the vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-tools-list sits in the upper section of the left sidebar within the main two‑column band. The sidebar itself occupies roughly 25–30% of the container width, starting at the left inner edge, and extends vertically from just below the navigation band down toward the footer. The featured-tools-list is the first module in this column, positioned near the top with a small top margin (≈12–16px) below the navigation bar. It appears as a narrow, boxed card with a subtle border or lightly shaded background, aligned flush left and right within the sidebar. The module’s heading sits at the top with minimal padding, followed by a dense vertical list of tool links, each on its own line with tight vertical spacing (~4–8px) and small left indentations or bullets/icons. Internal padding is modest (≈8–12px on all sides), and the list may extend downward, but it remains clearly bounded by its card’s border. A vertical gap of about 12–16px separates the bottom of this module from the upcoming-webinars-list module below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored to the top of the left sidebar; maintains a fixed width relative to the sidebar and scrolls with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The upcoming-webinars-list is stacked directly beneath the featured-tools-list within the left sidebar. It shares the same narrow column width (about 25–30% of the container) and aligns perfectly with the left and right edges of the sidebar. A modest vertical gap (≈12–16px) separates its top edge from the bottom of the featured-tools-list, visually distinguishing it as a separate module. Like the tools list, it appears as a compact card with a subtle border or faint background, using a heading bar or bold title at the top, followed by a vertically arranged list of upcoming webinar entries. Each entry may contain a date and title on one line or two tightly spaced lines, with minimal extra spacing between items to keep the module information‑dense. Internal padding is similar—about 8–12px on all sides—with narrow line-height to fit multiple webinars within a limited vertical space. The bottom of this module transitions into either additional sidebar content (if any) or the negative space above the footer, with only modest vertical separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the left sidebar stack; maintains its position beneath the featured-tools-list and scrolls normally with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-promo-strip is positioned in the right main column directly below the equipment-category-feature-grid. It begins at the same left alignment as the grid within the right column (around 30% of the container from the left edge) and extends to the right inner edge, fully spanning the width of the main column but not the entire container. A vertical gap of approximately 12–16px separates its top edge from the bottom of the equipment grid, making it feel like the next reference section. Visually, it is a slim, horizontal band with a light blue or white background and perhaps a thin top border, giving it a subtle callout appearance without appearing overly modern. An icon or small graphic sits on the left side of the strip, followed by a concise text description and a link or button, all aligned in a single line or two tightly spaced lines. The strip’s height is relatively shallow, with minimal top and bottom padding (~8px), reinforcing the compact, information‑focused style. Another narrow vertical gap (≈8–12px) separates this strip from the services-promo-strip below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored in the main right column; behaves like a horizontal callout just under the main feature grid and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-promo-strip follows immediately beneath the resources-promo-strip in the right main column. It uses the same horizontal bounds—starting where the main column begins (around 30% of the container width from the left) and extending to the right inner edge—creating a visually aligned pair of promotional bands. It is separated from the resources strip by a small vertical gap (~8–12px), maintaining the tight stacking of sections. Stylistically, it mirrors the resources strip: a slim, boxed band with a subtle border or contrasting light background (perhaps a pale grey) and minimal vertical padding (~8px). An icon or marker on the left leads into concise services‑focused copy and a link or small button, arranged in a single row or two tightly stacked lines. The strip’s shallow height and full main‑column width give it the feel of a secondary reference section—noticeable but not dominant compared to the equipment-category-feature-grid above. Below this strip, a modest vertical space (≈16px or more) leads into any additional main content or directly toward the footer area as the page ends.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, aligned to and stacked beneath the resources-promo-strip within the main right column; scrolls together with the surrounding content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer-contact-links component forms the bottommost visible band inside the white container. It spans the full width of the container from left inner edge to right inner edge and sits directly below the main two‑column content area, separated by a moderate top margin (~16–24px) that visually signals the end of the main content. The footer band has a slightly darker background (a deep blue or grey) with a subtle gradient or beveled top edge, in keeping with the conservative corporate style. Within this band, contact links are arranged in a dense, multi‑column layout: for example, 2–4 narrow vertical columns of links aligned left, with each column having tight vertical spacing (~4–8px) between items. The columns start near the left side of the container and may extend toward the center, leaving some breathing room on the far right or accommodating additional contact details. Vertical padding at the top and bottom of the band is modest (~12–16px), making the footer compact yet clearly distinct. This band visually anchors the page’s bottom and completes the boxed layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, fixed at the bottom of the content stack (not fixed to the viewport); always spans the full container width and scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of the page.

name: Equipment (Overview & Listing)
title: Equipment
description: Page for browsing industrial wastewater treatment equipment by category, with optional deep filtering and sorting. The page works in two modes: an overview of equipment sections and categories, and a product listing when a specific equipment category is selected.

core_features: Display equipment sections and categories using getEquipmentSectionsAndCategories, optionally focused by the 'section' URL parameter.
Switch into category listing mode when a 'categoryId' URL parameter is present, using listProducts to fetch paginated products for that category.
Provide dynamic filter controls (for price, flow capacity, plant flow capacity, tank volume, etc.) using configuration from getEquipmentFilterOptions for the active category.
Support sorting of product results using sort options from getEquipmentFilterOptions and the 'sort' URL parameter.
Support pagination of product results using listProducts with the 'page' URL parameter and reflecting total_results/page_size.
Allow navigation from equipment product cards to the detailed product page (product.html) by passing the selected product.id as a URL parameter.
Display and update header context (quote list items, comparison item counts, reading list, service inquiry counts) using getSiteHeaderContext.
Display a comparison status bar with current comparison session and item count using getComparisonStatus, and navigate to compare.html on user request.
Accept equalization tank calculator recommendations via 'tankSeriesId' and 'minTankVolumeM3' URL parameters and apply them as pre-filters when listing equalization tank products.
user_workflows: Overview browsing from header: (1) User clicks 'Equipment' in the main header navigation without URL parameters. (2) The page reads the URL (no categoryId), calls getSiteHeaderContext and getEquipmentSectionsAndCategories. (3) The overview of sections and categories is displayed. (4) User optionally focuses a section (manually or via a 'section' URL parameter). (5) User clicks a category; the page updates the URL to include categoryId (and removes page/tank-specific parameters unless explicitly set) and re-initializes into listing mode.

Section-focused overview from home: (1) User clicks an equipment section link on index.html that navigates to equipment.html?section=<section>. (2) Page initialization reads 'section', calls getSiteHeaderContext and getEquipmentSectionsAndCategories. (3) The overview displays all sections but programmatically focuses or scrolls to the section matching the URL parameter. (4) User selects a category, which updates the URL with categoryId and clears or preserves 'section' according to navigation design, then reloads into listing mode.

Category listing via direct link: (1) User arrives from index.html or tools.html on a link such as equipment.html?categoryId=<categoryId>. (2) Page initialization detects categoryId and switches to listing mode. (3) The page calls getSiteHeaderContext, getEquipmentFilterOptions(categoryId), listProducts(categoryId, filters derived from URL, sort_by from 'sort', page from 'page'), and getComparisonStatus. (4) The user sees category-specific filters, sort options, and a paginated product list. (5) User interacts with filters or sort; the page updates the URL parameters accordingly and re-runs listProducts.

Equalization tank recommendation follow-up: (1) User completes a calculation on calculator_equalization_tank.html, then clicks a link to view suitable equalization tanks, which navigates to equipment.html?categoryId=equalization_tanks&tankSeriesId=<id>&minTankVolumeM3=<value>. (2) Page initialization detects categoryId, tankSeriesId, and minTankVolumeM3, switches to listing mode, and calls getEquipmentFilterOptions and listProducts with filters that include the tankSeriesId and minimum tank volume derived from the URL. (3) The page indicates that filters are pre-applied from the calculator (using only URL state and listProducts results). (4) User refines filters or clears them; the page updates URL parameters, resets page to 1, and re-fetches listProducts.

Filtering and sorting within a category: (1) In listing mode, user adjusts a filter control (e.g., price range, flow capacity, plant flow capacity, tank volume) that is configured based on getEquipmentFilterOptions. (2) The filter-sidebar component updates the corresponding filter parameters in the URL query string (while preserving categoryId and other relevant parameters) and resets 'page' to 1. (3) The page controller reacts to the URL change, recomputes the filters object, and calls listProducts with the updated filters and current 'sort'. (4) User changes the sort selection; the sort control updates the 'sort' URL parameter and resets 'page' to 1, triggering another listProducts call.

Pagination within a category: (1) After filters and sort are applied, listProducts returns total_results, page, and page_size. (2) The pagination-controls component displays available page numbers based on these values. (3) User clicks a different page index; the component updates the 'page' URL parameter while preserving categoryId, filters, and sort. (4) The page controller reads the updated URL, calls listProducts with the new page value, and replaces the displayed product list.

Navigating to product details: (1) User reviews the product list; each product is represented as a clickable card. (2) User clicks a product card or a dedicated 'View details' interaction. (3) The product-card component navigates to product.html?productId=<product.id> using the product.id from listProducts. (4) The new page (product.html) handles detailed presentation; equipment.html does not maintain additional state about the detail view.

Using the comparison status bar: (1) The page initializes and calls getComparisonStatus to retrieve the current comparison_session and comparison_item_count. (2) The comparison-bar component displays the number of products currently in comparison and a subset of their names. (3) When comparison_item_count indicates available items, user clicks the 'Compare' action on the bar. (4) The component navigates to compare.html without additional parameters. (5) Any changes to the comparison session made elsewhere (e.g., via product interactions) will be reflected when getComparisonStatus is called again on this page (e.g., on reload or URL change).

Using header quote list and counts: (1) On initialization, site-header components call getSiteHeaderContext. (2) Header icons display counts and total values (quote_list_item_count, quote_list_total_value, comparison_item_count, reading_list_item_count, service_inquiry_item_count) to give context while browsing equipment. (3) When user clicks the quote list icon, the browser navigates to quote_list.html. (4) When user clicks comparison counts in the header, they navigate to compare.html; the page itself does not modify the comparison session.

Returning to higher-level navigation via breadcrumbs: (1) In listing mode, breadcrumb-nav uses current category information (from listProducts.category or from getEquipmentSectionsAndCategories) to render a hierarchy such as Home > Equipment > [Section] > [Category]. (2) User clicks 'Home' to navigate to index.html, or 'Equipment' to navigate to equipment.html without URL parameters (overview mode). (3) If user clicks the section crumb, the page navigates to equipment.html?section=<section>; if user clicks the category crumb while already in that category, no navigation or a refresh with the same URL occurs.
interactions: Section selection: User clicks on a high-level equipment section label in overview mode. The section-category-overview component updates the URL with 'section=<selectedSection>' (without categoryId), causing the page to re-render the overview while focusing that section. No API calls beyond getEquipmentSectionsAndCategories are needed for this interaction, unless a full page reload triggers them again.

Category selection: User clicks on a category within a section. The URL is updated to include 'categoryId=<selectedCategoryId>' and to remove or preserve 'section' based on navigation design. The page switches to listing mode, which triggers getEquipmentFilterOptions(categoryId), listProducts(categoryId, derived filters, current sort, page), and getComparisonStatus.

Filter control changes: When a user interacts with any filter (price, flow capacity, plant flow capacity, tank volume, or filters derived from tankSeriesId/minTankVolumeM3), the filter-sidebar component updates the corresponding query parameters in the URL (e.g., price_min, price_max, flow_min_m3h, flow_max_m3h, plant_flow_min_m3h, plant_flow_max_m3h, tank_volume_min_m3, tank_volume_max_m3, tankSeriesId, minTankVolumeM3). It then resets 'page' to 1 in the URL. The page controller reads the URL and passes a filters object to listProducts that is entirely derived from the current query parameters and the configuration returned by getEquipmentFilterOptions.

Sort selection: User chooses a sort option from a dropdown populated using sort_options from getEquipmentFilterOptions. The listing-toolbar component updates 'sort=<selectedSortValue>' in the URL and resets 'page' to 1. The page controller then calls listProducts with sort_by set to the 'sort' URL parameter.

Pagination click: User clicks a page number or next/previous control in the pagination-controls component. The component updates the 'page' parameter in the URL to the selected index, preserving categoryId, filters, and sort. The page controller reads the updated 'page' and calls listProducts with the new page value.

Clear filters: User clicks a 'Clear filters' action in the filter-sidebar or listing-toolbar. The component removes filter-related query parameters from the URL (retaining categoryId and sort as needed) and sets 'page' to 1. The page controller then calls listProducts with an empty or default filters object matched to the active category and the current sort.

Product card click: User clicks anywhere on a product card that is configured as a navigation affordance. The product-card component uses the bound Product.id to construct a navigation URL product.html?productId=<Product.id> and performs a location change.

Comparison bar click: User clicks the main action in the comparison-bar (shown when comparison_item_count > 0 in getComparisonStatus). The component navigates to compare.html. Any optional interactions like removing an item from comparison would dispatch events that are handled by other parts of the system; this page only refreshes its comparison view by re-calling getComparisonStatus when reloaded or otherwise instructed.

Header icon clicks: User clicks the quote list, comparison, reading list, or service inquiry icons in the header. Based on the site-header-context configuration and getSiteHeaderContext result, the header triggers navigation to quote_list.html, compare.html, or other target pages defined globally. Equipment.html does not change header state directly; it only displays the counts it receives.

Breadcrumb clicks: In listing mode, user clicks breadcrumb elements. Clicking 'Home' navigates the browser to index.html. Clicking 'Equipment' navigates to equipment.html with no URL parameters (overview). Clicking a section crumb navigates to equipment.html?section=<section>. Clicking the category crumb keeps the user on the current URL, optionally re-triggering the current listing logic.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is driven entirely by URL parameters. There is no use of localStorage or other persistent client-side storage for state.

Mode determination:
- Overview mode is active when no 'categoryId' query parameter is present. In this mode, the page calls getSiteHeaderContext and getEquipmentSectionsAndCategories. It does not call getEquipmentFilterOptions or listProducts.
- Listing mode is active when 'categoryId' is present in the URL. In this mode, the page calls getSiteHeaderContext, getEquipmentFilterOptions(categoryId), listProducts(categoryId, filters, sort_by, page), and getComparisonStatus.

URL parameters:
- section (string): When present and no categoryId is specified, the page remains in overview mode and focuses the overview on the specified section (e.g., by ordering, expanding, or scrolling), but still displays all sections retrieved by getEquipmentSectionsAndCategories. When categoryId is present, 'section' is optional; it may be used by breadcrumb-nav and navigation logic but does not affect the listProducts call directly.
- categoryId (string): When present, the page switches to listing mode. categoryId is passed to getEquipmentFilterOptions and listProducts. The category metadata from these calls is used to derive labels and breadcrumbs. When categoryId changes, page is reset to 1 and filter parameters may be cleared or preserved based on navigation design, but the authoritative source is the URL.
- tankSeriesId (string): Used primarily in the context of equalization tank listings. When present in the URL along with a suitable categoryId (such as an equalization_tanks category), the filter-sidebar and page controller include tankSeriesId as part of the filters object passed to listProducts. If tankSeriesId is present but the active category lacks relevant tank_series filters (according to getEquipmentFilterOptions), the parameter is ignored for filtering but may still be used for display (e.g., indicator that a recommendation exists).
- minTankVolumeM3 (number): When present and relevant for the active category (based on getEquipmentFilterOptions.tank_volume_range_m3), it is treated as a lower bound for tank volume in the filters passed to listProducts. The filter-sidebar reads this URL parameter on initialization to pre-populate volume filter controls. If the user changes volume filters, minTankVolumeM3 is updated or removed from the URL according to the new selection.
- sort (string): Represents the selected sort key. It must correspond to one of the values returned by getEquipmentFilterOptions.sort_options for the current category. On initialization, listing-toolbar reads 'sort' from the URL; if it's absent or invalid for the category, a default from sort_options is assumed (but not hardcoded; it is determined from the API response and the implementation). When sort changes, 'sort' is updated in the URL and page is reset to 1.
- page (number): Represents the current pagination index. On initialization in listing mode, the page controller reads 'page' (defaulting to the first page if missing or invalid) and passes it to listProducts. When filters or sort change, 'page' is reset to 1 and this is reflected in the URL. When pagination-controls changes the page, it updates 'page' in the URL and triggers a new listProducts call.

Filters object passed to listProducts:
- The filters parameter in listProducts is constructed entirely from the current URL query string in conjunction with the ranges and fields specified by getEquipmentFilterOptions for the active category. For example, if getEquipmentFilterOptions.flow_capacity_range_m3h is defined, the page may map URL parameters such as flow_min_m3h and flow_max_m3h into filters.flow_capacity_min_m3h and filters.flow_capacity_max_m3h. Similarly, price-related URL parameters are mapped into filters.price_min and filters.price_max within the limits defined by price_range, plant-flow-related URL parameters into filters.plant_flow_capacity, and tank-volume-related URL parameters into filters.tank_volume. The exact shape of the filters object is implementation-specific but must be derived deterministically from the URL and the filter options response.

State updates:
- Any user interaction that changes filters, sort, page, or category updates the URL first. The page logic responds to URL changes (either through full reloads or a route listener) by recomputing the state and re-calling the relevant interfaces. This ensures that the URL at any time fully represents the current state of the page, allowing bookmarking and sharing.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width grid, approximately 2-column layout within a centered 900–980px container. Not a modern 12-column system, but conceptually a main-content column (~70–75%) and a left sidebar (~25–30%) with occasional full-width header and navigation rows.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same classic stacked-band structure as the mockup: a full-width patterned background, with a centered fixed-width white container (~960px). Inside the container, content is arranged top-to-bottom in distinct horizontal bands: (1) logo/header strip; (2) full-width main navigation; (3) two-column content band. Within the two-column band, the left sidebar is a tall, narrow column (~25–30% width) with stacked compact modules (category tree, quick filters, highlighted equipment, small help/info block). The right main column (~70–75% width) is the primary content area, switching between two modes: (a) Overview mode: stacked section headings with short descriptive paragraphs and occasional small icon or thumbnail rows; (b) Listing mode: a dense list of equipment items in vertical sequence, with each item presented as a compact card row (name, key data, small thumbnail), and optional inline filter summary at the top.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same classic stacked-band structure as the mockup: a full-width patterned background, with a centered fixed-width white container (~960px). Inside the container, content is arranged top-to-bottom in distinct horizontal bands: (1) logo/header strip; (2) full-width main navigation; (3) two-column content band. Within the two-column band, the left sidebar is a tall, narrow column (~25–30% width) with stacked compact modules (category tree, quick filters, highlighted equipment, small help/info block). The right main column (~70–75% width) is the primary content area, switching between two modes: (a) Overview mode: stacked section headings with short descriptive paragraphs and occasional small icon or thumbnail rows; (b) Listing mode: a dense list of equipment items in vertical sequence, with each item presented as a compact card row (name, key data, small thumbnail), and optional inline filter summary at the top.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve ~15–20% of the viewport height for the header/logo and imagery at the very top and ~5–8% for the navigation bar directly beneath it, both spanning the full width of the central container. The remaining ~70–80% is devoted to the two-column content. Horizontally, assign ~25–30% of the content band to the left sidebar and ~70–75% to the main equipment content on the right. Within the main column, allocate a narrow strip at the top (about one card height) for mode-specific context: in overview mode, a short intro block; in listing mode, a compact filter summary and sort bar. Below that, the majority of vertical space is for text sections (overview mode) or stacked item rows (listing mode). Vertical spacing between modules and sections stays modest (8–16px), with 8px between tightly related elements (titles and their lists, labels and inputs) and 12–16px between distinct blocks (e.g., between filter block and listing).
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a compact, information-heavy feel: paragraphs use tight leading, with minimal spacing between consecutive paragraphs in the same section. Lists of equipment items are vertically compact: each item uses a single row or short block with 8–12px padding, small thumbnail on the left, text on the right, and little whitespace between items (8–12px). Sidebar modules are dense, with short titles, link lists, and compact filter controls stacked tightly. Internal paddings for sections and cards are 8–12px all around; navigation items have about 8–12px horizontal padding and 4–6px vertical padding. There are no large hero spaces; the emphasis is on fitting many equipment categories, filters, and items within one screen while keeping it legible.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the F-pattern: the eye starts at the top-left logo and company name, scans right across any header imagery, then moves down to the horizontal navigation bar, scanning left-to-right again. From there, attention drops diagonally into the main column heading (e.g., 'Industrial equipment' or a specific category name) just above the main content, while the left sidebar is noticed peripherally as a column of supportive navigation and filters. Reading then continues downward through either the overview sections (each beginning with a blue heading and small icon followed by dense paragraphs and occasional icon/thumbnail rows) or through the listing of equipment items (each row starting with a bold, blue equipment name). Red highlights are reserved for the active navigation item, currently selected category, and key sidebar emphasis (e.g., featured item or critical filter), acting as secondary visual anchors along the main vertical reading path.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a fixed-width, centered white box (~960px wide) against a subtly patterned background, conveying a conservative corporate feel. Along the very top of this box runs a full-width header band: the company logo and name are aligned to the left, with any industrial imagery (pipes, tanks, water treatment visuals) filling the space to the right. Below this, a beveled or slightly gradient navigation bar spans the full container width, with compact blue text links and a single red-highlighted 'Equipment' tab to mark the current section. Under the navigation, the layout transitions into a two-column band that occupies the rest of the visible page: a narrow left sidebar and a wider main content area on the right, both reaching downward together. The left sidebar is built of stacked, lightly framed modules with small headings and dense content—categories list, filters, and small reference links—all separated by modest 8–16px gaps. The main content column begins with a bold blue heading and a brief intro or filter summary, then flows downward into either descriptive overview sections (each with blue strip-style headings and text-heavy paragraphs) or a vertical listing of equipment items, each row containing a small thumbnail, equipment name, and key specs. The page as a whole feels compact and utilitarian, with restrained color, subtle gradients, and a clear F-shaped reading pattern that moves from the top-left logo, across navigation, then down through the main equipment content while glancing toward the sidebar for context and tools.

name: Item Detail (Products & Maintenance Plans)
title: Item Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a single equipment product or maintenance plan, with actions to add equipment to a quote list, add equipment to a comparison list, and add maintenance plans to a service inquiry.

core_features: Determine whether to show an equipment product or a maintenance plan based on URL parameters (productId or maintenancePlanId) and load the corresponding details via getProductDetails or getMaintenancePlanDetails.
Display item summary information (name, key specs, description, image) and detailed technical or commercial data using the Product or MaintenancePlan models.
Show NaOH consumption and other relevant metrics for pH neutralization systems to support comparison and quote decisions when product_type indicates a pH neutralization system.
Provide a breadcrumb navigation that links back to the appropriate listing (equipment.html with categoryId for products, services.html with serviceType=maintenance_plans for maintenance plans).
For equipment products, allow users to set a quantity and add the product to their quote list via addProductToQuoteList, and expose a navigation action to open quote_list.html after items are added.

For equipment products that support comparison, allow users to add the product to a comparison session via addProductToComparison, and expose a persistent comparison status bar that can navigate to compare.html.

For maintenance plans, allow users to set a quantity and add the plan to their service inquiry via addMaintenancePlanToServiceInquiry, and expose a navigation action to open service_inquiry.html after plans are added.

Fetch and show header-level context (counts and totals for quote list, comparison, reading list, and service inquiry) via getSiteHeaderContext so that header icons and badges reflect current user state.
Fetch and show comparison status via getComparisonStatus so the user can see how many items are currently in the comparison list and navigate to compare.html.
Handle unavailable or inactive items by showing status messages and disabling actions when Product.status or MaintenancePlan.status indicates that the item is not active.
user_workflows: View an equipment product and add it to a quote list: 1) User lands on product.html with a productId URL parameter. 2) Page loads header context using getSiteHeaderContext and loads product details using getProductDetails(productId). 3) User reviews summary and technical data. 4) User adjusts the quantity field in the quote action bar. 5) User clicks the 'Add to quote list' button, which calls addProductToQuoteList(productId, quantity). 6) On success, the page updates local quote-list state from the response, optionally refreshes header indicators, shows a confirmation message, and enables a 'View quote list' navigation action that links to quote_list.html.

Add a product to comparison and navigate to the comparison page: 1) User is viewing a product with productId and the product is available for comparison. 2) The comparison status bar is initialized using getComparisonStatus to display the current comparison item count. 3) User clicks 'Add to comparison', triggering addProductToComparison(productId). 4) On success, the page updates the comparison status bar using the returned items and comparison_item_count and/or by re-calling getComparisonStatus. 5) User clicks the comparison bar (e.g., a button labeled with the current item count), which navigates to compare.html.

View a maintenance plan and add it to a service inquiry: 1) User lands on product.html with a maintenancePlanId URL parameter. 2) Page loads header context via getSiteHeaderContext and maintenance plan details via getMaintenancePlanDetails(maintenancePlanId). 3) User reviews plan summary, coverage, response time, and annual cost. 4) User adjusts quantity in the service inquiry action bar. 5) User clicks 'Add to service inquiry', triggering addMaintenancePlanToServiceInquiry(maintenancePlanId, quantity). 6) On success, the page updates service-inquiry related state from the response, optionally refreshes header indicators, shows a confirmation message, and enables or highlights a 'View service inquiry' navigation action that links to service_inquiry.html.

Navigate back to relevant listings using breadcrumbs: 1) User is viewing a product loaded via getProductDetails. 2) The breadcrumb component displays a link to the equipment listing using the product's category_id. 3) User clicks the breadcrumb link, which navigates to equipment.html with categoryId set to product.category_id. 4) For maintenance plans, the breadcrumb displays a link back to services.html with serviceType set to maintenance_plans, and clicking it navigates to that listing.

Use header icons to access global lists: 1) On any item detail view, the header context component calls getSiteHeaderContext to get counts and totals. 2) The header shows numeric badges for quote list items, comparison items, reading list items, and service inquiry items. 3) After the user adds a product to a quote list or comparison list, or adds a maintenance plan to a service inquiry, the page updates the relevant header badge state using data returned from addProductToQuoteList, addProductToComparison, or addMaintenancePlanToServiceInquiry. 4) When the user clicks the quote list icon, the page navigates to quote_list.html; when they click the comparison icon, it navigates to compare.html; when they click the service inquiry icon, it navigates to service_inquiry.html.

Handle invalid or unavailable items: 1) If neither productId nor maintenancePlanId is present in the URL, or if the requested item cannot be loaded (e.g., getProductDetails or getMaintenancePlanDetails returns an error), the page displays an error state with an appropriate message and does not render quote/comparison/service inquiry actions. 2) If the item loads but its status indicates it is not active, the page shows a status banner and disables buttons that would add it to quote, comparison, or service inquiry.
interactions: Changing item type by URL parameter: User or navigation sets the browser URL to product.html?productId=... or product.html?maintenancePlanId=..., which triggers the page to re-initialize, detect which parameter is present, and call the appropriate detail interface (getProductDetails or getMaintenancePlanDetails).

Quote quantity input for products: User enters or adjusts a numeric quantity in the product quote action bar; this changes internal state used when calling addProductToQuoteList. Validation ensures that only valid numeric input is accepted before enabling the 'Add to quote list' button.

Add product to quote list: User clicks the 'Add to quote list' button, triggering addProductToQuoteList with the current productId and quantity. The UI handles loading and error states, and on success, updates quote list count in the header context and enables navigation to quote_list.html.

Add product to comparison: User clicks the 'Add to comparison' button or toggle, triggering addProductToComparison(productId). On success, the comparison status bar is updated with the new comparison_item_count and list of products, and a 'Compare' navigation element becomes visible or shows the updated count. If the operation fails, an error message is displayed.

Open comparison page from status bar: User clicks the comparison status bar or a 'Compare' button when comparison_item_count is greater than zero. This action navigates the browser to compare.html without additional parameters (the comparison session is resolved server-side).

Service inquiry quantity input for maintenance plans: User enters or adjusts a numeric quantity in the maintenance plan action bar; this value is held in component state and used when calling addMaintenancePlanToServiceInquiry.

Add maintenance plan to service inquiry: User clicks the 'Add to service inquiry' button, triggering addMaintenancePlanToServiceInquiry(maintenancePlanId, quantity). On success, the page updates service inquiry-related counts in the header (using data from the response) and surfaces a 'View service inquiry' navigation action that links to service_inquiry.html.

Navigate to quote list: After at least one successful call to addProductToQuoteList, the user can click a 'View quote list' link or the quote list icon in the header; this triggers navigation to quote_list.html.

Navigate to service inquiry: After at least one successful call to addMaintenancePlanToServiceInquiry, the user can click a 'View service inquiry' link or the service inquiry icon in the header; this triggers navigation to service_inquiry.html.

Breadcrumb navigation: User clicks a breadcrumb segment representing the equipment category or maintenance plan listing. For products, this navigates to equipment.html with categoryId set to the current product.category_id; for maintenance plans, this navigates to services.html with serviceType set to maintenance_plans.

Refresh header context: On initial page load (and optionally after successful add-to-quote, add-to-comparison, or add-to-service-inquiry actions), the header component invokes getSiteHeaderContext to update numeric indicators for quote list, comparison list, reading list, and service inquiry.

Handle unavailable actions based on availability flags: If getProductDetails or getMaintenancePlanDetails indicates that an item is not available for quote, comparison, or service inquiry (via is_available_for_quote, is_available_for_comparison, or status fields), the corresponding buttons are disabled or hidden and user interaction does not trigger the respective interfaces.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is determined entirely by URL parameters and runtime interface responses. On initialization, the page parses the query string of product.html to read two mutually exclusive parameters: productId and maintenancePlanId. If exactly one of these exists, the page enters either 'product mode' or 'maintenance-plan mode'. In product mode, the page calls getProductDetails(productId) and uses the returned Product, EquipmentCategory, and flags such as is_available_for_quote and is_available_for_comparison to configure available actions. In maintenance-plan mode, the page calls getMaintenancePlanDetails(maintenancePlanId) and uses the returned MaintenancePlan and derived labels to configure maintenance-plan-specific actions. If both productId and maintenancePlanId are present, or if neither is present, the page enters an error state that shows an informative message and does not invoke addProductToQuoteList, addProductToComparison, or addMaintenancePlanToServiceInquiry. The header context is independent of item type and is always initialized by calling getSiteHeaderContext after determining the mode, so that header icons reflect current counts. The comparison status bar always calls getComparisonStatus on load, but only displays comparison-related actions when the current mode is 'product mode' and the product supports comparison. After user actions that modify server-side lists (addProductToQuoteList, addProductToComparison, addMaintenancePlanToServiceInquiry), the page updates its local state (such as counts and messages) using the returned data and can optionally trigger a refresh of header context or comparison status, without relying on any client-side storage mechanisms beyond current in-memory component state. Navigation to related pages (equipment.html, services.html, quote_list.html, compare.html, service_inquiry.html) uses URL parameters to reconstruct context when those pages load, and the item detail page itself always re-computes its state and re-calls the appropriate interfaces whenever the URL changes to a different productId or maintenancePlanId.

components.functionality: Initializes and displays header-level context, including counts and totals for quote list, comparison list, reading list, and service inquiry, and exposes navigation to quote_list.html, compare.html, and service_inquiry.html via header icons.

Determines page mode based on URL parameters (productId or maintenancePlanId), loads either product or maintenance plan details, and exposes the resulting data and mode (product or maintenance-plan) to downstream components.

Shows the availability or status of the current item (e.g., active, draft, discontinued, inactive) and informs other components whether quote, comparison, or service inquiry actions should be enabled.

Displays hierarchical navigation for the current item and allows navigation back to the appropriate listing (equipment.html with categoryId for products, services.html with serviceType=maintenance_plans for maintenance plans).

When in product mode, shows key product information such as name, sku, section label, short description, image, and key specification highlights (including NaOH consumption for pH neutralization systems when applicable).
When in maintenance-plan mode, shows key maintenance plan information such as name, equipment type label, country label, and a brief description.

In product mode, displays a structured list or table of technical and commercial attributes from the Product model, including flow capacities, tank volume or series for tanks, plant flow capacity for dewatering units, NaOH consumption for pH systems, and pricing information.
In maintenance-plan mode, displays plan attributes such as service_type, equipment_type, response_time_hours, coverage_level, annual_cost, and currency, along with any extended description.

In product mode, allows the user to set a quantity and add the product to the quote list and, when supported, to the comparison list. Also exposes navigation to view the quote list after successful addition.
In maintenance-plan mode, allows the user to set a quantity and add the maintenance plan to the service inquiry. Also exposes navigation to view the service inquiry after successful addition.

Displays a lightweight summary of the current comparison session, including the number of items and their names and categories, and provides a control to navigate to compare.html. This remains visible when relevant to the current product mode.

Handles and displays error states when the requested productId or maintenancePlanId is invalid, missing, or when the corresponding detail interface fails, and ensures that no quote, comparison, or service inquiry actions are active.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width grid, approximately 2-column layout within a centered 900–980px container. Not a modern 12-column system, but conceptually a main-content column (~70–75%) and a left sidebar (~25–30%) with occasional full-width header and navigation rows.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the classic boxed layout from the mockup: a centered, fixed-width page container (≈960px) over a patterned full-width background. Inside the container, stack full-width horizontal bands at the top for contextual information (header context, availability, breadcrumb/comparison). Below these bands, switch into a persistent two-column layout: a narrower left sidebar (~25–28% width) with stacked, tool-like modules (comparison and maintenance plan panels) and a wider right main column (~72–75% width) dedicated to the primary item (product summary, actions, and specifications). Error and loading states overlay or replace the two-column content but retain the overall banded structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the classic boxed layout from the mockup: a centered, fixed-width page container (≈960px) over a patterned full-width background. Inside the container, stack full-width horizontal bands at the top for contextual information (header context, availability, breadcrumb/comparison). Below these bands, switch into a persistent two-column layout: a narrower left sidebar (~25–28% width) with stacked, tool-like modules (comparison and maintenance plan panels) and a wider right main column (~72–75% width) dedicated to the primary item (product summary, actions, and specifications). Error and loading states overlay or replace the two-column content but retain the overall banded structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate ~20–25% of the vertical space above the fold to header-like bands (header context bar, availability banner, breadcrumb/comparison strip). The remaining ~75–80% is the two-column content area. Within that, about three-quarters of the width goes to the product-focused main column and one-quarter to the maintenance/comparison sidebar. Each module has compact internal padding (8–12px) and modest vertical gaps (8–16px) between modules, with slightly more space under the top bands to separate them from the dense content below.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain an information-dense, utilitarian feel. Panels and bands have small padding and tight stacking. Text blocks and spec tables are compact: minimal spacing between lines and between paragraphs within the same panel. Actions are grouped into slim horizontal bars rather than large button blocks. Sidebar modules are tight stacks of links and short descriptions, mirroring the packed news/search blocks in the mockup. There is just enough spacing to distinguish sections without introducing modern, airy whitespace.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Follow a strong F-pattern. The eye first hits the top-left header-context bar, then scans right across it, then down to the availability-status banner, then across the breadcrumb/comparison strip. From there, attention drops diagonally into the upper-right of the two-column area where the product-summary-panel sits as the main focal block. The user then scrolls vertically down the product actions and specifications, while the left sidebar (comparison status and maintenance plan modules) remains in peripheral vision as compact, secondary content. Red and tinted status colors in the banners and action bars act as secondary visual attractors without overpowering the blue–white corporate base.

layout.overall_description: Inside a centered, white, boxed page container over a subtle patterned background, the page builds up in horizontal layers. Immediately under the site’s logo and main navigation (outside this spec) sits a slim blue header-context bar stretching the full container width. Below it, a slightly taller, colored availability-status banner forms a second band. A third, lighter band holds the breadcrumb on the left and a compact comparison-status block on the right, giving a clear left-to-right scan line. Beneath these stacked bands, the layout splits into two columns: a narrow left sidebar with small, framed modules for the comparison status (when expanded) and maintenance plan information, and a wider right column containing the primary product summary at the top, followed by a tight action bar and a dense specifications panel. Maintenance plan details mirror the product structure but in a compressed form in the sidebar, visually echoing the mockup’s stack of sidebar blocks like search and news. All elements sit close together with modest 8–16px gaps and 8–12px padding, creating a compact, utilitarian page where the main vertical reading path flows down the right column, anchored by occasional horizontal bars, while the left column quietly supports with secondary tools and maintenance details. Loading and error states appear as overlays or full-width interruption panels, but always within the same boxed, banded framework.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-context-bar sits at the very top of the content area, directly under the site’s main navigation/logo band, spanning the full width of the fixed-width page container (≈960px). It appears as a slim horizontal strip, about 1.2–1.4 times the height of a standard line of text, with a blue or blue-gradient background and a subtle beveled or inset effect to match the skeuomorphic style. Text is left-aligned, starting roughly 12px from the left edge, with 8–10px vertical padding. There is a narrow 8px gap below it before the availability-status-banner. This bar reads as a contextual label for the entire page, anchoring the top-left of the F-pattern and visually binding the content area to the corporate header above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width horizontal bar fixed to the top of the content stack within the page container; it scrolls away with the page (not sticky).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The item-detail-loader appears only while data is being fetched. When active, it overlays the entire inner content area beneath the header-context-bar, covering from just below the header-context-bar down to the bottom of the visible content. It spans the full container width and both columns, with a semi-opaque white or light-grey veil that slightly dims the underlying layout. At the visual center of the container, a compact loading indicator and brief status text are displayed in a small framed box (approximately 40% of the container width), with 12px internal padding and a subtle drop shadow, echoing the boxed feel of other panels. When the loader is inactive, it collapses completely and does not reserve space in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolute-positioned overlay anchored to the top-left of the content area beneath the header-context-bar, covering both columns and scrolling with the page while active; otherwise not rendered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability-status-banner is placed directly below the header-context-bar as the next full-width band, spanning the entire page container from left to right. It is slightly taller than the header-context-bar (roughly 1.5–2 lines of text tall), with 8–12px vertical padding and 12px horizontal padding on both sides. The background color is a solid or gently gradient-tinted status color (green for available, amber/red for limited or unavailable), with a beveled top or bottom edge to match the older corporate style. A status icon is aligned to the far left, followed by concise status text; any secondary detail may wrap into a second line within the same band. There is an 8px vertical gap before the next band containing the breadcrumb and comparison status. Visually, this banner reads like an important alert strip that applies to the whole item, occupying a clear horizontal slice just beneath the context bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width horizontal band within the content stack, scrolling normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb-nav sits directly below the availability-status-banner in a shared horizontal strip that spans the full container width. The strip itself is lightly differentiated from the white background (e.g., a very pale grey or a 1px bottom border). The breadcrumb takes up the left two-thirds of this strip, starting 12px from the left edge, with a single line of small, blue link text separated by angle or chevron separators. Vertical padding is modest (~6–8px), keeping the band slim and compact. To the right, the comparison-status-bar occupies the remaining one-third, aligned to the right edge, so the eye naturally scans from the breadcrumb to the comparison summary in a single left-to-right motion. The bottom of this strip has a faint divider line, and there is a 12–16px vertical gap below before the two-column content area begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, left-aligned component within a full-width horizontal strip; shares its row with the right-aligned comparison-status-bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-summary-panel anchors the top of the right main column in the two-column area. After the breadcrumb/comparison strip, there is a vertical gap of about 12–16px, then the layout splits: the right column (≈72–75% of the container width) begins at the same top baseline as the left column. The product-summary-panel occupies the full width of this right column, with a boxed white background, a thin grey border, and potentially a very subtle drop shadow to echo the mockup’s card-like sections. Internal padding is 12px on all sides. The product name appears as a bold blue heading at the top-left of the panel, mirroring the mockup’s blue section headings, possibly accompanied by a small product icon. Below the heading, a short set of key facts and possibly a medium-sized product photo are arranged in a compact, left-weighted layout. Vertical spacing inside the panel is tight (8px between text blocks), and the bottom of the panel sits about 8–12px above the product-actions-bar, maintaining the dense, stacked feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, topmost module in the right main column of the two-column layout; its width is constrained to the right column and it scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance-plan-summary-panel resides in the left sidebar column as a secondary but still prominent module. After the breadcrumb/comparison strip, the layout splits into columns: the left sidebar occupies about 25–28% of the container width, with a 16px horizontal gap separating it from the main column. Within this left column, the maintenance-plan-summary-panel typically sits just below the comparison-status-bar (or at the top of the sidebar if comparison is minimal), occupying the full sidebar width. It is a compact box with 8–12px internal padding, a thin grey border, and a plain white background. A small blue heading such as “Maintenance Plan” or the plan name appears at the top, followed by 1–2 short, dense lines of descriptive text or key bullet points. The module’s height is kept modest so that multiple sidebar elements can fit above the fold. An 8–12px gap separates this panel from the maintenance-plan-actions-bar below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked module within the left sidebar column; its width is limited to the sidebar and it scrolls with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-specs-panel is placed in the right main column directly beneath the product-actions-bar, forming the dense, article-like body of the item detail. It spans the full width of the right column and is one of the taller content blocks on the page. The panel has 12px padding, a subtle border, and a white background.
