5. The maintenance-plan listing re-renders, ordered by the selected sort criterion so the user can identify cost-effective options more easily.

Access service inquiry and contact flows from the Services page:
1. At any point, the header component uses getSiteHeaderContext to display the count of service inquiry items.
2. The user clicks the header's "View service inquiry" icon.
3. The page navigates to service_inquiry.html without additional parameters, allowing the user to review or submit their service inquiry.
4. Alternatively, the user clicks the "Contact us about services" call-to-action within the Services page content.
5. The page navigates to contact.html for direct contact with the provider.
interactions: Click on a service category item in the overview: sets or updates the serviceType URL parameter and reloads/refreshes the page state so that components react to the new service context.

Load page with no URL parameters: initializes header summary via getSiteHeaderContext, retrieves service categories via getServicesOverview, and shows only the services overview without maintenance-plan-specific filters or listing.

Load page with serviceType set to a maintenance-plan-related value: initializes header summary, retrieves service categories, fetches filter options via getMaintenanceFilterOptions(serviceType), reads existing URL parameters (country, equipmentType, maxResponseTimeHours, maxAnnualCost, sort), and calls listMaintenancePlans with built filters and sort_by value.
Change country filter: updates the country URL parameter, preserves other existing URL parameters, and triggers a new call to listMaintenancePlans with the updated filters.
Change equipment-type filter: updates the equipmentType URL parameter, preserves other existing URL parameters, and triggers a new call to listMaintenancePlans with the updated filters.
Change maximum response-time filter: updates the maxResponseTimeHours URL parameter, preserves other filters, and triggers a new call to listMaintenancePlans using the new maximum response time.
Change maximum annual-cost filter: updates the maxAnnualCost URL parameter, preserves other filters, and triggers a new call to listMaintenancePlans using the new maximum annual cost.
Change sorting option: updates the sort URL parameter and triggers a new call to listMaintenancePlans with the existing filters and the selected sort_by value.
Click on a maintenance-plan card: reads the corresponding plan.id from the listMaintenancePlans results and navigates to product.html?maintenancePlanId=<plan.id>.
Click on the header "View service inquiry" icon: navigates to service_inquiry.html.
Click on the "Contact us about services" call-to-action: navigates to contact.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page reads the serviceType, country, equipmentType, maxResponseTimeHours, maxAnnualCost, and sort query parameters from the URL. If serviceType is omitted, the page operates in overview mode: it calls getSiteHeaderContext and getServicesOverview and does not call getMaintenanceFilterOptions or listMaintenancePlans. If serviceType is present and corresponds to maintenance plans, the page calls getSiteHeaderContext and getServicesOverview, then calls getMaintenanceFilterOptions with the current serviceType. The filter component initializes its internal state from the current URL parameters and from the options returned by getMaintenanceFilterOptions. The page then constructs a filters object for listMaintenancePlans using the URL parameters, mapping them to the expected argument structure (for example, mapping country to the filters key that represents country, mapping equipmentType to the equipment_type filter, mapping maxResponseTimeHours to a maximum response-time filter, and mapping maxAnnualCost to a maximum annual-cost filter), and passes sort from the URL as the sort_by argument. Whenever a user changes a filter control or the sorting option, the corresponding URL parameter is updated via query-string manipulation without using localStorage, and the filtered and sorted state of the page is derived solely from the current URL. After each such change, listMaintenancePlans is invoked again with the updated filters and sort_by, and the maintenance-plan listing re-renders. This makes the URL the single source of truth for the current service context and maintenance-plan search state, enabling bookmarking and sharing of specific filtered views.

components.functionality: Fetches and displays aggregated header information about the user's quote list, comparison items, reading list, and service inquiry items, and provides a navigation entry point to the service inquiry page.
event_handlers: onInit: call getSiteHeaderContext and bind returned counts and totals to header icons and summary text.
onClickQuoteListIcon: navigate to a quote-list-related page if available in the global navigation (navigation details handled outside this page's scope).
onClickComparisonIcon: navigate to a product-comparison-related page if available in the global navigation.
onClickReadingListIcon: navigate to a reading-list-related page if available in the global navigation.

components.functionality: Displays all service categories returned by the services overview interface and manages switching of the active service context via the serviceType URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit: call getServicesOverview and render the list of service categories with their titles and descriptions.

onServiceCategoryClick: read the clicked category's service_type from the bound data and update the serviceType URL parameter to that value; trigger a page reload or state refresh so that other components (filters and maintenance-plan listing) respond to the new serviceType.

components.functionality: Coordinates page-level behavior depending on the current serviceType URL parameter, determining whether to operate in overview-only mode or to activate maintenance-plan-specific filters and listing.
event_handlers: onInit: read serviceType from the URL. If no serviceType is present, set internal mode to 'overview' so that only services-overview-list is visible. If serviceType is present and corresponds to maintenance plans, set internal mode to 'maintenance_plans' so that the maintenance-filters-form and maintenance-plans-results-list components are activated.
onURLChange: re-evaluate the serviceType parameter and update internal mode accordingly; trigger re-rendering or visibility toggling for dependent components.

components.functionality: Provides interactive controls to filter maintenance plans by country, equipment type, maximum response time, maximum annual cost, and sorting. Synchronizes all filter and sort choices with URL parameters and triggers maintenance-plan list updates.
event_handlers: onInit: read serviceType from URL; if it corresponds to maintenance plans, call getMaintenanceFilterOptions(serviceType) to obtain filter options. Read country, equipmentType, maxResponseTimeHours, maxAnnualCost, and sort from URL parameters and initialize form controls to these values when present or fall back to reasonable defaults based on the options returned by getMaintenanceFilterOptions.

onCountryChange: when the user selects a new country option, update the country URL parameter to the selected option value, preserving other query parameters; emit a filtersChanged event for downstream components such as maintenance-plans-results-list.
onEquipmentTypeChange: when the user selects a new equipment type, update the equipmentType URL parameter; emit filtersChanged.
onResponseTimeChange: when the user selects or adjusts the maximum response-time control, update the maxResponseTimeHours URL parameter to the corresponding numeric value; emit filtersChanged.
onAnnualCostChange: when the user adjusts the maximum annual-cost control, update the maxAnnualCost URL parameter; emit filtersChanged.
onSortChange: when the user selects a new sort option from the sort_options provided by getMaintenanceFilterOptions, update the sort URL parameter to the chosen value; emit filtersChanged.

onClearFilters: optionally, allow the user to reset filters by removing the country, equipmentType, maxResponseTimeHours, and maxAnnualCost parameters from the URL while keeping serviceType; emit filtersChanged with the cleared state.

components.functionality: Displays maintenance plans returned by the listing interface according to the current serviceType, filters, and sort order reflected in the URL parameters. Shows result count and supports navigation to detailed plan views.
event_handlers: onInit: read serviceType from URL; if it corresponds to maintenance plans, build a filters object using country, equipmentType, maxResponseTimeHours, and maxAnnualCost from URL parameters (when present), and read sort from the URL as the sort_by value. Call listMaintenancePlans(serviceType, filters, sort_by) and bind the returned maintenance_plans and total_results.

onFiltersChanged: whenever the maintenance-filters-form component emits a filtersChanged event (or whenever URL parameters change), rebuild the filters object from the current URL parameters, read sort, and re-call listMaintenancePlans with the updated arguments. Update the displayed list and total result count based on the new response.
onPlanCardClick: when the user clicks a maintenance-plan item, read the associated plan.id from the bound listMaintenancePlans result and navigate to product.html?maintenancePlanId=<plan.id>.

onNoResults: if listMaintenancePlans returns total_results equal to zero, display an informative message and guidance (for example, suggesting that the user broaden filters), without altering filters automatically.

components.functionality: Shows a concise summary above the maintenance-plan listing, including the active serviceType context and the number of matching maintenance plans.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: when listMaintenancePlans returns new data, read total_results and service_type from the response and update the summary text to reflect the number of plans currently matching the filters.

onURLChange: when serviceType or other filter parameters change in the URL, update the contextual text (for example, indicating which serviceType is active) and wait for the next listMaintenancePlans response to update the result count.

components.functionality: Provides a simple call-to-action for users who prefer to contact the company directly about services rather than using filters and listings.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width grid, approximately 2-column layout within a centered 900–980px container. Not a modern 12-column system, but conceptually a main-content column (~70–75%) and a left sidebar (~25–30%) with occasional full-width header and navigation rows.
layout.layout_pattern: Inside the fixed-width (≈940px) centered page container, the page continues the mockup’s stacked-band pattern: global logo/banner and main navigation sit above (outside this spec). Within the content area, a full-width site-header-context-bar forms the first band, followed immediately by a full-width, low-height band for the service-type-state-controller. Below that, the layout resolves into the familiar two-column interior: a narrow left sidebar (≈25–30% of container width) with stacked utility modules (including the services-contact-cta) and a wider right column (≈70–75%) for all Services and Maintenance content. In the main column, content is vertically linear and information-dense: services-overview-list appears first, followed further down by the maintenance section, which itself stacks as maintenance-filters-form > maintenance-plans-summary-header > maintenance-plans-results-list. URL parameters and the state controller toggle visibility/emphasis of the overview section vs the maintenance section but do not fundamentally change the spatial hierarchy.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Inside the fixed-width (≈940px) centered page container, the page continues the mockup’s stacked-band pattern: global logo/banner and main navigation sit above (outside this spec). Within the content area, a full-width site-header-context-bar forms the first band, followed immediately by a full-width, low-height band for the service-type-state-controller. Below that, the layout resolves into the familiar two-column interior: a narrow left sidebar (≈25–30% of container width) with stacked utility modules (including the services-contact-cta) and a wider right column (≈70–75%) for all Services and Maintenance content. In the main column, content is vertically linear and information-dense: services-overview-list appears first, followed further down by the maintenance section, which itself stacks as maintenance-filters-form > maintenance-plans-summary-header > maintenance-plans-results-list. URL parameters and the state controller toggle visibility/emphasis of the overview section vs the maintenance section but do not fundamentally change the spatial hierarchy.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly 15–20% of the visible page height is occupied by the site-header-context-bar and the immediately adjacent service-type-state-controller, forming a compact contextual header and sub-navigation area. The remaining 80–85% is dedicated to the two-column content band. Horizontally, the left sidebar occupies about 1/4 of the container width (≈220–250px), while the main content column uses the remaining 3/4 (≈690–720px). Within the main column, the services-overview-list typically uses the upper 20–30% of the scrollable area, with the maintenance stack (filters, summary, results) potentially extending through the rest of the page height. Vertical spacing between major blocks (overview versus maintenance, filters versus summary versus results) follows the modest 12–16px section margins from the mockup, with tighter 4–8px spacing between related elements inside each component.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page is deliberately compact and text-heavy, mirroring the mockup’s utilitarian feel. The site-header-context-bar uses modest padding (≈12px) and tightly stacked title/description text. The service-type-state-controller is a slim bar with small tab-like controls separated by 8–12px of horizontal padding, minimal vertical padding, and no large breathing spaces. In the main column, services-overview-list presents a dense stack of service rows with brief descriptions and little vertical whitespace (≈4–8px between rows). The maintenance-filters-form arranges fields in one or two horizontal lines where possible, using small input padding and narrow gaps between fields. The maintenance-plans-results-list presents compact, information-dense rows with short spacing between them. The sidebar, containing the services-contact-cta, uses small paddings and tight stacking, making the whole page feel packed with information rather than airy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows the classic F-pattern. The eye lands on the left-aligned page title and context in the site-header-context-bar, sweeps right across the full-width band, then drops down to the service-type-state-controller, scanning its horizontal options (e.g., 'Service overview' vs 'Maintenance plans'), with the active state highlighted (likely in red) to catch attention. From there, the gaze shifts down and slightly right into the main content column, hitting the top of the services-overview-list first. As users scroll, the flow continues vertically through the overview rows and then into the maintenance section: filters at the top (with left-aligned labels), then the maintenance-plans-summary-header as a thin horizontal bar, and finally down into the dense maintenance-plans-results-list. Meanwhile, the left sidebar—with the services-contact-cta rendered in a contrasting red highlight—sits in peripheral vision as a secondary vertical attractor, offering a clear action path without competing with the main reading flow.

layout.overall_description: Within a centered, white, boxed container set against a subtly patterned full-page background, the Services page is built from stacked horizontal bands and a persistent two-column interior. Just below the global logo/banner and main navigation bar, a full-width, slightly tinted site-header-context-bar introduces the page with a left-aligned 'Services' heading and a short explanatory line, all packed into a relatively shallow band with modest padding. Immediately under this, a thin, full-width sub-navigation band—the service-type-state-controller—runs horizontally across the container, with two or three compact tab-like options aligned to the left; the active tab is called out in a red or darker-blue skeuomorphic pill, echoing the old-corporate style. Under this control band, the content band splits into two columns: on the left, a narrow sidebar rises from the same top line, stacking small site modules, with a compact services-contact-cta box positioned in the upper third to keep it visible; on the right, a wider main column contains all text-heavy services content. At the top of this main column, a dense services-overview-list presents each service category as a concise row with a blue heading and a short description, separated by narrow gaps. Below, the maintenance section begins with a slightly boxed maintenance-filters-form, followed by a slim maintenance-plans-summary-header bar, and then a tall, scrolling maintenance-plans-results-list of tightly packed maintenance plan entries. The result is a conservative, information-oriented layout where the eye moves from the contextual header, through the sub-navigation, into the main column’s overview and maintenance sections, while the left sidebar’s red-accent contact callout remains a subtle but constant secondary attractor.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The site-header-context-bar appears directly below the global logo/banner and main navigation, as the first content band inside the white, fixed-width page container. It spans the full internal width of the container (≈900–980px) and has a modest height, roughly 80–100px depending on text length. A light, slightly beveled blue-tinted background differentiates it from the plain white content below, in keeping with the skeuomorphic style. The primary page title 'Services' is left-aligned near the top of this band, in bold blue text, with a concise description or subtitle directly beneath it, separated by about 4–8px. Padding around the content is consistent on all sides (≈12px), and the bottom edge of this bar sits about 8px above the next band (the service-type-state-controller). There are no large whitespace areas; everything is compact and utilitarian, guiding the eye from the top-left title across the band and preparing users for the content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block within the central page container; it scrolls away with the page and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-overview-list is positioned in the main (right) content column, starting near the top of that column and sitting directly beneath the service-type-state-controller band, with a vertical gap of about 12–16px. Horizontally, it aligns flush with the main column’s left edge and spans the full column width (≈70–75% of the total container), stopping short of the sidebar. The component consists of a vertically stacked list of service categories; each category is presented as a compact row or card: a blue, left-aligned service title at the top of the row, followed by one or two lines of dense descriptive text underneath, with only 4–6px separation between the title and description and a modest 8–12px gap before the next service row. Any background tint or border treatment is subtle (a light gray or white with a thin border), keeping the emphasis on text rather than visual framing. Overall, the list occupies roughly the upper 20–30% of the scrollable main column by default, but if there are many services, it can extend further down, pushing the maintenance section lower in the vertical flow. When the page is in a 'maintenance plans' focused mode, this block may be visually de-emphasized (e.g., collapsed title with fewer details) but remains in the same spatial position.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main column, flowing naturally beneath the service-type-state-controller and above the maintenance-related components. It expands vertically as needed based on the number of services.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-type-state-controller is a slim, full-width horizontal band immediately below the site-header-context-bar and above the two-column content band. It spans the entire width of the inner page container (matching the header-context-bar) but is visually shallow, with a height of about 40–48px. The controller’s content is left-aligned: two or three tab-like options (e.g., 'Service overview', 'Maintenance plans', and possibly 'All services') are arranged in a horizontal row starting from the left margin, each item having roughly 8–12px horizontal padding and 4–6px vertical padding. The active tab is highlighted in a red-accented or darker blue pill with a slightly beveled edge, echoing the navigation style from the mockup, while inactive tabs are plain text or lightly outlined. A very thin separator line or subtle gradient may distinguish this sub-nav bar from the white background below. The bottom edge of this band sits only 8px or so above where the two columns begin, making it feel directly connected to the content it controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width sub-navigation band that scrolls with the page. It controls which main-column sections (services-overview-list vs maintenance components) are shown or emphasized but does not change position or stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance-filters-form is located in the main right-hand column, below the services-overview-list. When maintenance mode is active or URL parameters request maintenance plans, this form appears with a vertical gap of approximately 16px beneath the last overview item or section divider. The form spans the full width of the main column (aligned with the overview list) and is enclosed in a low-contrast, slightly beveled box with a light gray or extremely pale blue background, differentiating it from the plain white content but keeping it subtle. Inside, filter controls (such as dropdowns, checkboxes, and text fields) are arranged as densely as possible: primary filters appear in a horizontal row, with individual fields placed side-by-side where space allows, each with small internal padding (≈4–6px) and only 8–12px horizontal gaps between them. Secondary filters may appear on a second row directly beneath, with only 4–8px vertical spacing. Labels sit immediately to the left or above each input, using compact typography and minimal label-input spacing. A primary 'Filter' or 'Apply' button is right-aligned within the form’s content area, but it does not dominate visually; it’s just another tightly placed element. The outer padding of the form’s box is about 8–12px on all sides, consistent with other modules in the design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main column. It appears or hides based on state/URL parameters but otherwise flows naturally above the maintenance-plans-summary-header and scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance-plans-results-list occupies the largest vertical portion of the maintenance section in the main column. It is positioned directly below the maintenance-plans-summary-header, with a narrow 8–12px gap between them, and spans the full width of the main column. The list is a dense vertical stack of maintenance plan entries. Each plan row begins with a bold or slightly larger plan name in dark blue on the left, followed by one or two lines of compressed supporting details (e.g., key features, applicable equipment, frequency) directly beneath or slightly indented, with only 4–6px vertical spacing between heading and details. Rows may have alternating lightly tinted backgrounds or thin horizontal separators to help distinguish them without introducing large vertical gaps; spacing between rows is tight (≈4–8px). Any supplementary labels (e.g., 'Standard', 'Premium') appear as small, right-aligned badges within the same row. The list can extend downwards for several viewport heights and will be the main scrollable content area when many plans are available. Pagination controls, if present, are positioned at the bottom of this list, aligned right or center, with only a small margin (≈12–16px) above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding list inside the main content column. It scrolls as part of the page and sits below the maintenance-plans-summary-header, with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance-plans-summary-header appears immediately below the maintenance-filters-form (or, in states without visible filters, directly under the services-overview-list or a section divider), separated by about 12px. It is a low-height (≈32–40px) horizontal strip spanning the full width of the main column, visually lightweight but clearly distinct: often a light gray bar or a white bar with a thin top border. On the left side, compact text summarizes the results (e.g., 'Showing 12 maintenance plans' and/or current filter criteria), aligned to the main column’s left edge. On the right side, within the same bar, compact 'Sort by' controls (dropdown or small links) are right-aligned with minimal horizontal spacing between them. Internal vertical padding is small (≈4–6px), keeping the bar shallow and in line with the information-dense style. This summary header acts as a subtle anchor for the results list, visually tying the filters above to the plans below without taking up much space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, single-row block that sits between the maintenance-filters-form and the maintenance-plans-results-list. It scrolls with the page and maintains its position relative to these components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-contact-cta is placed in the left sidebar column, within the stacked sequence of sidebar modules. It appears relatively high in the sidebar—preferably in the upper half, immediately below any site-wide search or navigation widget—so it remains visible while users scan main content on the right. The component matches the sidebar’s width (≈25–30% of the total container) and is framed as a small box or panel with a subtle border and perhaps a light background tint to distinguish it from the plain sidebar background. Inside, a short, bold heading (e.g., 'Need help with services?') is left-aligned at the top, followed by a brief single-line prompt. Below that, occupying nearly the full internal width of the box, a prominent red or red-accented button-style link invites users to contact the service team. The button has horizontal padding of about 12–16px and vertical padding of 6–8px, but remains compact and in line with the dense style. The panel’s outer padding is modest (≈8–12px), and its vertical margins relative to neighboring sidebar modules are about 12–16px, ensuring it is clearly separated but still tightly integrated into the sidebar stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar module that scrolls with the page as part of the left column. It maintains its relative position among other sidebar blocks and does not stick or float independently.

name: Resources Listing
title: Resources
description: Unified resources page that lists case studies or webinars/events based on URL parameters, with server-driven filters and sorting. Users can switch resource type, filter by attributes such as industry or topic and date, sort results, and navigate to detailed resource pages.

core_features: Use the resourceType URL parameter to switch between listing case studies or webinars/events; default to case studies when resourceType is not provided.

On page load and whenever resourceType changes, call getResourceFilterOptions(resourceType) to retrieve available filters (industries, years, capacity ranges, geographies, topics, months, sort options) and populate filter controls dynamically.
Apply filters for case studies (industry, year, minimum plant capacity, geography if supported) based on URL parameters and pass them as the filters argument to listResources.
Apply filters for webinars/events (topic, month, year, optional time window) based on URL parameters and pass them as the filters argument to listResources.

Call listResources(resourceType, filters, sort_by) whenever URL parameters related to filters or sort change, and render the returned list of ResourceItem records with quick visual cues (industry_label, date_display, capacity_display, and time information for webinars).

Use sort options returned by getResourceFilterOptions to allow users to sort the listing (for example by recency or start time) and map the selected option to the sort URL parameter and listResources sort_by argument.
Allow users to click on any resource card to navigate to resource_detail.html with the resourceId set to the selected ResourceItem.id.

Integrate getSiteHeaderContext on page initialization to display current header counters (quote list, comparison, reading list, service inquiry) and provide navigation to reading_list.html via the header.
Optionally support pagination via filters.page and filters.page_size in listResources, keeping page information in URL parameters to make result sets shareable.
user_workflows:
Browse case studies by default:
1. User arrives at resource_list.html without URL parameters or with resourceType=case_studies.
2. Page calls getSiteHeaderContext to populate header counters.
3. Page calls getResourceFilterOptions('case_studies') to retrieve available case study filters and sort options.
4. Page parses URL parameters (industry, year, minCapacityM3PerDay, sort, and optional geography/page) to construct a filters object.
5. Page calls listResources('case_studies', filters, sort_by) and displays the resulting resources as case study cards with industry_label, date_display, capacity_display, and geography where available.
6. User adjusts filters or sorting; the page updates URL parameters and re-calls listResources with the new filters/sort_by to refresh the list.

Filter food & beverage (or other) case studies by year and capacity:
1. User is in case study mode (resourceType=case_studies).
2. In the filters panel, user selects an industry from options provided by getResourceFilterOptions.industries.
3. User selects a publication year from getResourceFilterOptions.years.
4. User sets a minimum capacity value within the range from getResourceFilterOptions.plant_capacity_range_m3_per_day.
5. Each change updates the corresponding URL parameters (industry, year, minCapacityM3PerDay) and triggers a call to listResources('case_studies', updatedFilters, currentSort).
6. The resource list updates to show only case studies that match the selected industry, year, and capacity threshold.

Switch to webinars and filter PFAS webinars by month/year:
1. User toggles the resource type switcher from case studies to webinars.
2. The page updates the URL parameter resourceType=webinars and clears or adapts incompatible filter parameters (e.g., removes year and minCapacityM3PerDay, preserves sort if valid).
3. The page calls getResourceFilterOptions('webinars') to load webinar-specific options (topics, months, years, sort options).
4. User selects a topic from the topics list, potentially choosing PFAS-related topics as provided by the API.
5. User selects a month and year from the months and years arrays returned.
6. The page updates URL parameters (topic, month, yearWebinar, resourceType) and calls listResources('webinars', webinarFilters, currentSort) to display matching webinars.
7. Each webinar card displays date_display and time information derived from start_datetime/end_datetime, plus industry_label or topic where available.

Sort resources by recency or event start time:
1. After filters are set, user opens the sort control populated from getResourceFilterOptions.sort_options.
2. User selects a sort option.
3. The page updates the sort URL parameter to the selected option value.
4. The page calls listResources(currentResourceType, currentFilters, selectedSort) and refreshes the listing in the new order.

Navigate to a resource detail page:
1. User reviews the resource listing and clicks on a resource card (title or designated clickable area).
2. The browser navigates to resource_detail.html with resourceId set to the clicked ResourceItem.id (resource_detail.html?resourceId=<id>).
3. User can use browser back navigation to return to resource_list.html with all filters preserved via URL parameters.

Access the reading list from the resources page:
1. On page load, getSiteHeaderContext returns reading_list_item_count, which is shown on the reading list header icon.
2. User clicks the reading list icon or text link in the header.
3. Browser navigates to reading_list.html, preserving no additional parameters from this page (unless global navigation policies add them).

Breadcrumb navigation back to home:
1. Page displays a breadcrumb trail with a link to Home.
2. User clicks the Home breadcrumb.
3. Browser navigates to index.html, exiting the resources listing context.
interactions: Changing resource type (case studies vs webinars) updates the resourceType URL parameter, reloads filter options via getResourceFilterOptions, and refreshes the resources list via listResources.
Adjusting any filter control updates the corresponding URL parameter(s), reconstructs the filters object, and re-calls listResources with the new filters while keeping resourceType intact.
Selecting a sort option updates the sort URL parameter and re-calls listResources with the same filters and new sort_by value.
Clicking a resource card navigates to resource_detail.html with resourceId set to the selected resource.id.
Clicking the reading list icon or label in the header navigates to reading_list.html, using current header context only for display (no state is modified by this click).
Clicking the breadcrumb link labeled Home navigates to index.html.

Optional: Using pagination controls updates a page URL parameter (and possibly pageSize), which is passed through filters.page and filters.page_size to listResources to retrieve the appropriate subset of results.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is managed via URL parameters and server responses, without localStorage or other client-side persistence.

1) resourceType
- Accepted values: 'case_studies' or 'webinars'.
- If resourceType is missing, the page assumes 'case_studies'.
- On any change to resourceType (e.g., via a toggle), the page updates the URL parameter and re-initializes filters and listings:
  - Calls getResourceFilterOptions(resourceType).
  - Resets or adapts URL filter parameters that are incompatible with the new type.
  - Calls listResources(resourceType, filtersFromUrl, sort_from_url).

2) Case study-related URL parameters
- industry: string; when present and resourceType='case_studies', it is passed to listResources as filters.industry.
- year: number; when present and resourceType='case_studies', it is passed as filters.publication_year or equivalent filter field.
- minCapacityM3PerDay: number; when present and resourceType='case_studies', it is passed as filters.minCapacityM3PerDay to filter by minimum plant_capacity_m3_per_day.
- Optional: geography: string; if implemented, when present and resourceType='case_studies', it is passed as filters.geography to listResources.

3) Webinar-related URL parameters
- topic: string; when present and resourceType='webinars', it is passed as filters.topic.
- month: number; when present and resourceType='webinars', it is passed as filters.month to indicate the month portion of start_datetime.
- yearWebinar: number; when present and resourceType='webinars', it is passed as filters.year to indicate the year portion of start_datetime.
- Optional: timeWindow or similar parameter name; if implemented, it is passed directly within filters for listResources and interpreted on the server.

4) Sorting
- sort: string; when present, it is passed as the sort_by argument to listResources.
- If sort is not present, the page uses the first option returned by getResourceFilterOptions.sort_options as a default (but does not need to write it back to the URL unless desired).

5) Pagination (optional but supported via filters)
- page: number; when present, it is passed as filters.page to listResources to request a particular page of results.
- pageSize: number; when present, it is passed as filters.page_size.
- The page reads total_results from listResources to compute page counts or next/previous availability.

6) Synchronization
- On any URL change (initial load, back/forward navigation, or interactions that modify query parameters), the page parses URL parameters, reconstructs the filters object and sort_by value, and calls listResources accordingly.
- getResourceFilterOptions is re-called whenever resourceType changes to ensure filter options remain consistent with the selected resource type.
- getSiteHeaderContext is typically called on initial page load (and optionally on subsequent reloads) to keep header counters in sync.

7) No client-side persistence outside URL
- The page does not rely on localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies for filters or sorting.
- All shareable state (resource type, filters, sorting, page) is encoded in the URL so the listing can be revisited or shared while preserving the same view.

components.functionality: Fetches and displays header-level context such as counts for quote list, comparison, reading list, and service inquiry. Provides a clickable reading list link that navigates to reading_list.html.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getSiteHeaderContext() when the page loads to retrieve header counters and bind them to header icons or labels.
onClickReadingList: When the reading list icon or label is clicked, navigate to reading_list.html without modifying any server-side state.
onClickQuoteListIcon: Optionally navigate to a quote_list-related page (if exists) or simply display totals; no additional interfaces are called from this page.
onClickServiceInquiryIcon: Optionally navigate to a service inquiry-related page; again, no new interfaces are invoked on this page.

components.functionality: Displays a breadcrumb trail indicating the current location within the site hierarchy and allows navigation back to the home page.
event_handlers: onInit: Determine current resourceType from URL to display 'Resources' and the current mode label (e.g., case studies or webinars) in the breadcrumb.
onClickHome: Navigate to index.html when the Home breadcrumb segment is clicked; no API calls are required.
onClickResourcesRoot: Optionally navigate to resource_list.html without additional URL parameters to reset filters.

components.functionality: Allows the user to switch between case studies and webinars/events by updating the resourceType URL parameter and reinitializing filters and results.
event_handlers: onInit: Read resourceType from the URL; if absent, set internal state to 'case_studies'. Set the toggle position accordingly.

onChangeResourceType: When the user changes the toggle, update the resourceType URL parameter and remove any URL parameters that are incompatible with the new resource type. Then call getResourceFilterOptions(newResourceType) to load filter options and trigger a refresh of the resources list via listResources.
onUrlParamChange: When the browser navigation changes the URL (back/forward), ensure the toggle reflects the resourceType in the URL and, if different, re-fetch filter options.

components.functionality: Displays dynamic filters for the current resource type. For case studies, shows industry, year, minimum plant capacity, and optionally geography filters. For webinars, shows topic, month, year, and optionally time window filters. All options are fetched from getResourceFilterOptions.
event_handlers: onInit: After resourceType is resolved, call getResourceFilterOptions(resourceType). Use the returned industries, years, plant_capacity_range_m3_per_day, geographies, topics, and months to render available filters. Pre-select values based on current URL parameters (industry, year, minCapacityM3PerDay, topic, month, yearWebinar, and any optional geography/time window parameters).

onChangeIndustry: When the industry filter is changed in case study mode, update the industry URL parameter (add or remove) and trigger a refresh via listResources(resourceType, updatedFilters, currentSort).
onChangeYearCaseStudy: When the case study year filter changes, update the year URL parameter and refresh results.

onChangeMinCapacity: When the minimum capacity input changes, validate it within the bounds given by plant_capacity_range_m3_per_day and update the minCapacityM3PerDay URL parameter, then refresh results.
onChangeGeography: If geography is supported, update a geography URL parameter and refresh results.
onChangeTopic: In webinar mode, when the topic filter changes, update the topic URL parameter and refresh results.
onChangeMonth: In webinar mode, when the month filter changes, update the month URL parameter and refresh results.
onChangeYearWebinar: In webinar mode, when the webinar year filter changes, update the yearWebinar URL parameter and refresh results.
onChangeTimeWindow: If a time window control is implemented, update its corresponding URL parameter and refresh results.

onClearFilters: Provide a control to clear all filters for the current resourceType by removing relevant URL parameters and re-calling listResources with an empty filters object (or minimal defaults).

onUrlParamChange: When URL parameters change externally (e.g., browser back button), re-sync the filter controls to represent the new URL state without re-calling getResourceFilterOptions unless resourceType changed.

components.functionality: Displays sorting options for the current resource type and triggers reloading of resources when the sort order changes.
event_handlers: onInit: After getResourceFilterOptions(resourceType) resolves, read sort_options and populate the sort dropdown. Read the sort URL parameter; if present, select the corresponding option; otherwise select a default (e.g., the first option) for internal state.
onChangeSort: When the user selects a new sort option, update the sort URL parameter and call listResources(currentResourceType, currentFilters, selectedSortValue) to refresh the listing.
onUrlParamChange: If the sort URL parameter changes due to navigation, update the selected option in the sort dropdown to match.

components.functionality: Shows a summary of the current result set such as total_results and possibly a short description of applied filters.
event_handlers: onResourcesLoaded: After each call to listResources, update the displayed total_results count and, optionally, badges or text summarizing key filters (derived from current URL parameters).

onClearAllFiltersClick: When user clicks a 'Clear all' control within the summary (if present), remove all filter-related URL parameters for the current resourceType and trigger listResources with empty filters.

components.functionality: Renders the list of resources returned by listResources as a set of resource cards, handling both case studies and webinars in a unified structure.
event_handlers: onInit: Once filters and sort_by are determined from URL, call listResources(resourceType, filters, sort_by) and store the returned resources and total_results.
onRefreshResources: Whenever filters or sort change (as indicated by URL parameter changes), re-call listResources with updated parameters and re-render the cards.
onEmptyResults: If listResources returns total_results=0, display an appropriate empty state message and provide quick access to clear filters or adjust them.
onError: If listResources fails, display an error state and allow retry without changing filters.

components.functionality: Represents a single resource in the list. Displays key metadata including title, summary, industry_label, date_display, capacity_display, and, for webinars, time-related details such as event start time and registration status. Provides navigation to the resource detail page.
event_handlers:
onRender: For each resource from listResources.resources, extract and display:
          - resource.title
          - resource.summary (if available)
          - industry_label
          - date_display
          - capacity_display
          - For webinars (resource.resource_type='webinars'): start_datetime, end_datetime, time_zone, registration_open, and any speakers as plain text.
        Use only the fields provided by listResources and ResourceItem; do not hardcode any values.
onClickCard: When the card (or its title) is clicked, navigate to resource_detail.html?resourceId=<resource.id>.
onKeyPressEnter: Support keyboard accessibility so pressing Enter on a focused card triggers the same navigation as a click.
onHover: Optionally show more metadata in a tooltip or expanded view, using only data available on the ResourceItem; no additional API calls are made from this component.

components.functionality: Controls and displays pagination for the resource list based on total_results from listResources and page/pageSize values stored in URL parameters and passed through filters to listResources.
event_handlers: onInit: Read page and pageSize from the URL (if present) and incorporate them into the filters object passed to listResources. After listResources returns, compute the number of pages based on total_results and pageSize.
onChangePage: When the user selects a different page, update the page URL parameter and re-call listResources with updated filters.page (and filters.page_size if applicable).
onChangePageSize: If a page size selector is provided, update the pageSize URL parameter, reset page to 1, and re-call listResources.
onUrlParamChange: If page or pageSize change due to navigation, re-sync the pagination controls and re-fetch data if needed.

components.functionality: Provides user feedback when no resources match the current filters and offers quick actions to adjust or clear filters.
event_handlers: onResourcesLoaded: After listResources returns, if total_results is 0, display an empty state message in place of or above the resource list.

onClickClearFilters: When a 'Clear filters' action in the empty state is clicked, remove filter-related URL parameters for the current resourceType and trigger a new call to listResources with an empty filters object.
onClickAdjustFilters: Optionally focus the filters panel or provide guidance for adjusting filters without clearing them.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width grid, approximately 2-column layout within a centered 900–980px container. Not a modern 12-column system, but conceptually a main-content column (~70–75%) and a left sidebar (~25–30%) with occasional full-width header and navigation rows.
layout.layout_pattern: The page sits inside the existing boxed, fixed‑width site shell. Above this page content, the global logo/banner strip and full‑width main navigation bar are already present. Directly beneath the global navigation, the page introduces a full‑width (within the white container) header context bar, followed by a narrow breadcrumb strip. Below that, the layout locks into the familiar two‑column band: a narrow left sidebar (≈25–30% of the container width) dedicated to filters, and a wider right main column (≈70–75%) dedicated to resource‑type switching, result controls, and the resource list. Within the main column, elements stack vertically in a linear, text-heavy sequence: resource type switcher at the top, then a thin control band with results summary and sorting, followed by a single‑column stack of resource cards and pagination at the bottom. If there are no results, the empty state occupies the same main content region where the cards would appear.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page sits inside the existing boxed, fixed‑width site shell. Above this page content, the global logo/banner strip and full‑width main navigation bar are already present. Directly beneath the global navigation, the page introduces a full‑width (within the white container) header context bar, followed by a narrow breadcrumb strip. Below that, the layout locks into the familiar two‑column band: a narrow left sidebar (≈25–30% of the container width) dedicated to filters, and a wider right main column (≈70–75%) dedicated to resource‑type switching, result controls, and the resource list. Within the main column, elements stack vertically in a linear, text-heavy sequence: resource type switcher at the top, then a thin control band with results summary and sorting, followed by a single‑column stack of resource cards and pagination at the bottom. If there are no results, the empty state occupies the same main content region where the cards would appear.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Within the visible page area, the global header and banner (outside this spec) occupy the top 15–20%, the global navigation 5–8%. The page-specific content starts below that: roughly 10% of the page height is used by the header context bar and breadcrumbs combined. The remaining 70–75% is the two‑column content band: around 25–30% width is reserved for the filter sidebar running nearly full height of the content area, while 70–75% width is assigned to the main content column containing controls, results list, and pagination. Vertical spacing between bands and blocks follows the modest rhythm from the mockup: 8–12px gaps for closely related items (breadcrumb to filters, filters modules, between cards), and 12–16px gaps between larger structural bands (header context bar to breadcrumb, control band to resource list). Internal padding within components is compact (~8–12px).
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a compact, information‑dense feel. All text runs in relatively long lines within the constrained 900–980px container. Paragraphs and metadata within resource cards have minimal vertical separation (4–8px), filters are tightly stacked with short labels and minimal line height, and controls (sort, pagination) present multiple options in a small vertical footprint. There are no large hero areas or overly generous whitespace zones: containers have modest padding (≈8–12px on each side), and margins between them are small but clear. The overall impression matches an old‑generation corporate reference page: utilitarian, text‑heavy, designed to show many resources and filter options at once without scrolling excessively.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows a classic F‑pattern. After the user scans the global header/navigation, the gaze lands at the top left of the page-specific content: the header context bar with the page title. It then tracks right across this bar, then drops to the breadcrumb just below. From there, the eye moves down into the two‑column area, falling diagonally into the right main column where the resource type switcher and results summary live, while the left peripheral vision picks up the filters sidebar. The first strong focal point in the main column is the resource type switcher (with a likely red or more saturated active state), then the results summary and sort control row. Reading continues vertically down through the resource cards, each starting with a bold blue title line and possibly a small icon, followed by dense descriptive text and, when present, a narrow image strip. Red accents on active navigation, currently selected resource type, or highlighted filters draw secondary attention, echoing the mockup’s subtle but purposeful color cues.

layout.overall_description: Inside a centered, white, fixed‑width page container set against a patterned background, the resources listing page extends the site’s conservative, boxed aesthetic. A light, beveled header context bar spans the full content width just below the main site navigation, anchoring the page title on the left. Immediately underneath, a narrow breadcrumb line hugs the top left, creating a light textual path back through the site. Below this, the content snaps into a two‑column band. On the left, a tall, narrow sidebar occupies about a quarter of the container width, filled with stacked filter modules that resemble compact cards with small headings and tightly packed controls. On the right, a broader main column consumes the remaining width, starting with a text‑based resource type switcher aligned at the top, then a slim control strip that balances a results summary on the left and a sort dropdown on the right. Beneath this, a vertical stack of resource cards runs down the column, each beginning with a bold blue title bar and small icon, followed by dense text and occasional thumbnail rows, mirroring the reference entries from the mockup. Pagination sits at the very bottom of the main column, centered within its width but still feeling compact. Overall, the layout feels orderly and information‑rich, with modest spacing and subtle gradients, guiding the user from contextual header through filters and down through a text‑centric list of resources.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header context bar sits immediately below the site’s global navigation within the centered white page container, stretching the full inner width of the container (≈900–980px). It forms a horizontal band about 56–64px tall, echoing the mockup’s beveled, slightly gradient header strips: a light blue or grey‑blue background with a subtle top highlight and bottom shadow line. The page title (e.g., “Resources”) is left‑aligned inside this band, starting about 16px from the left edge, with 12–16px top and bottom padding so the text feels comfortably centered vertically but not too spacious. To its right, there is empty space or optional secondary context text; the bar feels like a full‑width header but visually weighted toward the left, reinforcing the F‑pattern. The bottom edge of this bar lines up above the breadcrumb row with a modest separation of about 8–12px, preserving the compact, stacked‑band structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element, fixed within the overall vertical flow of the page. It always occupies the full width of the inner container and does not scroll or stick independently from the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation is placed directly beneath the header context bar, within the same fixed‑width container, forming a slim horizontal strip. It starts flush with the left content margin, aligning vertically with the page title above. The breadcrumb text sits on a plain white background with no heavy banding, giving it a lighter, tertiary feel. The top margin between the header context bar and the breadcrumbs is around 8px, and the bottom margin before the two‑column content area begins is another 8–12px. The breadcrumb line occupies the full available width but the actual crumbs are compact, sitting within the leftmost third of the width, leaving open whitespace to the right. Text is small and tightly spaced, with subtle separators (e.g., “>”) and minimal vertical padding (4–6px), so the strip adds context without increasing height significantly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline/ block hybrid element at the top of the content area. It participates in the normal vertical flow, pushing down the two-column layout that follows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource-type switcher sits at the very top of the right main column, horizontally aligned with the top of the filter sidebar to its left. It is positioned just below the breadcrumb strip, with a vertical gap of about 12px, so that the first line of the type switcher title or tabs visually anchors the main content column. The switcher occupies the left half to two‑thirds of the main column width, leaving some breathing room to its right and ensuring it doesn’t feel like a full-width slab. The controls themselves (e.g., two or three toggles for ‘Case Studies’, ‘Webinars/Events’, etc.) are laid out in a horizontal row, with each option in a small beveled segment or tab approximately 80–120px wide, 28–32px tall, and separated by 4–8px gaps. The active state uses a stronger blue base with a subtle red underline or accent, mirroring the mockup’s red active nav item, while inactive options sit on a light grey/blue. The vertical padding above and below the switcher controls is compact (8–12px total), and there is a modest 8–12px gap below before the results-summary/sort band begins, contributing to a tight but legible stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static component anchored to the top of the main content column. It scrolls away with the rest of the main content as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource filters panel forms the left sidebar of the two‑column layout, starting immediately below the breadcrumb strip and extending downward for the full height of the main content area, stopping above the global footer. It occupies about 25–30% of the inner container width, with its left edge aligned to the container edge and its right edge forming a clear vertical division between sidebar and main column. The panel is composed of several vertically stacked filter modules (e.g., ‘Industry’, ‘Topic’, ‘Date’), each rendered as a compact white box with a light border or subtle shadow against a slightly tinted sidebar background. Each module has a small blue heading, potentially on a thin blue top bar that echoes the mockup’s blue section headers, with 8px padding around the heading text. Inside each module, checkboxes, dropdowns, and links are tightly arranged with 4–6px vertical spacing and 8–12px side padding, mirroring the dense news and link lists from the original design. Modules are separated from each other by about 12px of vertical space. The overall sidebar feels tall, continuous, and information-rich, visually secondary to the main content but always present to the left as the user scrolls within the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical sidebar column. It scrolls together with the main content; it is not sticky or independently scrollable. Its width remains fixed as a fraction of the page container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource sort control is part of a narrow control strip at the top of the main column, directly beneath the resource-type switcher. This strip spans the full width of the main column but is visually divided: on the left sits the resource-results-summary, and on the right the sort dropdown. The strip has a faint top border or very light grey/blue background to subtly separate it from the content above, reminiscent of the navigation bar style but slimmer. The sort control itself is right‑aligned, positioned about 16px from the main column’s right edge, and the dropdown width is moderate (≈160–200px), with the label ‘Sort by’ and the select element on the same line. Vertical height of the strip is about 32–40px, with minimal vertical padding (4–8px). There is a small 8–12px vertical gap below the strip before the first resource card begins, keeping the layout compact and control-focused at the top of the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within a horizontal control band. It remains in normal document flow and scrolls out of view as the user moves down the resource list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource results summary (‘X resources found’, active filters summary, etc.) shares a horizontal row with the sort control, located directly under the resource-type switcher at the top of the main column. It is left‑aligned, beginning about 8–12px from the main column’s left edge, with text flowing across roughly the left half of the column. The summary text is compact and one or two lines tall, with minimal vertical padding (4–8px), matching the dense, informational style of the mockup. To maintain the F‑pattern, the primary message (e.g., ‘24 resources’) is bolded or slightly darker, while filter details are lighter and follow after a small divider. The right side of the same horizontal band is occupied by the resource-sort-control, creating a balanced, utilitarian control strip. The entire band sits on a subtle, slightly darker background or thin top/bottom border, visually framing this area as the control center for the listing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text block participating in the same horizontal band as the sort control. It does not float; instead, it aligns via standard horizontal layout within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource list occupies the majority of the right main column below the results summary/sort band. It runs as a single vertical stack of resource cards, each card spanning nearly the full width of the main column (with 8–12px internal padding from the sides). In keeping with the older corporate style, the ‘grid’ is effectively a one‑column grid rather than modern multi‑column tiles, mirroring the reference article entries in the mockup. The left and right edges of the list align flush with the main column boundaries, creating a continuous reading column. Vertical spacing between cards is modest: each card is separated by about 12–16px, with sometimes a faint horizontal rule or bottom border to further delineate entries. The list begins about 8–12px below the control band and extends downward until pagination, creating a long, text-centric scrollable area. When thumbnails are present in cards, they appear in a consistent position within each card, forming subtle visual rows as the user scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list within the main column’s content flow. The list grows or shrinks with the number of resources; no masonry or dynamic reflow beyond simple top-to-bottom stacking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each resource card is a compact, rectangular block inside the resource list, echoing the structure of the mockup’s reference entries. The card spans almost the full width of the main column, with 8–12px padding on all sides and a subtle light border or faint drop shadow to distinguish it from the white background. At the top of the card, a horizontal blue header bar or a bold blue text line introduces the resource title: a small icon (e.g., document, video) is positioned to the left of the title, sized around 16–20px, with the title text immediately to its right. This top row occupies roughly the first 24–32px of the card’s height. Beneath this header line, the card contains one or two dense paragraphs of descriptive text, each separated by only 4–8px of vertical space, maintaining the dense reading feel. Metadata (date, category, industry) can appear as a single compact line either directly below the description or closely under the title line, styled in smaller, grey text. At the bottom of the card, if the resource includes imagery, a single row of small thumbnail photos spans the width of the card, each thumb about 48–64px wide, aligned horizontally with 4–8px gaps, forming a narrow visual strip similar to the mockup’s photo rows. The vertical distance from the bottom of the thumbnails to the next card above is about 12–16px, providing just enough separation while keeping the overall list dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list item within the resource list. Cards stack vertically and do not have hover-driven size changes; they maintain consistent dimensions based on content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination control is placed at the very bottom of the main content column, directly below the last resource card in the resource list, with a vertical gap of about 16–20px. It is centered horizontally within the main column, but only occupies about half its width, echoing the compact, tool‑like feel of older corporate sites. The control shows page numbers in small, bordered rectangles or links arranged in a single horizontal line, with ‘Previous’ and ‘Next’ links or arrows on either side. The active page number may use a stronger blue background or red underline, recalling the red active navigation from the mockup. Vertical padding is minimal (4–8px), and the entire pagination row does not extend high; it feels like a slim, utilitarian bar at the base of the list. A faint top border or extra 8px of white space above can visually separate it from the final card without making it feel isolated.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control anchored at the bottom of the main content area for each page. It participates in the normal document flow and scrolls with the resource list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state message appears in the main column exactly where the resource list would normally begin, directly beneath the results summary/sort band. It is centered within the main column, but its width remains aligned with other content: a rectangular message box spanning about 60–70% of the main column width, with 12–16px of internal padding and a light blue or grey tinted background to differentiate it from the plain white surroundings. The top of the message box sits roughly 12–16px below the control band, giving it similar spacing to what the first resource card would have. Inside, an optional small icon appears on the left, with the primary message (‘No resources match your filters’) in a bold or slightly larger font to its right, followed by a compact, single-paragraph explanation and a short list of suggested actions (e.g., ‘Clear filters’, ‘Change date range’) with 4–8px spacing between lines. The overall height remains modest, reflecting the design’s utilitarian nature—informative without becoming a large hero. Below the empty state, pagination is hidden, so the message visually becomes the end of the main column content before the global footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional static block that replaces the resource list and pagination area when no results exist. It occupies the same region in the vertical flow but does not alter the two-column structure.

name: Resource Detail (Case Study / Webinar)
title: Resource Detail
description: Displays the full details of a single case study or webinar and allows users to save case studies to their reading list or register for webinars directly from the page.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a single resource (case study or webinar) based on the resourceId URL parameter using getResourceDetails.
Display contextual header information (counts for quote list, comparison, reading list, service inquiry) using getSiteHeaderContext.

For case studies: show complete case study metadata including industry, publication date, geography, and formatted plant capacity, and provide a control to save the case study to the reading list using saveResourceToReadingList.

For webinars: show full webinar details including date, time window, topic, time zone, and speakers based on ResourceItem fields and computed formatted_date, and indicate whether registration is open.

Provide an embedded webinar registration form (for webinar resources) with fields for name, email, company, role, and country; submit registrations via registerForWebinar and show confirmation or error state.
Offer breadcrumb navigation back to the relevant resource listing (case studies or webinars) via a link to resource_list.html with resourceType derived from resource.resource_type.
Allow navigation to the user's reading list via the header link, typically used after saving a case study.
user_workflows: View case study details workflow: 1) User arrives at resource_detail.html with a resourceId in the URL. 2) On page initialization, the page calls getSiteHeaderContext and getResourceDetails(resourceId). 3) If getResourceDetails indicates is_case_study, the page shows case study metadata (including industry_label, formatted_date, plant_capacity_display, and geography) and a link or control to access the full content via content_url. 4) User can optionally click the 'Save to reading list' control, which calls saveResourceToReadingList(resource.id). 5) On successful save, the page shows a success message using the returned message value, updates the local reading-list-saved state, and optionally refreshes header counts by calling getSiteHeaderContext again. 6) The user may then use the header 'Reading list' link to navigate to reading_list.html.

View webinar details workflow: 1) User arrives at resource_detail.html with a resourceId corresponding to a webinar. 2) On initialization, getSiteHeaderContext and getResourceDetails(resourceId) are called. 3) If getResourceDetails indicates is_webinar, the page shows webinar-specific metadata, including formatted_date plus start and end times derived from start_datetime and end_datetime with the time_zone. 4) The page shows topic and speakers and indicates whether registration_open is true or false. 5) If registration_open is true, the embedded registration form is enabled; otherwise, it is disabled and a suitable closed-state message is displayed.

Save case study to reading list workflow: 1) While viewing a case study, the user clicks the 'Save to reading list' button. 2) The page calls saveResourceToReadingList(resource.id). 3) On success, the button state is updated (for example, disabled or toggled) to reflect that the resource is saved, and a confirmation message is displayed using the API's message. 4) Optionally, the page refreshes the header context via getSiteHeaderContext so the reading_list_item_count reflects the new total. 5) The user can click the header 'Reading list' link to go to reading_list.html.

Webinar registration workflow: 1) User views a webinar resource (is_webinar is true and registration_open is true). 2) User fills in the registration form fields (full_name, email, company, role, country). 3) On clicking the submit button, the form validates required fields and then calls registerForWebinar with webinarId set to resource.id and form field values. 4) If the API returns success=true, the page displays a confirmation message using the returned message and registration data, and the form can be locked to prevent duplicate submissions. 5) If the API returns success=false or an error occurs, the page shows the error message and leaves the form enabled so the user can correct and resubmit.

Breadcrumb navigation workflow: 1) User is on the resource detail page with loaded resource data. 2) The breadcrumb component shows a link back to the relevant listing: if resource.resource_type is 'case_studies', the link targets resource_list.html?resourceType=case_studies; if it is 'webinars', the link targets resource_list.html?resourceType=webinars. 3) When the user clicks this breadcrumb link, the browser is navigated to the corresponding resource_list.html URL with the resourceType parameter populated from the resource data.
interactions: Initial page load: On load, the page reads the resourceId URL parameter. If present, it calls getSiteHeaderContext with no parameters and getResourceDetails(resourceId). While waiting for responses, loading states are displayed in the relevant components. On success, the page state is populated with header context data and resource details; on error, an error message is displayed.

Saving to reading list: When the user clicks the 'Save to reading list' control on a case study, the page triggers saveResourceToReadingList(resource.id). During the API call, the button is disabled to prevent repeated clicks. On success, the returned ReadingList and ReadingListItem array may be stored in transient state if needed, and the UI shows the success message and marks the resource as saved locally. On failure, the error message is shown and the control is re-enabled.

Webinar registration form interactions: The form fields are bound to local state variables. On each change, local validation can run (for example, ensuring email is in a valid format and required fields are not empty). On submit, if validation passes, the page calls registerForWebinar with the current field values and resource.id. While the request is in progress, the submit button is disabled. On success, the form displays a confirmation section (using the registration and message from the API) and may hide or disable the inputs. On error, the error message is displayed and the form remains visible for corrections.

Breadcrumb click: When the user clicks the breadcrumb link pointing back to the resource listing, the page constructs the target URL resource_list.html?resourceType=<resource.resource_type> where the resource_type comes from the currently loaded ResourceItem. The browser is then navigated to this URL.

Header 'Reading list' click: The header component exposes a 'Reading list' link. When clicked, it navigates to reading_list.html without additional parameters. This is often used immediately after a successful saveResourceToReadingList action.

Conditional display based on resource type: Components that are specific to case studies (such as the reading list save control and detailed plant capacity metadata) are only rendered when is_case_study is true. Components specific to webinars (such as schedule details and the registration form) are only rendered when is_webinar is true. If neither flag is true or the resource fails to load, a not-available state is shown instead of these components.
page_functionality.state_logic: The main state driver is the resourceId URL parameter. When resource_detail.html is requested, the page reads resourceId from the query string (e.g., resource_detail.html?resourceId=<id>). If resourceId is missing or empty, the page enters an 'invalid parameter' state and shows an appropriate error message instead of attempting API calls. If resourceId is present, the page initializes by calling getSiteHeaderContext (to populate header badges) and getResourceDetails(resourceId) (to obtain the ResourceItem and related computed values). The response from getResourceDetails determines the primary page state: if is_case_study is true, case study components are enabled; if is_webinar is true, webinar components are enabled. The resource.resource_type field is used to set the breadcrumb's target URL to resource_list.html?resourceType=<resource.resource_type>. The plant_capacity_display, industry_label, and formatted_date fields from getResourceDetails are used directly for display without additional transformation. When the user saves a resource to the reading list, the result from saveResourceToReadingList updates local state indicating that this resource is saved and can optionally trigger a refresh of header context via getSiteHeaderContext to keep the reading_list_item_count accurate. When the user submits the webinar registration form, the result from registerForWebinar updates registration state (success, failure, or in-progress); if successful, the form components may change into a 'registered' state (for example, disabling further submissions) based on the returned WebinarRegistration status. All navigation to other pages uses constructed URLs with query parameters (for example, resource_list.html?resourceType=...) and does not rely on localStorage or other client-side persistence.

components.functionality: Controls initial data fetching for the page by reading the resourceId URL parameter and calling getSiteHeaderContext and getResourceDetails. Manages loading and error states for downstream components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read resourceId from URL and call getSiteHeaderContext() and getResourceDetails(resourceId).
onResourceLoadSuccess: Store resource, industry_label, formatted_date, plant_capacity_display, is_case_study, and is_webinar in page state.
onResourceLoadError: Set error state used by content components to show an error message instead of details.

components.functionality: Displays summary counts in the site header (quote list items, comparison items, reading list items, service inquiry items) based on getSiteHeaderContext, and exposes navigation to reading_list.html.
event_handlers: onInitContext: Consume the getSiteHeaderContext response from the page-init component and bind quote_list_item_count, quote_list_total_value, quote_list_currency, comparison_item_count, reading_list_item_count, and service_inquiry_item_count to header badges.
onContextRefresh: Optionally re-request getSiteHeaderContext when notified (e.g., after a successful saveResourceToReadingList) and update header badges.

components.functionality: Shows breadcrumb navigation from the site root to the current resource and provides a link back to the appropriate resource listing (case studies or webinars) based on resource.resource_type.
event_handlers: onResourceDataAvailable: Use resource.resource_type to determine the label and target for the listing breadcrumb (e.g., Case Studies or Webinars) and construct the URL resource_list.html?resourceType=<resource.resource_type>.
onClickListingBreadcrumb: Navigate the browser to the constructed resource_list.html URL.

components.functionality: Displays key summary information of the current resource, such as title, industry label, publication date, plant capacity (if available), and geography. Acts as the primary detail header for both case studies and webinars.
event_handlers: onResourceDataAvailable: Bind title, industry_label, formatted_date, plant_capacity_display, publication_year, and geography to read-only fields in the summary panel.
onErrorState: If resource data failed to load, show an error state message instead of the summary.

components.functionality: Shows detailed case study information and access to full content when the loaded resource is a case study. It surfaces plant capacity, industry, publication year, and a link to open the full content via content_url.
event_handlers: onResourceDataAvailable: If is_case_study is true, render case-study-specific fields such as plant_capacity_display, industry (using industry_label), publication_year, and geography.
onClickOpenContent: If content_url is present on the ResourceItem, open that URL (for example, in a new browser tab or window) so the user can read the full case study content.
onNonCaseStudy: If is_case_study is false, this component remains hidden.

components.functionality: Provides a 'Save to reading list' action for case studies. Calls saveResourceToReadingList and updates UI state based on the response. Intended only for resources where is_case_study is true.
event_handlers: onResourceDataAvailable: If is_case_study is true, enable the save control; otherwise, hide it.
onClickSaveToReadingList: Call saveResourceToReadingList(resource.id) and set a local 'saving' state to disable the control while the API call is in progress.

onSaveSuccess: Use the returned ReadingList and ReadingListItem array to update local 'isSaved' state for this resource, display the success message from the API, and emit an event to the header component to refresh context via getSiteHeaderContext.
onSaveError: Display the error message returned by the API (if any), reset the 'saving' state, and keep the control enabled for retry.
