components.functionality: Displays webinar-specific details such as date, start and end times, topic, time zone, and speakers when the current resource is a webinar. Also indicates whether registration is open.
event_handlers: onResourceDataAvailable: If is_webinar is true, display formatted_date along with start_datetime and end_datetime formatted according to time_zone using the application's date/time formatting utilities, show topic and speakers, and show whether registration_open is true or false.
onNonWebinar: If is_webinar is false, hide this component.

onRegistrationStatusChange: When registration_open is false or when the registration form component indicates successful registration, update the panel to reflect the current registration state (for example, 'registration closed' or 'registered').

components.functionality: Embedded registration form for webinar resources, allowing users to register by providing their name, email, company, role, and country. Validates input and submits to registerForWebinar.
event_handlers: onResourceDataAvailable: If is_webinar is true and resource.registration_open is true, render the form in an enabled state; if registration_open is false, render a disabled or informational state and do not allow submission.
onFieldChange: Update local form state for full_name, email, company, role, and country; run client-side validation (e.g., required fields and email format) based on form configuration.

onSubmit: If validation passes and registration_open is true, call registerForWebinar with webinarId set to resource.id and other parameters from the form; set a 'submitting' state to disable the submit button during the request.

onRegistrationSuccess: Use the returned WebinarRegistration and message to show a confirmation state (for example, displaying the registration status and timestamp), clear or lock form fields, and update an internal 'hasRegistered' flag so the user cannot submit again.
onRegistrationError: Display the API error message if provided, clear the 'submitting' state, and keep the form fields and submit button enabled so the user can correct input and retry.

components.functionality: Centralized component for displaying success and error messages from saving to the reading list and webinar registration, as well as resource loading errors.
event_handlers: onSaveToReadingListResponse: When saveResourceToReadingList completes, show a success or error notification using the message field from the result.
onRegisterForWebinarResponse: When registerForWebinar completes, show a success or error notification using the message field from the result.

onResourceLoadError: When getResourceDetails fails (e.g., invalid resourceId), present an error notification and optionally provide a link that the user can use to navigate back to resource_list.html without specifying resourceType.
onDismissNotification: Allow the user to close the notification; this clears the current message state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width grid, approximately 2-column layout within a centered 900–980px container. Not a modern 12-column system, but conceptually a main-content column (~70–75%) and a left sidebar (~25–30%) with occasional full-width header and navigation rows.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the classic stacked-band structure: patterned full browser background; centered fixed-width (≈960px) white page box. At the very top inside this box, place the header strip with logo and banner imagery, followed immediately by a full-width horizontal navigation bar. Beneath that, show a full-width notification band when needed, then a slim breadcrumb row. Below the breadcrumbs, occupy the rest of the page with a persistent two-column content band: a narrow left sidebar (≈25–30% width) hosting compact stacked modules (summary, webinar info, registration), and a wider right column (≈70–75%) containing the main resource article/content. Within the right column, content flows vertically in an article style: title and introductory section at the top (inside the case-study-detail-content), followed by dense paragraphs and occasional subhead+image-strip sections.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the classic stacked-band structure: patterned full browser background; centered fixed-width (≈960px) white page box. At the very top inside this box, place the header strip with logo and banner imagery, followed immediately by a full-width horizontal navigation bar. Beneath that, show a full-width notification band when needed, then a slim breadcrumb row. Below the breadcrumbs, occupy the rest of the page with a persistent two-column content band: a narrow left sidebar (≈25–30% width) hosting compact stacked modules (summary, webinar info, registration), and a wider right column (≈70–75%) containing the main resource article/content. Within the right column, content flows vertically in an article style: title and introductory section at the top (inside the case-study-detail-content), followed by dense paragraphs and occasional subhead+image-strip sections.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Keep the overall proportions similar to the mockup: about 15–20% of the vertical viewport for the logo/banner header, 5–8% for the navigation bar, and the remaining 70–80% for the two-column content band. The page box is fixed at roughly 900–980px wide and centered, with modest padding (8–16px) inside. Horizontally, allocate roughly 25–30% (≈240–280px) to the left sidebar and 70–75% (≈680–720px) to the right main article column. Vertical spacing between major blocks (sidebar modules, main sections) uses 12–16px margins; tighter spacing of 4–8px is used within components for related items.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a compact, information-heavy feel. Paragraphs in the main article sit close together with minimal vertical gaps (4–8px). Sidebar cards (summary, webinar details, form) have small internal padding (8–12px) and tight stacking of labels and values. Controls like the reading-list save control and registration inputs are fairly compact, with 4–6px padding inside fields and buttons. Avoid large white-space areas; use just enough breathing room to separate modules (≈12–16px between cards) while keeping the overall impression dense and utilitarian.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Respect the F-pattern: the eye starts at the top-left logo, moves across the header imagery, then scans left-to-right along the navigation bar. From there it drops down past any global feedback banner and lands on the breadcrumbs at the top-left of the content area. The gaze then continues down into the left edge of the two-column band, catching the resource summary card (and its red-accent save control), before moving diagonally into the main article title in the right column. Reading then proceeds vertically in the right column through headings and paragraphs, with occasional horizontal scanning across image strips. In webinar cases, the prominent registration form and date/time panel in the left sidebar act as strong secondary attractors, especially via red-highlighted labels or buttons, while still preserving the primary flow toward the main article content.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a centered, boxed white container about 960px wide, floating on a subtle patterned background. At the top of this box is a wide header strip: on the left, the company logo and name; to the right, a band of conservative industrial imagery. Directly beneath it runs a full-width navigation bar with a beveled, slightly skeuomorphic treatment and horizontally arranged text links, one highlighted in red to indicate the current section. Below the navigation, a narrow colored notification band appears when there are global feedback messages; otherwise the content slides up. A slim breadcrumb line then runs across the top-left of the content area inside the box, underlining the path back through the resource library. The rest of the page is a two-column band. On the left, a narrow sidebar column stacks small white cards with light grey borders: first a compact summary of the resource with a red-accent ‘Save to reading list’ control in its top-right; for webinars, additional cards follow with date/time, speakers, and a dense, vertically arranged registration form. On the right, a wider main column holds the detailed article: the resource title at the top, then long, closely spaced paragraphs of case-study or webinar description, broken into sections by blue heading bars and occasional single-row image strips stretching across the column. The spacing between modules is modest, creating an information-dense, utilitarian layout where the header and navigation anchor the top, the left sidebar offers quick metadata and actions, and the main right column carries the detailed narrative.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is non-visual and occupies no explicit space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits behind the page, initializing all resource data before any visible components render. It does not affect spacing, alignment, or stacking order; the visual layout begins with the header at the top of the centered page container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, no rendered box; purely functional, running before and around other components without taking layout space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The site header occupies the top of the centered white page container, spanning its full fixed width (≈960px). It is composed of two vertically stacked bands. The upper band (≈15–20% of the visible page height) has a white or very light background: on the left half, the company logo and name are aligned flush left with 16px padding from the container edge; on the right half, a horizontally stretched industrial imagery strip fills the remaining width, aligning to the top and right edges of this band. Directly beneath this, the navigation bar forms a full-width horizontal strip (≈5–8% of page height) with a slightly darker, beveled background. Navigation links are laid out in a single row from left to right, each with 8–12px horizontal padding and 4–6px vertical padding. The currently active navigation item is styled with a red accent (text or underline) to stand out. This entire header block sits flush with the top of the white page container, with only minimal bottom margin (≈8–12px) before the next band (global feedback or breadcrumbs) begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the page container (not sticky). It scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global feedback messages appear as a narrow horizontal banner directly beneath the navigation bar and above the breadcrumbs, spanning the full width of the inner page container (matching the 960px width). When present, it occupies a shallow band (≈40–56px tall) with a light, attention-drawing background color (for example, pale yellow or soft red), slightly darker than the page background but consistent with the conservative corporate palette. The message text is left-aligned, starting about 12–16px from the left edge, and can wrap onto a second line if needed. There is 4–8px of vertical padding inside the banner. This band pushes the content below downward but maintains the same central alignment as the rest of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and full-width within the page container. Appears and disappears dynamically while preserving the stacking order between the header and breadcrumbs; not sticky or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation forms a slim horizontal strip directly below the header region (or below the global feedback banner if it is visible). It is aligned with the left edge of the main content area inside the fixed-width container, but visually it spans the full page width (≈960px) as a light, unobtrusive row. The breadcrumb text sits in a single line with modest typography, starting 16px in from the left edge, and uses small separators (e.g., ‘>’) between levels. There is minimal vertical height—around 24–32px including 4–8px of vertical padding above and below the text. A small 8–12px gap below the breadcrumbs separates this strip from the two-column content band that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline, forming the topmost row of the content area. It scrolls with the rest of the page and does not resize or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource summary panel occupies the topmost position within the left sidebar column of the main content band. This left column starts immediately below the breadcrumbs, approximately 16px down, and takes about 25–30% of the container width (≈240–280px). The summary panel itself is a compact card filling the full width of this sidebar, with a white background, a thin grey border, and slightly beveled or shaded top edge to echo the skeuomorphic style. Inside, it has 8–12px of padding on all sides. At the top-left of the card, the resource type (e.g., ‘Case Study’ or ‘Webinar’) and title or short label appear in bold blue text, aligned to the left. Beneath this, in tight vertical stacking with 4–8px spacing, sit key metadata lines such as industry, application, equipment model, and duration or length. These lines are left-aligned and text-dense, each on its own row. For webinars, the panel might also include a one-sentence description at the bottom, still with compact line spacing. The card’s height flexes based on the amount of metadata but remains relatively shallow compared to the height of the main article area on the right. A 12–16px vertical gap separates this panel from the sidebar module beneath it (webinar details or other components).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, always at the top of the sidebar stack. It scrolls with the main content and maintains fixed width relative to the page container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list save control is visually anchored inside the resource-summary-panel as a compact action in its upper-right area. Within the summary card’s padded interior, the save control sits either on the same horizontal line as the summary heading or immediately below it, aligned to the right edge of the card with 8–12px clearance from the top and right borders. It may appear as a small icon (e.g., bookmark) followed by red-accented text like ‘Save to reading list’, or as a small pill-shaped button with a subtle gradient and red text or outline. The control is modest in size—approximately 80–120px wide and 24–32px tall—with 4–6px internal padding. It does not break the density of the card: the surrounding metadata still sits closely below, and vertical spacing between the control and the next text line is kept to approximately 4–8px. By being positioned in the top-right of the left sidebar’s first module, it is easily discovered early in the F-pattern scan without dominating the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and anchored within the resource-summary-panel; it does not float independently. Its position is fixed relative to the summary card content and moves with the sidebar as the page scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The case study detail content (or main resource body) occupies the entire right column of the two-column content band. This right column begins at the same vertical level as the resource-summary-panel but to its right, taking roughly 70–75% of the page width (≈680–720px). The column has a simple white background and sits with 16px or so horizontal gutter space between it and the sidebar. At the top of this column, the resource’s main title appears in bold blue, left-aligned and visually larger than sidebar headings, echoing the blue heading style observed in the mockup. Immediately beneath the title, one or two introductory lines or a short summary paragraph appear with tight spacing (4–8px). Below that, the main body text flows in dense paragraphs, each separated by a small vertical gap (≈8px). For each logical section of the case study, a blue heading bar or line spans nearly the full width of this right column—slightly taller than the body text and possibly accompanied by a small icon at the left—followed by one or more paragraphs of text. After the paragraphs of a section, a single-row strip of small thumbnail images may appear, stretching horizontally across the width of the column, each thumbnail about 80–100px wide with 4–8px spacing between. This pattern (blue heading bar → dense paragraphs → image strip) repeats vertically for subsequent sections. The right column is significantly taller than the left sidebar due to the volume of text, reinforcing the content-heavy feel. The bottom of this column aligns roughly with or slightly extends below the bottom of the sidebar modules, preserving the sense of a continuous main reading area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable, forming the main vertical reading flow on the right side. It maintains its width relative to the fixed page container and does not overlap the sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the resource is a webinar, the webinar detail panel appears directly below the resource-summary-panel in the left sidebar, separated by approximately 12–16px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the sidebar (≈240–280px), matching the summary card’s width and aligning edges for a stacked, modular look. This panel is a compact card with a white background and a thin grey border, mirroring the summary’s skeuomorphic style. Inside, with 8–12px of internal padding, the top line is a small blue heading such as ‘Webinar details’ or similar, left-aligned. Below that, key webinar specifics (date, time, time zone, speakers, format) are listed in a vertical list, each on its own line with 4–6px spacing. Labels (e.g., ‘Date:’) may be bold, with values in regular weight to increase legibility while preserving density. If capacity or registration status is shown, it can be highlighted subtly in red to draw attention while aligning with the site’s accent color. The overall height of this card is moderate, not exceeding the height of the registration form below, and it remains visually secondary to the main article while providing quick, scannable information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and stacked directly underneath the resource-summary-panel in the left column. It only appears for webinar-type resources and scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The webinar registration form sits in the left sidebar directly beneath the webinar-detail-panel, separated from it by 12–16px. It spans the full width of the sidebar column and forms one of the taller sidebar modules. The form is contained in a white card with a subtle border and faint top shading to echo beveled containers used elsewhere. Inside, there is a small blue heading at the top-left such as ‘Register for this webinar’, followed by a short instruction line in smaller text, both using tight vertical spacing (4–8px apart). Below, form fields (name, email, company, role, etc.) are arranged in a single vertical column. Each label sits directly above its input field with 4px spacing, and each field has 4–6px of internal padding and nearly full card width (leaving 8–12px inset from the left and right borders). Vertical spacing between form fields is compact (8–12px). Any validation messages appear immediately under the relevant field in small text, slightly indented, preserving the dense stacking. At the bottom of the form, a horizontally centered submit button (e.g., ‘Register now’) spans roughly 60–75% of the card width, with 6–8px vertical padding and a slightly more pronounced gradient or bevel. The button uses the site’s red accent either in background or border/text to stand out but remains conservative in size. The card’s overall height extends significantly, but still shorter than the main article column on the right, keeping the left column visually lighter yet clearly action-oriented for webinar contexts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and stacked in the left sidebar. It scrolls with the page. It does not float or stick; its position is determined entirely by its order under the webinar detail panel.

name: Reading List
title: Reading list
description: Displays the user’s saved resources (such as case studies and webinars), with options to open items, remove them, and filter or group the list by attributes like resource type, industry, or year.

core_features: Load header context to show current counts for quote list, comparison, reading list, and service inquiry using getSiteHeaderContext.
Retrieve the user’s reading list and associated resources using getReadingList.
Display all saved resources with key metadata (title, resource type, industry, publication year, saved date).
Allow users to open a saved resource in resource_detail.html by clicking its title.
Allow users to remove an individual item from the reading list using removeReadingListItem and update the UI accordingly.
Provide client-side filtering of reading list items by resource type, industry, and publication year, based on URL parameters.
Provide optional grouping of items (e.g., by resource type, industry, or year) based on URL parameters.
Show an empty-state message and a CTA to navigate to resource_list.html when the reading list has no items.
Keep filter and grouping state in sync with URL parameters so that the view is reproducible and shareable.
user_workflows:
1) User opens reading_list.html from the header navigation.
2) Page initialization component calls getSiteHeaderContext to populate header counts.
3) Page initialization component parses URL parameters (e.g., resourceType, industry, year, groupBy, sortBy).
4) Page calls getReadingList to obtain the reading_list and items (ReadingListItem + ResourceItem).
5) Client-side logic applies filters and grouping based on the URL parameters.
6) Filtered and grouped items are rendered in the reading list component.

1) User wants to open a saved resource.
2) User clicks the resource title on a list item.
3) The click handler reads the resource_id from the bound ResourceItem.
4) Browser navigates to resource_detail.html with URL parameter resourceId set to the clicked item’s resource_id.

1) User wants to remove a saved resource from the reading list.
2) User clicks the 'Remove' control on the corresponding list item.
3) A confirmation interaction may be shown (inline or modal) based on client logic.
4) On confirmation, the remove-reading-list-item handler calls removeReadingListItem with the ReadingListItem.id of the selected item.
5) On success, the component updates the in-memory reading list with the returned reading_list and items and re-renders the list.
6) Optionally, the page re-calls getSiteHeaderContext to refresh the reading list count in the header.
7) If the last item is removed, the empty-state component is shown with a CTA to browse more resources.

1) User wants to narrow their reading list to a subset (e.g., certain industries or years).
2) User changes filter controls in the filter bar (resource type, industry, publication year, grouping, sorting).
3) The filter bar component updates the URL query string to reflect the new selections (e.g., resourceType, industry, year, groupBy, sortBy) without reloading the page when possible.
4) The reading list component listens for filter state changes (from URL or internal state) and re-applies client-side filtering and grouping to the full items array returned by getReadingList.
5) Filtered results are displayed; if no items match, a 'no results' state is shown with an option to clear filters.

1) User has an empty reading list (e.g., new user or all items removed).
2) getReadingList returns a reading_list object with an empty items array.
3) The reading list component detects there are no items and renders an empty-state view.
4) User can click a 'Browse more case studies' CTA.
5) Browser navigates to resource_list.html with URL parameter resourceType set according to the CTA configuration (e.g., case_studies).
interactions: Click on a resource title in the list item: navigates to resource_detail.html with the resourceId URL parameter set from the corresponding ResourceItem.id.

Click on a 'Remove' action for a list item: triggers removeReadingListItem with the ReadingListItem.id; on success, updates the list and header context; on failure, shows an inline error message without removing the item from the UI.
Change resource type filter (e.g., from 'All' to a specific ResourceItem.resource_type): updates the resourceType URL parameter and re-filters the in-memory items.
Change industry filter: updates the industry URL parameter and re-filters the in-memory items.

Change publication year filter (single year or range, as defined by the UI): updates the year or yearFrom/yearTo URL parameters and re-filters the in-memory items based on ResourceItem.publication_year.
Toggle grouping mode (e.g., group by resource type, industry, or year): updates the groupBy URL parameter and re-renders the list grouped accordingly.
Change sort order (e.g., by saved_at descending, publication_date, or title): updates the sortBy URL parameter and re-sorts the filtered items client-side.
Click 'Clear all filters': removes relevant filter parameters from the URL (resourceType, industry, year, groupBy, sortBy) and resets the reading list view to show all items.
Click 'Browse more case studies' in the empty-state or supplemental section: navigates to resource_list.html with resourceType URL parameter set to case_studies.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The reading_list.html page relies entirely on URL parameters and API responses for state; it does not use localStorage or other persistent client storage.

Supported URL parameters (all optional):
- resourceType: Maps to ResourceItem.resource_type values (e.g., 'case_studies', 'webinars'). When present, the reading list component filters the items array so that only resources whose resource.resource_type matches the parameter value are displayed.
- industry: Maps to ResourceItem.industry enum values. When present, only items whose resource.industry matches the parameter value are displayed.
- year: Single integer representing ResourceItem.publication_year. When present, the list filters to items whose resource.publication_year equals this value. Alternatively, the implementation may support yearFrom and yearTo to specify a range; in that case filtering checks that the publication_year falls within the range.
- groupBy: Controls grouping behavior for the display of items. Allowed values can correspond to 'resource_type', 'industry', or 'year'. When set, the reading list component groups the filtered items array by the chosen field and renders section headers for each group.
- sortBy: Controls client-side sorting of the filtered items. Expected values can include 'saved_at_desc', 'saved_at_asc', 'publication_date_desc', 'publication_date_asc', or 'title_asc'. The reading list component uses ReadingListItem.saved_at and/or ResourceItem.publication_date and ResourceItem.title to sort the items.

On initial page load:
1) The page initialization component reads the current URL parameters and constructs an internal filter/sort/grouping state object.
2) getSiteHeaderContext is called to populate header context.
3) getReadingList is called with no parameters. The API returns the ReadingList and its items, where each entry includes both ReadingListItem ('item') and ResourceItem ('resource') objects.
4) The reading list component applies filters (resourceType, industry, year/yearFrom/yearTo) and sorting (sortBy) to the items array in memory; this logic is fully client-side and does not affect the API call.
5) If groupBy is present, the filtered items are grouped by the relevant ResourceItem field or derived year value and rendered accordingly.

When the user changes filters, grouping, or sorting via the UI:
1) The corresponding component updates the URL parameters using standard navigation or history APIs (e.g., modifying the query string). Parameters unrelated to the changed control are preserved.
2) The reading list component is notified of the updated URL parameters (either via a centralized state store or via a URL change listener).
3) The component recalculates the filtered and grouped view from the existing items array; no additional getReadingList call is required unless the implementation decides to re-fetch for consistency.

When the user removes an item:
1) The remove-reading-list-item handler calls removeReadingListItem with the ReadingListItem.id for that entry.
2) The response returns an updated reading_list and items array, which replaces the existing in-memory list.
3) Client-side filters and grouping are re-applied based on the current URL parameters.
4) Optionally, getSiteHeaderContext can be re-called to keep header counts in sync.

Navigation connections:
- Navigating to resource_detail.html uses a resourceId parameter: resource_detail.html?resourceId=<item.resource_id>.
- Navigating to resource_list.html uses a resourceType parameter: resource_list.html?resourceType=case_studies. Other parameters can be appended as needed by that page’s logic but are outside the scope of this design.

components.functionality: On page load, retrieves global header context (quote list, comparison, reading list, service inquiry counts and totals) using getSiteHeaderContext and exposes it to the site header component so that header icons and badges reflect the current state.

Initializes the reading list page by parsing URL parameters into an internal state (filters, grouping, sorting), triggering data loads via getSiteHeaderContext and getReadingList, and passing the resulting data and state down to child components.

Provides controls for users to filter and organize their reading list, including filters for resource type, industry, and publication year, as well as grouping and sorting selectors. Updates URL parameters to reflect the user’s selections and informs the reading list component of state changes.

Interprets the current groupBy URL parameter and determines how reading list items should be grouped (by resource type, industry, or year). Produces a grouped structure that the main list component uses to render section headers and grouped item lists.

Displays the user’s saved reading list items based on the full items array from getReadingList and the current filter/sort/grouping state. Responsible for applying client-side filtering, sorting, and grouping, and for rendering grouped sections or a flat list depending on state.

Represents a single saved item in the reading list, showing the resource title and key metadata (e.g., resource type, industry, publication year, saved date). Handles navigation to the resource detail page and triggers removal of the item from the reading list.

Encapsulates the logic for removing an item from the reading list. Receives a ReadingListItem.id, calls removeReadingListItem, handles the response (success or failure), updates the page’s reading list data, and optionally triggers a refresh of the header context.

Shown when getReadingList returns no items after applying current filters. Provides explanatory text that the reading list is empty or that no items match the filters, plus actions such as clearing filters or navigating to resource_list.html to browse more case studies.
Handles error scenarios for data loading or item removal, such as failures from getReadingList or removeReadingListItem. Displays generic error messages and provides retry actions.

Provides a persistent or conditional call-to-action at the bottom of the page encouraging users to explore additional resources. When activated, navigates to resource_list.html with a resourceType parameter appropriate for the CTA configuration (e.g., case_studies).

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width grid, approximately 2-column layout within a centered 900–980px container. Not a modern 12-column system, but conceptually a main-content column (~70–75%) and a left sidebar (~25–30%) with occasional full-width header and navigation rows.
layout.layout_pattern: The page sits inside a centered, fixed-width white container (≈960px) on top of a patterned full-width background. At the top of the container are the existing site header band and navigation (not part of these components). Directly beneath the navigation, the content area uses the same two-column structure as the mockup: a narrow left sidebar (≈25–30% width) with stacked utility modules, and a wider right main column (≈70–75% width) holding the Reading List experience. Inside the main column, content is stacked vertically in a linear sequence: page context header, then a compact horizontal filter/grouping band, followed by the main list area (or empty/error states), ending with a footer call-to-action. Within the list, each reading-list-item-row follows a repeated pattern: title line, dense metadata block, then action strip aligned to the right, echoing the repeating reference-entry pattern in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page sits inside a centered, fixed-width white container (≈960px) on top of a patterned full-width background. At the top of the container are the existing site header band and navigation (not part of these components). Directly beneath the navigation, the content area uses the same two-column structure as the mockup: a narrow left sidebar (≈25–30% width) with stacked utility modules, and a wider right main column (≈70–75% width) holding the Reading List experience. Inside the main column, content is stacked vertically in a linear sequence: page context header, then a compact horizontal filter/grouping band, followed by the main list area (or empty/error states), ending with a footer call-to-action. Within the list, each reading-list-item-row follows a repeated pattern: title line, dense metadata block, then action strip aligned to the right, echoing the repeating reference-entry pattern in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly 15–20% of the viewport height is taken by the existing site header and navigation bands. The remaining 80–85% is the two-column content band. Within this band, the left sidebar occupies about 25–30% of the fixed content width and stretches vertically as far as the main content. The right main column occupies the remaining 70–75% width and is visually dominant. Within the main column, the page header-context block consumes a shallow strip at the top (≈8–12px padding, minimal vertical height), the filter/grouping bar spans the full main-column width as a narrow control band (≈40–56px high), and the reading-list-main region takes up most of the remaining height. The footer call-to-action sits at the bottom of the main column with slightly more vertical padding than standard list rows to visually cap the content.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density mirrors the utilitarian mockup: compact, information-heavy, with minimal whitespace. Vertical gaps between major bands (header-context, filter bar, list area, footer CTA) use 12–16px spacing, while related elements within the same band (filter controls, grouping toggles, text inside a row) use tighter 4–8px spacing. List rows are closely stacked, separated by thin rules and 8–12px vertical margins. Internal padding inside cards or strips is kept modest (≈8–12px on all sides). Typography emphasizes readability in dense layouts: headings are slightly larger and blue, but paragraphs and metadata remain tight with small line spacing, similar to the long, compact paragraphs in the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a classic F-pattern. The eye starts at the top-left of the container (logo, existing page title), then passes across the top navigation. Immediately below, within the main column, the blue-toned page heading from the header-context-loader draws attention, then the eye moves right across the filter and grouping controls band. From there, attention drops vertically into the reading-list-main area and follows the stack of item rows top-to-bottom. Within each list row, the eye first catches the blue resource title at the left/middle, then moves horizontally across to the right-aligned actions (including the red remove link), similar to how the mockup uses red accents to pull attention. Secondary elements like tags, metadata, and sidebar content are picked up peripherally as the user scans down.

layout.overall_description: The Reading List page appears inside a centered white box with a fixed width of about 960px, framed by a subtle patterned background that fills the browser window. At the very top of this box are the site’s existing logo/banner band and a full-width horizontal navigation bar with beveled edges and gentle gradients, establishing the old-generation corporate feel. Directly beneath the navigation, the page splits into two columns: a narrow left sidebar of stacked modules (not specified here) and a wider right column dedicated to the Reading List. The right column begins with a compact page header showing the context for the Reading List, followed immediately by a low-profile horizontal strip of filters and grouping controls. Below this control band, the main reading list occupies the bulk of the column, presenting a dense stack of rows with blue titles, tight metadata, and right-aligned action links. When there are no items or an error occurs, a single empty or error panel replaces the list rows but stays constrained within the same main-column area. At the bottom, a slightly more noticeable but still conservative footer call-to-action block closes the page, aligned with the main column and sitting just above the global site footer band. Overall, the page feels compact and utilitarian, with a clear F-pattern surfacing the page title and controls first, then drawing the eye straight down through the saved resources.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Position this component at the top of the right main column, directly below the global navigation band and aligned to the left edge of the main column. It spans the full width of the main column (≈70–75% of the container width) with 12–16px of top margin from the navigation and 8–12px padding inside its own area. Visually, it appears as a simple text block: a small breadcrumb or section label in a muted tone on the top line, and beneath it, the page title 'Reading List' set in bold blue, similar in hierarchy to the 'Flotation graphite' heading from the mockup. Any short descriptive sentence about saved resources sits right below the title, in compact, tightly spaced body text with only 4–8px vertical separation, reaffirming the dense, information-first feel. The left sidebar modules begin at the same vertical level but remain narrower to the left, so this header-context-loader aligns horizontally with the main column but does not extend into the sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element; it flows with the document layout at the top of the main column and pushes subsequent components (filter bar, list) downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates behind the scenes to initialize the page state and does not have a visible representation. Conceptually, it is associated with the entire main content area: it is executed as soon as the main two-column band is laid out, before the header-context-loader and filter bar render. In layout terms, it occupies no physical space and does not affect spacing or dimensions of neighboring components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-size; purely behavioral and does not participate in layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Place the filter bar immediately below the header-context-loader within the right main column, with a modest 8–12px vertical gap separating it from the header block. It appears as a full-width horizontal strip across the main column, stopping at the boundary where the left sidebar begins. The strip is about 40–56px high, with 8–12px of internal padding on all sides, and uses a light blue or gray gradient background with a subtle beveled top or bottom edge to match the mockup’s skeuomorphic navigation styling. Inside this strip, filter label text ('Filter by:') is left-aligned near the top-left, and a row of compact dropdowns or pill-style filter controls stretches to the right, with 8px horizontal gaps between each control. Controls are tightly packed, with small internal padding (≈4–6px), emphasizing density over spaciousness. The bar maintains a straight bottom edge that lines up horizontally with the top of the reading-list-main area immediately beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band; it sits between the header-context block and the list area, and scrolls away with the page content (no sticky behavior).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The grouping controller is visually integrated into the same horizontal band as the reading-list-filter-bar. Within that band, anchor the grouping controls to the right side: they sit on the same vertical line as the filters but are right-aligned, creating a left-to-right tension similar to a navigation bar with right-aligned utility links. There is a small 8–12px horizontal buffer between the last filter control and the first grouping control. The grouping UI (e.g., 'Group by: Resource type / Industry / Year') is compact, using small text and pill or tab-like toggles with minimal padding (≈4px) and 4–8px spacing between options. The active grouping option uses a stronger blue or subtle bevel to echo the active navigation state in the mockup, while still remaining visually lighter than the main page title. Vertically, the grouping controller is centered within the 40–56px height of the filter bar so that its baseline aligns visually with the filter controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the filter bar; right-aligned within the same static band and moves together with the filter bar during scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component occupies the main block of the right column, directly beneath the filter/grouping bar. Leave a modest 12–16px vertical gap between the bottom of the filter bar and the top of the reading-list-main area, matching the inter-section spacing seen between content blocks in the mockup. The reading-list-main spans the full width of the main column, with 8–12px internal padding on the left and right so that list rows do not touch the container’s edges. Its background remains white, blending with the overall page container to maintain the information-heavy, text-first feel. Vertically, this region expands as needed to accommodate all items; when long, it forms a continuous scroll of dense columns of text reminiscent of the reference entries in the mockup. Any error or empty state panels are positioned at the top of this region, pushing the list rows downward or replacing them entirely, but staying within the same left and right boundaries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flexible-height block element; expands vertically with content and scrolls as part of the main column. It forms the container for item rows, empty states, and error states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each item row is a horizontally oriented strip inside reading-list-main, stretching the full usable width of the main column (minus the 8–12px side padding of the container). Rows are stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing between them and optionally separated by a light horizontal divider line to echo the linear segmentation of reference entries in the mockup. The internal layout of a row follows a three-zone structure: on the far left, a small square thumbnail or resource-type icon (≈32–40px) aligned to the top; to its right, a wider content block containing the resource title and metadata; on the far right, a narrow actions area. The title appears in bold blue text at the top of the central block, occupying a single or double line, with 4–8px of space below it. Immediately beneath, a dense block of metadata (resource type, industry, year, duration) is presented in smaller, slightly muted text, with very tight line spacing and 4px separation between lines to maintain high information density. Tags or status labels appear at the bottom of the central block, aligned left and separated by 4–8px. The rightmost actions area is about 15–20% of the row width and is vertically centered; it contains small inline links or icons such as 'Open' and the red 'Remove' control. Overall row height ranges around 72–96px depending on content. Internal padding within the row is modest (≈8px on all sides), and the background stays white to keep the list visually continuous.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Block-level children of reading-list-main; rows stack vertically and push each other downward. No fixed or sticky behavior; they scroll with the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each reading-list-item-row, the remove handler appears in the rightmost actions area as a compact, high-contrast control. Visually, it is a small red text link ('Remove') or a red-accented icon-button aligned to the far right edge of the row, with 4–8px of internal horizontal padding and minimal vertical padding to stay compact. It is vertically centered relative to the row’s height or aligned with the baseline of the primary 'Open' link if present. The red color echoes the mockup’s use of red in active navigation and sidebar highlights, making it noticeable without dominating the row. There is a 4–8px gap between this remove control and any adjacent actions, with the remove action usually placed last on the right. Hover or focus states are subtly underlined or darkened but do not change the layout footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the row’s right-side action cluster; it does not alter the row’s layout beyond occupying its small fixed area. It scrolls with its parent row and does not become sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When no items exist, this component replaces the list of rows inside reading-list-main. It is centered horizontally within the main column and uses approximately 60–70% of the main-column width, leaving equal white margins on the left and right. The empty state appears as a bordered, lightly emphasized panel with a pale blue or gray-tinted background and a subtle gradient to echo the skeuomorphic panels in the mockup. It sits 16–24px below the filter/grouping bar, with 24px of internal padding on all sides, giving it slightly more breathing room than standard rows while still fitting within the dense layout. At the top-left inside the panel, a small, neutral icon (e.g., an empty folder) appears, followed by a bold blue heading on the same horizontal line. Beneath the heading, one or two lines of explanatory text are stacked with tight 4–8px spacing. A simple inline link or small button inviting users to 'Browse resources' or 'Explore case studies' is placed at the bottom right of the panel, aligned to reinforce the F-pattern path from top-left heading to bottom-right action. The rest of the reading-list-main area below remains blank white space, blending into the container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Block element contained within reading-list-main. It replaces the list rows when active and scrolls as part of the main column, with no fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state appears at the very top of the reading-list-main component, directly under the filter/grouping bar, and above any list rows or the empty state. It spans the full width of the main column’s content area, matching the horizontal padding of reading-list-main (8–12px on each side). Visually, it is a shallow alert strip with a pale red or cream background and a thin, slightly darker red border on the top or left edge, consistent with the conservative corporate aesthetic. The strip has 8–12px of padding vertically, making it noticeable but not tall. On the left, a small warning icon precedes a concise error message in bold or semi-bold text; any additional details or retry instructions follow on the same line or on a second line directly below, with only 4px spacing between lines to keep it compact. The content below (rows or empty state) starts 8–12px beneath this alert, creating a clear separation without large gaps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of reading-list-main’s content stack; when present, it pushes list rows downward but does not overlay or float. It scrolls away with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer call-to-action sits at the bottom of the right main column, inside the reading-list-main area, after the last item row or empty/error panel. A 16–24px vertical gap separates it from the preceding content, giving it slightly more prominence as a closing element. It spans the full width of the main column (respecting the same 8–12px side padding as the list), and appears as a low-profile horizontal band with a subtle blue gradient or lightly shaded background, visually echoing the header or navigation bars while remaining smaller. Inside, there is 12–16px of padding. The main call-to-action copy (e.g., 'Looking for more resources? Explore our full library.') is centered horizontally or left-aligned depending on the site’s convention, but in either case it remains on a single line or two tightly spaced lines. A small, conservative button or underlined link sits to the right of the text or directly beneath it with 4–8px spacing, nudging users toward additional content without overpowering the page. Immediately below this band, the site’s global footer (outside the scope of these components) begins, creating a clear visual transition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static terminal block within the main column; it anchors the bottom of the reading list experience and scrolls with the page content, with no sticky or floating behavior.

name: Tools & Calculators Overview
title: Tools & Calculators
description: Lists all available engineering tools and calculators, allows filtering and highlighting of recommended tools, and links to each tool (including the equalization tank sizing calculator) and related equipment categories.

core_features: Retrieve and display header context (counts and totals) using getSiteHeaderContext for quote list, comparison, reading list, and service inquiry icons.
Load the dynamic list of tools and calculators using listTools and render them as selectable tool entries.
Support client-side filtering of tools by tool_type (calculator, selector, guide) based on URL parameters.
Highlight tools marked as is_recommended, presenting them in a dedicated recommended-tools area and in the main list.
Provide usage guidance text for each tool based on its Tool.tool_type and Tool.description.
Navigate to the tool’s target URL (Tool.url) when a tool entry is activated, including the equalization tank sizing calculator.
For the tool with slug equalization_tank_sizing, provide an additional link that navigates to equipment.html with categoryId=equalization_tanks to browse compatible tanks.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View all tools and calculators
1. User opens tools.html (via header navigation or a direct link).
2. On page load, the page invokes getSiteHeaderContext to populate header icon counters.
3. The page invokes listTools to retrieve the array of Tool records.
4. URL parameters (if present) are parsed to determine active filters such as toolType, recommendedOnly, search, and focusToolSlug.
5. The tools array is filtered in memory according to the parsed parameters.
6. Recommended tools (Tool.is_recommended = true) are displayed in a dedicated recommended section; all filtered tools are displayed in the main tools list.
7. The user can scroll through the list, read brief descriptions, and select any tool to navigate to its Tool.url.

Workflow: Filter tools by type and recommendations
1. User opens tools.html; initial filters are derived from URL parameters (e.g., tools.html?toolType=calculator&recommendedOnly=1).
2. The filter component reads toolType and recommendedOnly from the URL and sets its UI controls accordingly.
3. The tools list component filters the tools returned by listTools based on the current toolType and recommendedOnly values.
4. When the user changes the tool type filter or toggles the recommended-only option, the filter component updates the URL query parameters (using pushState/replaceState) and emits a filter-change event.
5. The tools list component re-reads the URL parameters, recalculates the filtered set of tools, and re-renders the list without re-calling listTools.

Workflow: Search tools by name or description
1. The user types a query into the search input and submits it.
2. The search term is written to the URL as the search parameter (e.g., tools.html?search=... plus any existing parameters preserved).
3. The tools list component reads the search parameter from the URL and applies a client-side filter on the tools array, matching against Tool.name and Tool.description.
4. The filtered results are displayed; the user can refine or clear the search, which updates the search URL parameter and re-renders the list.

Workflow: Open equalization tank sizing calculator
1. User navigates to tools.html and the page loads tools via listTools.
2. Among the tools, the tool with Tool.slug = "equalization_tank_sizing" is identified.
3. This tool appears as a standard tool entry in both the recommended section (if Tool.is_recommended is true) and the main list.
4. When the user clicks the primary action on this tool entry, a navigation event is triggered.
5. The browser navigates to the URL provided by that Tool.url value, which corresponds to calculator_equalization_tank.html.

Workflow: From tools overview to EQ tank equipment listing
1. After the tools list is rendered, the page checks for the Tool whose slug is equal to "equalization_tank_sizing".
2. For that tool entry, an additional inline link labeled "Browse EQ tanks" is rendered.
3. When the user clicks the "Browse EQ tanks" link, the page triggers navigation to equipment.html with the query parameter categoryId=equalization_tanks.
4. The equipment listing page (not part of this specification) uses the categoryId parameter as needed.

Workflow: View detailed usage guidance for a single tool
1. The user clicks a "View details" or equivalent control on a tool entry.
2. The component handling this interaction updates the focusToolSlug parameter in the URL to the selected Tool.slug (e.g., tools.html?focusToolSlug=equalization_tank_sizing).
3. The detail panel component reads focusToolSlug from the URL and locates the corresponding Tool in the tools array.
4. The component renders extended usage guidance using the Tool.name, Tool.description, Tool.tool_type, and generic templates for that tool_type.
5. If the user closes the detail panel, the component removes focusToolSlug from the URL, and the detail panel hides.

Workflow: Update and reflect header context counts
1. On initial page load, the page calls getSiteHeaderContext.
2. The returned object provides counts and totals for quote list items, comparison items, reading list items, and service inquiry items.
3. The header summary component binds these values to the corresponding icons in the site header (e.g., badges showing item counts and quote list total value with currency).
4. If the page is reloaded or revisited, getSiteHeaderContext is called again to refresh the displayed values (no local state is persisted on this page).
interactions:
Interaction: Page initialization
- Trigger: DOMContentLoaded / initial render of tools.html.
- Actions:
  1) Invoke getSiteHeaderContext with no parameters; bind returned counts and totals to header icons.
  2) Invoke listTools; store result (tools array) in page memory.
  3) Parse current URL query parameters (toolType, recommendedOnly, search, focusToolSlug, view) and emit a state-ready event to filtering and listing components.
  4) Render recommended tools section, tools list, and any focused tool detail panel based on current URL-derived state.

Interaction: Change tool type filter
- Trigger: User changes a filter control (e.g., selects a different tool type) in the filter-bar component.
- Actions:
  1) Update the toolType parameter in the URL query string, preserving other parameters (recommendedOnly, search, focusToolSlug, view).
  2) Use history.pushState or history.replaceState to avoid full page reload.
  3) Emit a filter-change event listened to by the tools list component.
  4) Tools list component re-reads URL parameters, filters the tools array by tool_type, and re-renders.

Interaction: Toggle recommended-only view
- Trigger: User toggles a recommended-only control (e.g., a checkbox or switch).
- Actions:
  1) Set or clear recommendedOnly in the URL query string (e.g., recommendedOnly=1 when enabled; remove when disabled).
  2) Emit a filter-change event.
  3) Tools list and recommended tools components both re-filter their data source based on updated URL and re-render.

Interaction: Search submit or change
- Trigger: User submits the search form or changes the search input (depending on chosen UX, e.g., submit-on-enter or debounced input).
- Actions:
  1) Update the search parameter in the URL query string to reflect the current search term.
  2) Emit a search-change event.
  3) Tools list component filters the tools array by matching Tool.name and Tool.description against the search term and re-renders.

Interaction: Click tool entry to open tool
- Trigger: User clicks the primary action area of a tool entry in the main tools list or recommended section.
- Actions:
  1) Determine which Tool instance is associated with the clicked entry.
  2) Read Tool.url from that instance.
  3) Navigate the browser to Tool.url. For the Tool where slug = "equalization_tank_sizing", this corresponds to navigation to calculator_equalization_tank.html.

Interaction: Click "Browse EQ tanks" link for equalization tank sizing tool
- Trigger: Within the tool entry whose Tool.slug = "equalization_tank_sizing", user clicks the inline link labeled "Browse EQ tanks".
- Actions:
  1) Trigger navigation to equipment.html with a query parameter categoryId=equalization_tanks.
  2) No additional data is fetched on this page as a result of this click; navigation transfers control to equipment.html.

Interaction: Open tool usage detail panel
- Trigger: User clicks a secondary action on a tool entry (e.g., "View details").
- Actions:
  1) Read the Tool.slug of the selected entry.
  2) Update the focusToolSlug parameter in the URL query string to that slug.
  3) Emit a focus-change event.
  4) Detail panel component reads focusToolSlug, locates the corresponding Tool object from the existing tools array, and renders usage guidance.
  5) The main tools list may optionally highlight the focused tool based on focusToolSlug.

Interaction: Close tool usage detail panel
- Trigger: User clicks a close control on the detail panel.
- Actions:
  1) Remove focusToolSlug from the URL query string while preserving other parameters.
  2) Emit a focus-change event.
  3) Detail panel component hides itself and clears any tool-specific state associated with the previously focused tool.

Interaction: Change list view mode
- Trigger: User selects a different view mode (e.g., grid or list) in the tools list controls.
- Actions:
  1) Update the view parameter in the URL query string (e.g., view=grid or view=list).
  2) Tools list component reads the updated view parameter and adjusts layout logic accordingly (e.g., card vs. row layout) without changing underlying data.
  3) No additional API calls are made; only presentation logic is affected.
page_functionality.state_logic:
State is entirely derived from URL parameters combined with the data returned by getSiteHeaderContext and listTools. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used.

Recognized URL parameters on tools.html:
- toolType: optional; string matching Tool.tool_type (e.g., 'calculator', 'selector', 'guide'). When present, the tools list includes only tools where Tool.tool_type equals this value. When absent or empty, all tool types are included.
- recommendedOnly: optional; boolean-like flag (e.g., '1', 'true'). When present with a truthy value, the tools list and recommended section include only tools where Tool.is_recommended is true. When omitted, both recommended and non-recommended tools are shown (with the recommended section still highlighting tools where Tool.is_recommended is true but the main list showing all filtered tools).
- search: optional; string representing a free-text search term. When present, the tools list filters tools by matching the term against Tool.name and Tool.description using client-side string matching. When absent or empty, no search filter is applied.
- focusToolSlug: optional; string matching a Tool.slug value. When present, the tool-detail panel component attempts to find a Tool with a matching slug in the tools array. If found, detailed usage guidance is displayed for that tool. If no matching tool is found, the detail panel remains hidden. When focusToolSlug is removed from the URL, the panel closes.
- view: optional; represents the preferred presentation mode for the tools list (e.g., 'grid' or 'list'). This parameter affects only layout logic of the tools list, not which tools are shown.

State transitions:
- Any user interaction that changes filters, search term, focused tool, or view mode updates the appropriate URL parameters using the History API without reloading the page.
- Components subscribe to URL change events (or a centralized router/state manager) and recompute their internal derived state whenever parameters change.
- getSiteHeaderContext is called only on initial page load (or full reload) and does not depend on URL parameters.
- listTools is called only on initial page load (or full reload). All subsequent filtering and selection logic operates on the in-memory tools array using URL parameters to determine which subset to display.

components.functionality: Calls getSiteHeaderContext on page load and binds the returned counters and totals to the site header icons for quote list, product comparison, reading list, and service inquiry indicators.

Reads and controls the current tool filtering state (toolType, recommendedOnly, search) based on URL parameters. Allows the user to change tool type, toggle recommended-only view, and perform a text search across tools. Updates URL parameters without reloading the page.

Displays a subset of tools where Tool.is_recommended is true as a compact list or strip at the top of the page. Each item can be activated to navigate to the tool’s URL or to open detailed usage guidance.

Renders the main list of tools returned by listTools, filtered according to URL parameters (toolType, recommendedOnly, search). Handles click interactions for opening each tool’s URL and, for the tool with slug equalization_tank_sizing, renders and wires the additional "Browse EQ tanks" link to navigate to equipment.html with categoryId=equalization_tanks.

Shows extended usage guidance for a single tool identified by the focusToolSlug URL parameter. Uses the Tool record’s name, description, slug, and tool_type to render context-specific guidance (e.g., general calculator usage steps for tools with tool_type='calculator'). Provides a close action that removes focusToolSlug from the URL.

Controls the presentation mode (e.g., grid or list) for the tools list. Reads the view parameter from the URL and allows the user to change it. Updates the URL parameter without reloading and informs the tools list component to adjust its layout.

Coordinates calls to getSiteHeaderContext and listTools on initial load, stores the tools array in memory, parses URL parameters into a normalized state object, and propagates state changes to child components (filter bar, recommended strip, tools list, detail panel, and view-mode switcher). Centralizes URL updates when child components request state changes.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width grid, approximately 2-column layout within a centered 900–980px container. Not a modern 12-column system, but conceptually a main-content column (~70–75%) and a left sidebar (~25–30%) with occasional full-width header and navigation rows.
layout.layout_pattern: Inside the global site frame (patterned background, centered fixed-width white container, logo band, then full-width navigation), this page’s specific content sits in the familiar two-column band. The left column is a sidebar running from just below the global nav down to near the footer; the right column is the main content area. Within the right/main column, components are stacked top-to-bottom in a linear sequence: the site-header-context-summary at the top (acting like the main article header), followed by a compact control band combining the tools-filter-bar (left-aligned) and tools-view-mode-switcher (right-aligned), then the recommended-tools-strip as a short, full-width horizontal highlight, then the tools-list-main as a dense list of tools, followed by the tools-page-controller just beneath the list, and finally the tool-detail-panel occupying a full-width block toward the bottom. The left sidebar visually aligns with the top of the main column but contains other site modules (search, news, categories) consistent with the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Inside the global site frame (patterned background, centered fixed-width white container, logo band, then full-width navigation), this page’s specific content sits in the familiar two-column band. The left column is a sidebar running from just below the global nav down to near the footer; the right column is the main content area. Within the right/main column, components are stacked top-to-bottom in a linear sequence: the site-header-context-summary at the top (acting like the main article header), followed by a compact control band combining the tools-filter-bar (left-aligned) and tools-view-mode-switcher (right-aligned), then the recommended-tools-strip as a short, full-width horizontal highlight, then the tools-list-main as a dense list of tools, followed by the tools-page-controller just beneath the list, and finally the tool-detail-panel occupying a full-width block toward the bottom. The left sidebar visually aligns with the top of the main column but contains other site modules (search, news, categories) consistent with the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page uses a centered container around 900–980px wide. Within this, the left sidebar occupies roughly 25–30% of the width and the main content column about 70–75%. Vertically, only a small portion of the visible height is used by this page’s own header-context-summary and controls (≈10–15% of the content area); the bulk of the vertical space is occupied by the tools-list-main and tool-detail-panel (≈70–80% of the content area height), reflecting the information-heavy character. Horizontal components like the tools-filter-bar and recommended-tools-strip span the full width of the main column but maintain modest height, with internal paddings around 8–12px and vertical gaps between components around 8–16px in 4px increments.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a compact, text-dense style. Paragraph-like descriptions in the header-context-summary and tool-detail-panel have minimal spacing between lines and between consecutive paragraphs. The tools-list-main presents many items with tight vertical spacing, thin dividers, and small paddings, echoing the utilitarian reference-text feel. The tools-filter-bar and recommended-tools-strip are visually separated but still compact, with only a subtle banding effect and modest spacing. There are no large hero areas or generous whitespace; components sit close together, with 8–12px gaps that keep the page readable but clearly information-packed.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the classic F-pattern from the mockup. After the user’s eye passes the global logo and navigation (top bands), it drops into the main content column: first catching the bold blue title and summary in the site-header-context-summary at the top-left of the main column, then scanning right along the tools-filter-bar and noticing the tools-view-mode-switcher at the right end of that band. From there, attention moves down into the recommended-tools-strip (with subtle icons and possibly red-highlighted recommended labels), then vertically down the tools-list-main. The left sidebar content is visible peripherally but secondary. The eye occasionally shifts laterally to the right edge where the view-mode toggle and pagination cluster, then continues down into the tool-detail-panel, which mirrors the reference-entry style from the mockup (title line, dense text below). Red accents (e.g., active filters, recommended badges, selected tool) provide secondary attractors along this flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s existing logo/banner strip and full-width horizontal navigation, a centered white content box about 950px wide sits against a subtly patterned background. Inside this box, the page is divided into two columns: a narrower left sidebar and a wider right content column. The sidebar, about one quarter of the width, stacks small, tight modules (search, categories, perhaps quick links to key calculators) with thin beveled headings and minimal padding. To its right, taking roughly three quarters of the width, the main column begins with a bold blue page title and brief summary, followed immediately by a shallow horizontal control band with filters on the left and a view-mode toggle aligned to the right. Directly beneath this, a light blue or pale-tinted recommended-tools strip spans the main column, highlighting a few key calculators in a single row. Below that, the bulk of the screen is filled with a dense, vertically scrolling list of tools: rows of titles, icons, and metadata stacked closely together, broken only by thin dividing lines. At the bottom edge of this list, a compact pagination/page controller sits slightly right-of-center, followed by a wider detail panel that resembles the reference sections from the mockup—blue heading bar, followed by packed descriptive text and possibly small related links. Everything is boxed and slightly beveled, with modest 8–12px paddings and 8–16px gaps, yielding a conservative, utilitarian layout that prioritizes showing as many tools and details as possible without feeling chaotic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The site-header-context-summary sits at the very top of the main (right) content column, aligned to the same top baseline as the first module in the left sidebar, directly below the global navigation bar. It occupies the full width of the main column, which is about 70–75% of the centered page container (roughly 650–720px if the container is ~950px wide). Visually, it appears as a slightly taller, white or very light box with a subtle bottom border or beveled edge. The title line 'Tools & Calculators' is set in bold blue, left-aligned, and stretches horizontally, establishing the primary anchor point. Immediately below, a short paragraph or two of summary text fills the width, with tight line spacing and minimal vertical gap (4–8px) between lines, echoing the dense article intro from the mockup. Padding inside this block is about 8–12px on all sides. Below this component, there is a modest gap of around 8–12px before the next horizontal band (the combined filter/view-mode controls), maintaining a compact but clearly separated structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element contained within the main content column; it participates in the normal vertical document flow and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-filter-bar appears immediately beneath the site-header-context-summary within the main content column, forming the left side of a shallow control band. It runs horizontally from the left edge of the main column to roughly 60–70% of the column width, leaving room on the right for the tools-view-mode-switcher in the same row. The bar is compact in height—around 40–56px—with a light blue or gray background and a subtle gradient or bevel consistent with the old corporate style. Filter elements (dropdowns, text inputs, checkboxes) are left-aligned, arranged in one or two tight rows with small horizontal spacing (8–12px) between controls and minimal vertical padding (4–8px top and bottom). The top of this band aligns visually with the top of the view-mode toggle on the right, creating a unified strip similar in feel to the navigation bar beneath the logo. There is about 8px of vertical gap above (from the header-context-summary) and 8px below (before the recommended-tools-strip), reinforcing the stacked, banded layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline-block within a horizontal control strip; it sits to the left in a shared row with the view-mode toggle and follows normal flow without sticky or floating behavior beyond standard horizontal alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommended-tools-strip is placed directly below the filter/view-mode control band, occupying the full width of the main content column. It is relatively shallow in height, roughly 48–72px depending on content, and reads visually like the 'image strip' pattern from the mockup—one compact horizontal band that breaks up the text density. Inside, recommended tools are laid out in a single row of compact cards or pill-like links, each with a small icon or thumbnail on the left and a tool name in blue text on the right. Items are closely spaced with 8–12px gaps between them, and the strip has around 8–12px of internal padding top and bottom. The background is lightly tinted (e.g., pale blue) or bordered, making this band stand out slightly from the white content around it without feeling like a large hero area. The strip sits about 8–12px below the filter bar and about 8–12px above the top of the tools-list-main, maintaining a consistent banded rhythm while keeping overall vertical usage modest.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level band within the main column; it spans the width of the main content area and remains in normal scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-list-main occupies the largest portion of the main content column beneath the recommended-tools-strip. It spans the full width of the main column and extends vertically as far as needed to list many tools, forming the dense 'article body' equivalent in this layout. The top of the list sits about 8–12px below the recommended-tools-strip. Each tool entry appears as a compact row with a left-aligned icon or small thumbnail, a bold blue tool name beside it, and one or two lines of smaller descriptive text and metadata beneath or aligned to the right. Rows are separated by thin horizontal rules or subtle alternated backgrounds, with only 4–8px of vertical padding within each item and 4–8px between consecutive rows, matching the tightly packed paragraphs in the mockup. The overall effect is a tall column of information that can be scanned vertically. The list visually dominates the central viewport; as the user scrolls, this list keeps the right column feeling like an information-heavy reference page. At the bottom of the list, just before the page controller, there is a small vertical gap of ~8px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container in the main content column; scrolls normally with the page and does not have fixed height or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tool-detail-panel is positioned in the main content column directly below the tools-list-main and the tools-page-controller. It spans the full width of the main column and visually mimics the 'reference entry' pattern from the mockup: a top heading line in blue (possibly with a small icon to the left) followed by dense paragraphs of descriptive text, usage notes, and links. The panel begins approximately 12–16px below the page controller, clearly separated but still close enough to read as part of the same sequence. Internal padding is around 12px on all sides. Text inside the panel is arranged as compact blocks with minimal vertical spacing between paragraphs (4–8px), reinforcing the utilitarian, text-heavy feel. If there are related links or small thumbnails (e.g., related equipment categories), they appear in a single narrow horizontal row near the bottom of the panel, reminiscent of the image strip in the mockup but scaled for links rather than big photos. The panel typically extends for one or more screen heights when detailed content is present, anchoring the lower part of the page as a stable area for more in-depth tool information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block within the main column; it sits after the tools list in the natural vertical order and does not overlay or float, though its content may update when a different tool is selected.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-view-mode-switcher is integrated into the same horizontal band as the tools-filter-bar, aligned to the right edge of the main content column. It occupies the remaining 30–40% of that row’s width, forming a compact control cluster (e.g., two small beveled buttons or icons for list vs. grid view) that visually balances the left-aligned filters. Vertically, it is centered within the height of the filter bar, so the top and bottom edges of the switcher align with the filter controls, creating a unified, navigation-like strip. There is minimal internal padding (4–8px), and the buttons appear in a tight group with small spacing (4px) between states. The switcher sits about 8px below the site-header-context-summary (sharing that same gap with the filter bar) and about 8–12px above the recommended-tools-strip. When users scan horizontally across this band, their eye passes from filters on the left to view-mode options on the right, echoing the F-pattern’s top horizontal scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline-block element aligned to the right within the control band; it does not stick on scroll and moves with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-page-controller is located just below the tools-list-main in the main content column, providing pagination and basic list controls. It sits approximately 8px beneath the last list row, occupying a shallow horizontal strip that spans about 60–80% of the main column’s width, usually centered or slightly right-aligned to mirror traditional page number placement in older corporate sites. The controller includes small page number links, previous/next arrows, and possibly a compact 'items per page' selector, all arranged in a single line with tight spacing (4–8px) between elements and minimal vertical padding (4–6px). A light top border or subtle shading distinguishes this control band from the list above without adding much height. Below the controller, a 12–16px gap leads into the tool-detail-panel. Overall, the controller feels like a thin, utilitarian status/control bar, visually subordinate to both the list and the detail panel but clearly visible at the end of the scrolling list.
