Provide a control to reveal or hide full contact details (phone, email, website) and keep this state in the URL using a showContact parameter.
Offer click-to-call, email, and website links based on the distributor’s available contact fields.
Provide navigation back to the Distributor Finder search results (distributors.html).
Provide navigation to the manufacturer contact page (contact.html) if the distributor cannot assist.
Handle error and empty states when distributorId is missing or the distributor cannot be loaded.
user_workflows: View a distributor’s details: 1) User lands on distributor_detail.html with a distributorId URL parameter. 2) Page initializes and calls getSiteHeaderContext. 3) Page reads distributorId from the URL. 4) Page calls getDistributorDetails(distributorId). 5) While loading, a loading state is shown. 6) On success, the distributor’s basic information (name, address, region, supported markets) is displayed. 7) If loading fails or the distributor is not found, an error state is shown with a link back to distributors.html.

Reveal contact information: 1) After distributor details load, the contact information area is initially collapsed unless showContact=1 is present in the URL. 2) User clicks a 'Show contact details' control in the contact-details-toggle component. 3) The component updates the showContact parameter in the URL (e.g., distributor_detail.html?distributorId=...&showContact=1) without adding any other state. 4) The contact details panel reads the current URL state and becomes visible, showing available phone, email, and website fields. 5) If the user hides contact details (if supported), the URL is updated to remove or set showContact=0.

Start direct contact with the distributor: 1) With contact details visible, the user clicks the phone link to open a tel: action on supported devices. 2) Alternatively, the user clicks the email link, which opens a mailto: link populated with the distributor’s email and a subject line referencing the distributor’s name. 3) The user can also click the website link to open the distributor’s website in a new tab or window. 4) All these interactions use only fields retrieved from getDistributorDetails and do not use any static or hardcoded data.

Return to distributor search results: 1) After reviewing the distributor’s details, the user clicks the 'Back to search results' control. 2) The page navigates to distributors.html (no additional URL parameters are required or assumed). 3) Any further search behavior is handled by the distributors.html page.

Contact manufacturer instead of distributor: 1) If the user concludes the distributor cannot help (for example, due to missing contact information or unsupported markets), the user clicks the 'Contact manufacturer' control. 2) The page navigates to contact.html (no additional URL parameters are required or assumed). 3) The user can then use the contact.html page to reach the manufacturer.
interactions: On page load: The page reads the distributorId from the URL query string. If distributorId is present, it calls getDistributorDetails(distributorId). If distributorId is absent, the page displays an error message indicating that no distributor is selected and shows a link back to distributors.html.

Header context loading: On page load, the header-context-provider component calls getSiteHeaderContext with no parameters. When data is returned, it updates header UI elements that display counts and totals (quote list items, comparison items, reading list items, service inquiry items).

Show contact details button: User clicks the button in the contact-details-toggle component. The event handler sets or updates the showContact URL parameter to indicate that contact details should be visible (for example, showContact=1), using the history API or full navigation while preserving the distributorId parameter.

Hide contact details (if supported): User clicks a 'Hide contact details' control. The handler removes the showContact parameter from the URL or sets it to a value interpreted as false (such as 0). The contact-details-panel then hides the detailed contact information.

Click-to-call: When phone is available in the distributor data, the contact-details-panel component renders a phone link. User clicks the link, triggering a tel: URI using the phone field from the Distributor entity.

Click-to-email: When email is available in the distributor data, the contact-details-panel renders an email link. User clicks the link, triggering a mailto: URI using the email field and a subject line that includes the distributor name from the Distributor entity.

Open distributor website: When website is available in the distributor data, the contact-details-panel renders a website link. User clicks the link and is navigated to the URL from the website field, typically opening in a new browser tab.
Back to search results: User clicks the 'Back to search results' navigation control. The navigation-back-to-results component redirects the browser to distributors.html without additional parameters.

Contact manufacturer: User clicks the 'Contact manufacturer' navigation control when they want to bypass the distributor. The contact-manufacturer-link component redirects the browser to contact.html without additional parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven entirely by URL parameters and in-memory component state, with no use of localStorage or other client-side persistence. The key input parameter is distributorId, which is required to load a distributor. On initial load, the page parses the query string and extracts distributorId (string). If distributorId is missing or empty, getDistributorDetails is not called and the page transitions to an error state with a link back to distributors.html. If distributorId is present, the distributor-detail-provider component calls getDistributorDetails(distributorId) and exposes the resulting Distributor object to dependent components. A secondary optional parameter, showContact, controls the visibility of contact details. If showContact is present and represents a truthy value (e.g., '1', 'true'), the contact-details-toggle and contact-details-panel components initialize with contact details visible. If showContact is absent or falsy, contact details are collapsed until the user chooses to reveal them. When the user toggles contact details, the toggle handler updates the current URL accordingly, ensuring that reloading the page or sharing the URL preserves the visibility state. No other page behavior depends on URL parameters. Navigation actions replace the current URL with distributors.html or contact.html as specified; no additional parameters are added to those target URLs under this design.

components.functionality: On initial page load, calls getSiteHeaderContext to retrieve global counts and totals for quote lists, comparisons, reading lists, and service inquiries, and exposes this data to the header area for icon badges and summary displays.

Parses the distributorId URL parameter, validates it, and calls getDistributorDetails(distributorId). Manages loading, success, and error states and provides the loaded Distributor entity to child components.

Displays non-sensitive distributor information such as name, address lines, city, state/region, postal code, country, region_served, and supported_markets. Does not show phone, email, or website (these are managed separately by the contact components).
Controls whether the distributor’s contact details are visible. Initializes its state from the showContact URL parameter and updates the URL when the user toggles visibility.

Conditionally displays the distributor’s phone, email, and website fields when the visibility state from contact-details-toggle is true. Provides click-to-call, mailto, and external website navigation using only the dynamically loaded Distributor data.
Provides a control that navigates the user back to the Distributor Finder search results page (distributors.html) when clicked.
Provides a control that navigates the user to the manufacturer’s contact page (contact.html) when the user prefers to contact the manufacturer directly instead of the distributor.

Optionally surfaces geographic context from the Distributor entity, such as latitude and longitude, to support downstream components (e.g., maps or distance displays) without performing any additional API calls on this page.

Listens to the distributor-detail-provider loading status. If distributorId is missing or getDistributorDetails fails, this component displays an error message and provides a link back to distributors.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width grid, approximately 2-column layout within a centered 900–980px container. Not a modern 12-column system, but conceptually a main-content column (~70–75%) and a left sidebar (~25–30%) with occasional full-width header and navigation rows.
layout.layout_pattern: The page content sits inside the existing boxed, fixed‑width site shell: patterned full‑width background, centered white page container, then the global logo/banner and horizontal navigation bands at the top. Inside the main content band, a two‑column layout is used: a narrower left sidebar (about 25–30% of the inner width) and a wider main content column on the right (about 70–75%). Distributor‑specific information (basic info, contact toggle, contact panel, navigation links, error states) is stacked vertically in the right main column. Contextual/location information appears as a compact, framed module at the top of the left sidebar. Data‑loader components are non‑visual and do not affect physical layout directly.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page content sits inside the existing boxed, fixed‑width site shell: patterned full‑width background, centered white page container, then the global logo/banner and horizontal navigation bands at the top. Inside the main content band, a two‑column layout is used: a narrower left sidebar (about 25–30% of the inner width) and a wider main content column on the right (about 70–75%). Distributor‑specific information (basic info, contact toggle, contact panel, navigation links, error states) is stacked vertically in the right main column. Contextual/location information appears as a compact, framed module at the top of the left sidebar. Data‑loader components are non‑visual and do not affect physical layout directly.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height remains dedicated to the existing logo/banner area and about 5–8% to the global navigation bar, as in the mockup. The remaining vertical space is the two‑column content area. Horizontally within that area, about three‑quarters of the width is given to the main column (distributor‑basic‑info, contact‑details‑toggle, contact‑details‑panel, navigation links, error state), and about one‑quarter to the sidebar (distributor‑location‑context). Within the main column, the distributor basic info occupies the top portion, the contact toggle sits closely attached to it, and the contact panel expands downward beneath it. A thin horizontal strip near the top or just above the basic info holds back‑to‑results on the left and contact‑manufacturer on the right. Vertical gaps between these modules follow the modest spacing of the mockup (8–16px), avoiding large white spaces.
layout_strategies.content_density: The main column follows the information‑dense style of the mockup: text blocks are relatively long with tight line spacing and small vertical gaps (around 8–12px) between paragraphs and rows. Panels and boxes (basic info, contact details, sidebar context) have modest internal padding (approximately 8–12px) and clear but thin borders or subtle shading, echoing the conservative corporate skeuomorphic style. Links and actions are compact: small text sizes, limited vertical padding, and navigation links arranged in a slim strip rather than large buttons. There is enough separation for readability, but the priority is to fit as much reference and contact information as possible without airy spacing.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual attention follows the F‑pattern described in the mockup. The eye first hits the existing logo and banner at the top, then sweeps across the global navigation bar. From there, it drops diagonally toward the upper left of the content band, grazing the distributor location in the sidebar while landing on the main content heading within the distributor‑basic‑info block on the right. The distributor name and its blue heading style form the primary entry point into the page’s content. Immediately adjacent or just below, the contact‑details‑toggle and any red‑accented actions (notably contact‑manufacturer) act as secondary attractors. Reading continues vertically through the contact‑details‑panel if expanded, then down to the lower navigation actions (back to results, contact manufacturer) at the bottom of the main content. If an error state is present, a strongly bordered message near the top of the main column interrupts this flow and becomes the first focal point after the global navigation.

layout.overall_description: Inside the familiar boxed site frame with its patterned background and centered white container, the page continues the stacked horizontal bands of the mockup: a full‑width logo and banner strip at the top, followed by the full‑width navigation bar, and then the main two‑column content area. Within this content band, the left sidebar is a narrow vertical strip filled with a compact location context box for the distributor, visually aligned with the top of the main column. The right main column begins with a lean horizontal navigation row where the subtle 'Back to results' link anchors the left side and a red‑accented 'Contact manufacturer' link anchors the right, echoing the structural rhythm of the main navigation bar but in a more subdued, text‑level form. Directly beneath, a framed distributor basic info block spans the full width of the main column, topped with a blue heading line and small icon that mirror the reference headings in the mockup. Within this block, the distributor name and key identifiers are presented densely, with tight spacing. A disclose‑style contact details toggle sits at the top‑right or just below the heading, visually lightweight but clearly interactive. When activated, the contact details panel unfolds immediately beneath, forming a visually continuous stack of structured contact information with minimal padding and dense rows of phone, email, and address data. The overall look is conservative and functional: modest gradients, faint borders, and small gaps between sections maintain the sense of an efficient, information‑heavy corporate page rather than a marketing landing. If an error occurs, a red‑accented message box in the main column at the location where the basic info would normally appear becomes the primary focal point, effectively replacing the detailed view while leaving the surrounding shell intact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non‑visual data loader that sits logically above the visible page header and content bands. It does not render any UI and therefore occupies no explicit space in the 2‑column layout. Conceptually, it is associated with the full‑width header/logo/navigation area, ensuring that the existing top logo strip and global navigation have the correct context before the two‑column distributor content is displayed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered; it does not impact layout or spacing. It executes before or alongside the header but does not occupy visual space or affect flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This provider is also non‑visual and conceptually sits just above the two‑column content band where the distributor detail lives. It does not appear as a block or panel; instead, it ensures that data is available for both the main content column and the sidebar before they are rendered. Spatially, it corresponds to the top of the content area beneath the global navigation bar but does not introduce any additional margins, borders, or visible bands.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered; initializes data for components within the content area without reserving any visual space or altering the two-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The distributor basic info appears in the right main content column, directly below the slim horizontal strip that holds the in-page navigation links (back to results on the left, contact manufacturer on the right). It spans the full width of the main column, occupying approximately 70–75% of the inner page width, with the left sidebar running alongside it. The block resembles the reference heading pattern from the mockup: a blue title bar or underline at the top with a small icon on the left, followed by dense text lines. There is roughly 8–12px of internal padding on all sides and a subtle border or light beveled edge to give it a boxed feel. The top of this block aligns vertically with the top of the distributor location context box in the left sidebar, creating a clean horizontal alignment between columns. Vertically, it is fairly compact: text elements (distributor name, type, key attributes) stack with about 4–8px between lines, preserving a tight, information‑heavy feel. A small gap of about 8–12px separates the bottom of this basic info block from the contact details panel below (when present), so the two appear visually connected yet distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main column; it expands vertically based on content but does not float or stick. It maintains alignment with the sidebar modules and pushes subsequent components downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact details toggle is positioned tightly in relation to the distributor basic info block, acting like a local action control rather than a separate section. In its most prominent layout, it appears at the top-right corner inside the basic info block’s header area—aligned horizontally with the distributor name or heading, with roughly 4–8px of separation from the text. Alternatively, if visually clearer, it may sit immediately below the main distributor heading, right-aligned within the block. Its footprint is compact: a short piece of text styled as a link (e.g., 'Show contact details') or a small beveled button with minimal vertical padding (~4–6px) and horizontal padding (~8–12px). When the contact details panel is collapsed, there is virtually no vertical gap between the toggle and the lower edge of the basic info block; when expanded, the panel appears immediately below, with only about 8px of space. The toggle uses a slightly stronger color (blue or red accent) compared to the surrounding text so that it catches the eye without dominating the main heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, anchored to the distributor-basic-info block. It does not move independently; toggling it only expands or collapses the contact-details-panel below without shifting its own horizontal position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact details panel appears in the right main column directly beneath the distributor basic info block, maintaining the same column width (about 70–75% of the inner page width). When expanded, it looks like a slightly inset card with a light border and a faint gradient or shaded header row, echoing the skeuomorphic boxes of the mockup. There is about 8–12px of internal padding on all sides, and content (phone numbers, email, contact person, address, office hours) is arranged in tightly stacked rows with about 4–8px between lines, creating a dense, reference‑style block. Labels may be aligned in a left subcolumn with values to the right, but the overall panel remains a single-column structure within the main column, not split down the middle. The top edge of this panel sits approximately 8px below the lower edge of the basic info block, giving a modest but visible separation. The bottom of the panel sits about 12–16px above the lower navigation links, continuing the modest vertical rhythm from the mockup. When collapsed, the panel disappears entirely, allowing the lower navigation elements to move up so that the layout remains compact and free of empty space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expanding/collapsing section within the main column. It pushes content below it down when visible and collapses to zero height when hidden, without overlapping other components or altering the two-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Back to results' navigation link is displayed as part of a thin, full‑width row at the very top of the main content column, immediately under the global navigation bar and aligned with the right column’s left edge. It occupies the left side of this row, with the 'Contact manufacturer' link mirrored on the right. This row spans the full width of the main column and is only 24–32px tall, following the compact look of the site: minimal vertical padding (~4–6px) and a small horizontal offset (~8–12px) from the main column’s edge. The link itself is styled as a subtle text link, possibly accompanied by a small left‑pointing arrow icon, but does not use a large button treatment. A faint bottom border or subtle gradient may underline this strip, visually tying it to the navigation bar above while clearly belonging to the content area. A small vertical gap (about 8–12px) separates this strip from the top of the distributor basic info block, ensuring they read as distinct elements while still feeling closely related in the vertical stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline link within a fixed-positioned row at the top of the main column. It scrolls away with the rest of the content; it is not sticky or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Contact manufacturer' link shares the same thin horizontal row as the 'Back to results' link at the top of the main content column, positioned on the far right of that row to create a left–right balance similar to the main navigation bar. It is aligned with the right edge of the main column, with roughly 8–12px horizontal padding from the column boundary. The link text is more visually prominent than 'Back to results'—typically using the red accent color noted in the design DNA—to act as a secondary attractor in the F-pattern. The row that contains both links is low-profile, matching the compact density of the mockup: short height, minimal vertical padding, and tightly packed text. A small vertical gap (8–12px) below this row separates it from the distributor basic info block, ensuring hierarchy without consuming much vertical space. Optionally, a second instance or continuation of this link can appear at the bottom of the main column beneath the contact-details-panel, aligned to the right, to mirror the top layout and give users an exit after reading contact info, while still respecting the dense stacking.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline link anchored to the right side of the top navigation strip within the main column. It scrolls normally with content and does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The distributor location context appears in the left sidebar column, aligned vertically with the top of the distributor basic info block in the main column. It occupies the full width of the sidebar, which is about 25–30% of the inner page width. The module is presented as a compact sidebar box with a subtle border and light background, matching other sidebar modules in the mockup (e.g., news or search blocks). At the top, a small blue title or label (such as 'Location' or 'Territory') sits with a modest 4–8px padding, followed by tightly packed lines of text (city, region, coverage) and potentially a small thumbnail map or icon. Internal padding stays within 8–12px all around, and internal elements are separated by 4–8px vertical gaps, keeping the block compact and information-dense. The bottom of this module leaves about 12–16px of space before any additional sidebar modules (if present), mirroring the stacked sidebar pattern in the mockup. Because the main column content is longer, this sidebar module appears as a relatively short stack on the left, letting the eye catch the location context peripherally while focusing mainly on the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar block. It does not scroll independently or stick; it simply occupies the top of the left column and remains aligned with the main content as the page scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The distributor error state appears in the right main content column at the position where the distributor basic info and contact details would normally begin. When active, it effectively replaces the usual main column stack: rather than showing basic info and the contact panel, a single, prominent message block is displayed. This block spans the full width of the main column and uses stronger visual cues—a red border, red title text, or a small warning icon on the left—while still fitting within the conservative skeuomorphic style (e.g., a lightly shaded background with beveled edges). It includes about 8–12px of internal padding and minimal vertical spacing between lines of explanatory text, keeping it compact but clearly readable. The top of the error block sits about 8–12px below the in‑page navigation strip (back to results / contact manufacturer), aligning horizontally with the location context box in the left sidebar. A small gap (12–16px) may separate the error block from any secondary links or instructions below, but in most cases, other distributor‑specific panels are hidden entirely, allowing the error state to be the sole main content element. This makes the error message the first and primary focal point in the content area when present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement block within the main column. When shown, it hides or supersedes the normal distributor detail content; it does not overlay other elements, but rather occupies their space in the vertical stacking order.

name: Service Inquiry
title: Service Inquiry
description: Page to review selected maintenance/service plans, adjust quantities, enter contact details, and submit a service inquiry.

core_features: Load and display the current draft service inquiry and its items using getServiceInquiry.
Show a structured list of all service inquiry items, including maintenance plan attributes such as country, equipment type, response time, coverage level, and annual cost.
Allow users to navigate to the maintenance plan’s detail (product.html) by clicking on a maintenance plan name in the list.
Allow users to adjust the quantity of each service inquiry item and persist the change via updateServiceInquiryItemQuantity.
Allow users to remove items from the service inquiry via removeServiceInquiryItem.
Automatically display updated totals (total_estimated_cost and currency) based on the latest ServiceInquiry from API responses.
Provide a contact form bound to ServiceInquiry contact fields for name, email, phone, company, country, message, and preferred_contact_method.
Validate and submit the service inquiry with contact information using submitServiceInquiry and display submission confirmation based on the returned result.
Handle empty-state behavior by guiding users to browse more maintenance plans via navigation to services.html with the appropriate URL parameter.
Reflect header-level context (counts for service inquiry and other lists) using getSiteHeaderContext, so the header “service inquiry” indicator stays in sync with the current inquiry.
user_workflows:
Review and adjust a draft service inquiry:
1. User navigates to service_inquiry.html.
2. On page initialization, the page calls getSiteHeaderContext to populate header counters and getServiceInquiry to fetch the current ServiceInquiry and its items.
3. The service inquiry list component renders all items returned by getServiceInquiry, including key MaintenancePlan attributes and the current quantity and annual_cost per item.
4. The summary component displays total_estimated_cost and currency from the ServiceInquiry.
5. If the user changes a quantity for an item and confirms the change (e.g., via an update action), the page calls updateServiceInquiryItemQuantity with the item’s id and the new quantity.
6. After a successful update, the component refreshes its state from the returned service_inquiry and items, updating both line-level values and the total.
7. If the user chooses to remove an item, the page calls removeServiceInquiryItem with that item’s id and re-renders the list from the updated response.
8. If all items are removed, the list hides and an empty-state component appears with a link to services.html with a serviceType query parameter, enabling the user to add new maintenance plans.

Inspect maintenance plan details from the inquiry list:
1. User views the service inquiry list loaded via getServiceInquiry.
2. For any row, the maintenance_plan_name is rendered as a link.
3. When the user clicks the name, the browser navigates to product.html with a maintenancePlanId query parameter set to the row’s maintenance_plan_id.
4. On returning to service_inquiry.html (e.g., via navigation), the page reloads getServiceInquiry to reflect any changes to the inquiry made elsewhere.

Submit a service inquiry with contact preferences:
1. User ensures the service inquiry list is correct (items and quantities) using interactions described above.
2. In the contact form component, the user enters contact_name, contact_email, and any optional fields (contact_phone, company, country, message) and selects a preferred_contact_method.
3. The form validates required fields and basic formats (such as email syntax) on the client side.
4. On form submission, the page calls submitServiceInquiry with the current form values.
5. If submitServiceInquiry returns success=true, the page updates its local state with the returned service_inquiry, including its status, and shows a confirmation state (e.g., a submission confirmation component) without requiring a full page reload.
6. The page updates the URL query string to include a status parameter indicating submission, so that if the user reloads the page, the UI can re-enter the confirmation state when it detects both the ServiceInquiry.status from getServiceInquiry and the status query parameter.
7. If the API returns success=false, the page displays an inline error message based on the returned message and keeps the form enabled for correction and resubmission.

Continue browsing maintenance plans from an empty or incomplete inquiry:
1. If getServiceInquiry returns a ServiceInquiry with no items, the empty-state component is displayed instead of the standard list and summary.
2. The empty-state includes a primary action button or link.
3. When clicked, the browser navigates to services.html with a query parameter indicating serviceType for maintenance plans.
4. On services.html or product.html, the user can add maintenance plans using separate page functionality, and then return to service_inquiry.html via navigation.
5. When the user comes back, getServiceInquiry is called again to show the newly added items.
interactions:
Page initialization:
- Event: onPageLoad
- Actions:
  - Call getSiteHeaderContext and bind results to header counters.
  - Call getServiceInquiry and bind the returned ServiceInquiry and associated items/maintenance_plan data to the list, summary, and contact form components.
  - Read any URL query parameters (e.g., status, highlightItemId) and store them in the page state for conditional rendering (such as showing confirmation or highlighting a specific item).

Click maintenance plan name to view detail:
- Component: service-inquiry-list
- Event: onClickPlanName
- Data: maintenance_plan_id for the clicked item
- Action: Navigate to product.html with a maintenancePlanId query parameter set to the maintenance_plan_id of the selected ServiceInquiryItem’s MaintenancePlan.

Change service inquiry item quantity:
- Component: service-inquiry-list
- Event: onQuantityChange (e.g., user edits a numeric input)
- Action: Update local UI state to reflect the new input value but do not persist yet.
- Event: onQuantityUpdateConfirm (e.g., user blurs the field or clicks an update control)
- Validation: Ensure the quantity is a valid positive number according to client-side rules.
- Action: Call updateServiceInquiryItemQuantity with the relevant serviceInquiryItemId and quantity.
- Response handling:
  - If success=true, replace the current ServiceInquiry and items state with the returned service_inquiry and items, refreshing line totals and overall totals.
  - If success=false, revert the quantity UI to the last known valid value from the server and display an error message.

Remove a service inquiry item:
- Component: service-inquiry-list
- Event: onClickRemoveItem
- Action: Optionally show a client-side confirmation prompt.
- If confirmed: Call removeServiceInquiryItem with the serviceInquiryItemId.
- Response handling:
  - If success=true, use the returned service_inquiry and items to re-render the list and summary.
  - If there are no remaining items, automatically switch to the empty-state component.
  - Update the header context asynchronously by either reusing existing data (if available) or by calling getSiteHeaderContext again if required by the implementation.

Navigate to browse more maintenance plans:
- Component: service-inquiry-empty-state or service-inquiry-summary
- Event: onClickBrowseMorePlans
- Action: Navigate to services.html with a query parameter indicating the maintenance plan service type (e.g., services.html?serviceType=maintenance_plans) as defined by the outgoing navigation contract.

Fill and submit the contact form:
- Component: service-inquiry-contact-form
- Event: onFieldChange
- Action: Update local form state for each field (contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, company, country, message, preferred_contact_method) and optionally mirror changes into a local representation of the ServiceInquiry contact fields.
- Event: onSubmit
- Validation:
  - Ensure required fields (at minimum contact_name and contact_email) are not empty.
  - Validate email field format.
  - Optionally validate that preferred_contact_method is set when appropriate.
- If validation passes:
  - Call submitServiceInquiry with the current form values.
- Response handling:
  - If success=true:
    - Update the page’s ServiceInquiry state with the response’s service_inquiry.
    - Display the submission confirmation component.
    - Disable further edits in the form and list components if the ServiceInquiry.status indicates a submitted/closed state.
    - Update the URL query string using history APIs to add a status parameter indicating submission.
  - If success=false:
    - Show an inline error message using the returned message.
    - Keep the form enabled for further edits and resubmission.

Display submission confirmation on reload:
- Event: onPageLoad (after getServiceInquiry resolves)
- Action:
  - Read the status query parameter from the URL.
  - If the status query parameter indicates a submitted state and the ServiceInquiry.status from getServiceInquiry also reflects a submitted or closed state, render the submission confirmation component in place of the active form state.
  - If the URL status parameter does not match the ServiceInquiry.status, the page state follows the authoritative API status (for example, showing a draft form if the API says draft).

Header context refresh:
- Event: onInquiryChanged (quantity updated, item removed, or inquiry submitted)
- Action: Optionally call getSiteHeaderContext again to ensure the header’s service_inquiry_item_count (and other counters if relevant) reflect the updated state.
- Binding: Update header components with the returned counts.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Service Inquiry page is primarily driven by server-side state from getServiceInquiry, but URL query parameters are used to influence client-side presentation and navigation behavior. The following query parameters are read and/or written:
- status: Represents the high-level UI mode of the page related to submission (for example, indicating that a submission has just occurred). After a successful submitServiceInquiry call, the page updates the URL to include a status parameter that corresponds to a submitted state. On page load, the page reads this parameter and, in combination with the ServiceInquiry.status returned by getServiceInquiry, decides whether to render the draft mode (editable list and form) or the confirmation mode (read-only state with a confirmation message). The API’s ServiceInquiry.status remains the source of truth; the URL parameter is only used to restore the appropriate UI presentation after navigation or reload.
- highlightItemId (optional): Can be added to the URL when returning from product.html or other pages. If present, the service-inquiry-list component can use this value to visually emphasize the corresponding ServiceInquiryItem row (if it exists in the items array) without modifying server state. This is a purely client-side visual state derived from a URL parameter.

The page does not rely on localStorage or other persistent client storage; all authoritative data about the inquiry (items, totals, contact fields, and submission status) is loaded from getServiceInquiry or updated via updateServiceInquiryItemQuantity, removeServiceInquiryItem, and submitServiceInquiry. URL parameters only affect client-side view modes and navigation targets and do not override any server-side data.

components.functionality: Loads header-wide counters such as the number of items in the service inquiry and other lists and binds them to header icons or indicators.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getSiteHeaderContext when the page loads and bind returned values to header components.
onInquiryChanged: Optionally re-call getSiteHeaderContext after any change to the service inquiry (item quantity update, item removal, or successful submission) to refresh counts.

components.functionality: Initializes and refreshes the ServiceInquiry data model for the page, including items and associated MaintenancePlan details.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getServiceInquiry, store the returned service_inquiry object and items array in a shared page state, and notify downstream components (list, summary, contact form, empty state, confirmation) to render.
onRefreshRequested: Re-call getServiceInquiry when other components request a full reload (e.g., after navigation back from another page if needed).

components.functionality: Displays all ServiceInquiryItem entries along with their related MaintenancePlan attributes and supports quantity updates, item removal, and navigation to plan details.
event_handlers: onRender: For each item in items[], render maintenance_plan_name (as a link), maintenance_plan.country, maintenance_plan.equipment_type, maintenance_plan.response_time_hours, maintenance_plan.coverage_level (if available), item.quantity, item.annual_cost, and item.currency, as well as any combined calculations derived from the API response such as estimated line totals if provided.
onClickPlanName: Navigate to product.html with a maintenancePlanId query parameter set to the clicked item's maintenance_plan_id.
onQuantityChange: Update the local, unsaved quantity value for the row when the user changes the quantity field.

onQuantityUpdateConfirm: Call updateServiceInquiryItemQuantity with the row’s item.id and the current quantity value. On success, update the shared ServiceInquiry and items state with the API response. On failure, revert the UI to the last known valid quantity and display an error.

onClickRemoveItem: After optional client-side confirmation, call removeServiceInquiryItem with the item.id. On success, update the shared ServiceInquiry and items state. If no items remain, signal the empty-state component to show.
onURLStateApplied: If a highlightItemId parameter is present in the URL and matches an item.id, mark that list row as highlighted using client-side state only.

components.functionality: Shows aggregated information about the current ServiceInquiry, such as total_estimated_cost and currency, and provides a call-to-action to browse more plans when appropriate.
event_handlers: onRender: Display total_estimated_cost and currency as returned by the ServiceInquiry, and optionally show the count of items derived from the items array when available.
onInquiryUpdated: When the ServiceInquiry or items change (quantity updates, removals), re-render to reflect the new totals.
onClickBrowseMorePlans: Navigate to services.html with a query parameter specifying the maintenance plan service type as defined by the page’s outgoing navigation contract.

components.functionality: Displays a message and navigation option when the ServiceInquiry contains no items.
event_handlers: onRender: Check whether the items array from getServiceInquiry is empty. If so, hide the list and summary components and show this component instead.
onClickBrowseMorePlans: Navigate to services.html with a query parameter specifying the maintenance plan service type so the user can select new plans.

components.functionality: Captures the user’s contact details and message, binds them to ServiceInquiry contact fields, and submits the inquiry via submitServiceInquiry.
event_handlers: onInit: Pre-fill form fields from the ServiceInquiry contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, company, country, message, and preferred_contact_method properties if they are populated in the draft inquiry.

onFieldChange: Update local form state whenever the user edits contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, company, country, message, or preferred_contact_method, and optionally mirror these values in a local ServiceInquiry representation.

onSubmit: Validate required fields and then call submitServiceInquiry with contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, company, country, message, and preferred_contact_method from the form. On success, update the shared ServiceInquiry state with the returned object, disable form inputs if the status indicates submission or closure, and trigger the submission-confirmation component. Also update the URL query string to add or update a status parameter to reflect the submitted state. On failure, display the returned message as an inline error and keep the form editable.

onExternalStatusChange: If ServiceInquiry.status changes to submitted or closed from another source (e.g., due to a refresh), disable form fields and show read-only values while delegating user feedback to the submission-confirmation component.

components.functionality: Shows confirmation that the service inquiry has been submitted successfully and reflects the current status of the ServiceInquiry.
event_handlers: onRender: If ServiceInquiry.status indicates a submitted or closed state, render a confirmation message and key details (such as contact_name and the number of items) in a read-only format and hide interactive controls that would alter the inquiry.

onStatusURLCheck: On page load, compare the URL status query parameter with ServiceInquiry.status. If they both represent a submitted state, show this component immediately instead of the editable form. If ServiceInquiry.status indicates draft while the URL suggests submitted, prioritize the API status and either show or hide the confirmation accordingly.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width grid, approximately 2-column layout within a centered 900–980px container. Not a modern 12-column system, but conceptually a main-content column (~70–75%) and a left sidebar (~25–30%) with occasional full-width header and navigation rows.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the classic stacked bands: the existing site header and navigation occupy full width at the top inside a centered, boxed page container. Below the navigation, the Service Inquiry page content forms a two-column band: a narrower left sidebar (~25–30% of the container width) and a wider main content column (~70–75%) on the right. Within the main column, content flows vertically: page heading and header-context-badge at the top, then the service-inquiry-list (review and quantity adjustments), then the service-inquiry-contact-form, and finally any submission-confirmation state replacing the list + form when applicable. The left sidebar stacks compact boxes vertically, with the service-inquiry-summary at the top and any related empty/auxiliary text following similar module styling if needed. Empty states appear in the main column in place of list + form, preserving the overall two-column structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the classic stacked bands: the existing site header and navigation occupy full width at the top inside a centered, boxed page container. Below the navigation, the Service Inquiry page content forms a two-column band: a narrower left sidebar (~25–30% of the container width) and a wider main content column (~70–75%) on the right. Within the main column, content flows vertically: page heading and header-context-badge at the top, then the service-inquiry-list (review and quantity adjustments), then the service-inquiry-contact-form, and finally any submission-confirmation state replacing the list + form when applicable. The left sidebar stacks compact boxes vertically, with the service-inquiry-summary at the top and any related empty/auxiliary text following similar module styling if needed. Empty states appear in the main column in place of list + form, preserving the overall two-column structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Within a fixed-width (~900–980px) centered container, the top ~20–25% of the viewport height is taken by the logo/banner and navigation (already existing and consistent with other pages). The remaining ~75–80% is the two-column content area. In that area, the left sidebar occupies about 1/4 of the width and runs nearly the full height of the content, while the main column takes the remaining ~3/4 width. Vertical spacing between major modules (summary box, list, form, confirmation panel) is modest (≈12–16px). Internal module padding is tight (≈8–12px on all sides). Form fields and list rows are compact, with about 4–8px spacing between them to maintain the information-heavy feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a compact, utilitarian density: relatively small whitespace, long forms and dense list rows. The service-inquiry-list uses tightly stacked rows with thin dividers and minimal padding. The contact form places labels close to inputs with short vertical gaps. The sidebar summary uses a compact card with condensed text and closely spaced lines. Spacing between related elements (e.g., consecutive inputs in the form, rows inside the list) is kept around 4–8px, while larger section breaks (between list and form, or between form and confirmation) use about 12–16px. Overall, the goal is to surface as much list and form content as possible in a single view without feeling chaotic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows a classic F-pattern: from the logo at the top left across the header imagery, then across the navigation bar, with the active 'Service Inquiry' item highlighted in red. From there, focus drops into the main content area toward a bold blue page heading ('Service Inquiry') and the header-context-badge just beneath it. The initial attention then moves downward through the service-inquiry-list where users review and adjust plans. While scanning down the list, the left sidebar with the service-inquiry-summary sits in peripheral vision, giving at-a-glance totals. After the list, the eye continues vertically into the contact form, ending on the red-highlighted submit button as the primary call to action. If the inquiry is empty, the main column is dominated by the service-inquiry-empty-state, with summary or contextual cues in the sidebar. After submission, the focus shifts to a prominent confirmation band in the main column, supported by the unchanged summary in the sidebar, reinforcing completion.

layout.overall_description: Inside the familiar patterned background of the site, a white, boxed page container is centered with a fixed width around 900–980px. At the top of this container sit the existing logo strip and navigation bar, spanning the full width. Beneath the navigation, the content area is divided into two columns: a left sidebar taking roughly one quarter of the width and a right main column taking the remaining three quarters. At the top of the main column, a bold blue page title 'Service Inquiry' appears, directly followed by a narrow, slightly beveled blue context badge that indicates which facility or asset the service relates to. Below this, the main column is filled by a compact, table-like list of selected service plans with controls to adjust quantities, followed by a dense contact form that stretches down the page. The left sidebar starts with a framed summary panel showing totals and key details of the inquiry in a stacked, list-like format; this panel visually aligns with the top of the main content. All boxes have modest padding (about 8–12px), with 12–16px gaps between major blocks and 4–8px between related inner elements. The overall impression is that of a traditional, corporate, information-heavy page: lots of text and form controls within a rigid two-column grid, conservative colors (whites and blues with red accents on active elements and the main submit button), and horizontal bands and beveled separators that guide the eye in a structured, utilitarian way.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-context-badge sits in the main (right) content column, directly below the page-level heading 'Service Inquiry' and aligned to the left edge of that column. It appears as a narrow horizontal bar occupying about 80–90% of the main column width, with 8–12px of internal padding and about 8px of vertical spacing above and below. Visually, it resembles a small info strip: a subtle blue gradient or beveled bar with a small icon on the far left (e.g., a plant or gear symbol), followed by concise text such as the site name, customer ID, or equipment line. It does not extend into the sidebar; it is clearly anchored within the main column. When the page scrolls, the badge scrolls away as part of the content flow, keeping the layout simple and in line with the older corporate style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block element within the main column; it is not sticky or fixed. It always appears directly under the page heading and above either the service-inquiry-list (in a filled state) or the service-inquiry-empty-state (in an empty state).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-inquiry-loader occupies the same central content band as the two-column layout but visually overlays or replaces the main content area while data is loading. Within the fixed-width page container, a pale, beveled horizontal loading bar runs just beneath the navigation and page heading area, spanning nearly the full container width minus the standard side padding. Beneath this bar, faint rectangular skeleton placeholders mimic the left sidebar and main column: a narrower skeleton rectangle on the left (about 25–30% width) where the summary would be, and a larger rectangle on the right (about 70–75% width) where the list and form would appear. Vertical spacing between these skeleton blocks follows the usual 12–16px rhythm. The loader thus reinforces the two-column structure even during loading, while keeping the visual impression compact and utilitarian.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces or overlays the content area within the centered page container while data is being fetched. It scrolls with the page but is typically shown near the top of the content area so that users see it immediately without scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-inquiry-list is positioned in the main (right) column of the two-column layout, directly below the header-context-badge, with approximately 12–16px of vertical gap separating them. It spans the full width of the main column from left to right, leaving only the container’s internal side padding. At the top of the list, a narrow blue heading strip labeled 'Selected service plans' or similar runs horizontally, echoing the blue reference headings in the mockup. Below this heading, the list is made up of tightly stacked rows, each representing a service plan: on the far left of each row, the service name and type are shown in bold or darker text; to the right, a compact block of details such as frequency and duration; aligned toward the far right, dense controls for adjusting quantity (e.g., small input field or stepper) and a numeric total for that line. Each row has about 4–8px vertical padding and is separated by thin, light-grey horizontal rules, maintaining an information-heavy but orderly appearance. The list typically occupies the upper half to upper two-thirds of the main column’s height before the form begins, with no large whitespace gaps between the last row and the form below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a standard, scrollable block within the main column. Its height grows as more items are added, pushing the contact form further down the main column while the left sidebar summary remains aligned at the top and simply becomes shorter relative to the scrolling content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-inquiry-summary sits at the top of the left sidebar column within the main content band. It is contained in a boxed panel that aligns to the left edge of the page container’s inner padding and spans the full width of the sidebar (about 25–30% of the container). The panel uses a subtle, slightly darker border and a pale background or top header strip to differentiate it from the page background. At the top of the panel, a short blue title bar labeled 'Inquiry summary' or similar runs horizontally, matching the styling of other heading strips. Inside the box, totals and key metrics (e.g., number of services, estimated monthly cost, duration) are displayed as compact lines of text with about 4–6px spacing between them, keeping the information tightly packed. There is approximately 8–12px internal padding on all sides, and about 12–16px of margin below the panel before any subsequent sidebar modules (if added in the future). Vertically, the top edge of the summary panel aligns with the top of the header-context-badge and the first rows of the list, reinforcing the two-column grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A static block within the sidebar that scrolls naturally with the page. It remains visible in the upper-left area when the page initially loads, offering a persistent at-a-glance view while the user interacts with the list and form in the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-inquiry-empty-state is displayed in the main (right) column whenever no service plans have been selected, replacing both the list and the contact form. It is centered horizontally within the main column, occupying roughly 60–70% of the main column width, and positioned beneath the header-context-badge with about 16px of vertical spacing. The empty state appears as a lightly bordered, pale box with modest padding (around 16px) that contains a small icon on the left, a bold blue title message ('No service plans selected'), and one or two concise explanatory paragraphs stacked vertically with tight 4–8px spacing. A primary action link or button (e.g., 'Browse service plans') is aligned flush left within the box, styled in the site’s red accent color. The left sidebar still shows the service-inquiry-summary panel, but its content is minimal (e.g., zeros or a short note indicating no services), maintaining the two-column layout even in this state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the service-inquiry-list and service-inquiry-contact-form in the main column when there are no items. It behaves as a standard block element in the vertical flow and scrolls with the rest of the content; it does not affect the layout of the header-context-badge or the sidebar summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-inquiry-contact-form is located in the main (right) column, directly beneath the service-inquiry-list with a vertical gap of approximately 16px. It spans the full width of the main column, respecting the container’s inner padding. The form follows the dense, utilitarian style: fields are arranged in a single column, with each label placed directly above its input field, separated by 4–6px of vertical space. Consecutive field groups are separated by 8–12px, and broader logical sections (e.g., 'Contact details' and 'Company information') are introduced by small, bold blue section labels similar to the list heading strips. The form can extend down the page significantly, emphasizing information over whitespace. At the bottom-right of the form, aligned with the right edge of the main column, sits the primary submit button in the site's red accent color, with a slightly beveled or gradient style in keeping with the skeuomorphic design. A secondary link‑style action (e.g., 'Cancel' or 'Back') may appear to the left of this button. The left sidebar summary remains visible beside the form’s upper portion, but as the user scrolls down, the form continues vertically while the sidebar scrolls away naturally.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element within the main column that grows vertically with content. It follows the service-inquiry-list in the document flow and scrolls along with the page. There is no sticky footer behavior; the form and its actions appear inline and move off screen as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-confirmation panel appears in the main (right) column after a successful form submission, replacing the service-inquiry-contact-form and, optionally, collapsing or hiding the service-inquiry-list. It is anchored near the top of the main column content area, directly beneath the header-context-badge, with around 16px of vertical spacing. The panel runs nearly the full width of the main column and is styled as a prominent but conservative confirmation band: a pale blue background with a subtle border, a small checkmark icon on the left, and a bold blue heading such as 'Your service inquiry has been sent.' Below the heading, one or two short paragraphs detail what will happen next (e.g., response time, reference number), spaced closely with about 4–8px between lines. A secondary link or small button (e.g., 'Print summary' or 'Return to services') is aligned to the right within the panel. The left sidebar continues to show the service-inquiry-summary unchanged, reinforcing the context of what was submitted. Overall, the confirmation feels like another content band in the existing layout rather than a modern full-screen modal.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displays inline within the main column, replacing the list and/or form content after submission. It scrolls with the page and remains within the bounds of the fixed-width container. There is no overlay or fixed positioning; the user can scroll back up to see the site’s header and navigation as usual.

name: About
title: About
description: About page that loads dynamic company profile content and header context, and routes users to equipment, services, and contact pages using URL-based navigation.

core_features: Load and display company profile content (title, introductory text, sections, certifications, markets served, core product lines) using getAboutPageContent.
Load header context (quote list, comparison, reading list, service inquiry counts and quote total) using getSiteHeaderContext and bind it to header icons.
Provide structured navigation from the About page to equipment.html, services.html, and contact.html with URL parameters for contextual filtering or routing.
Support URL-parameter-driven focus and scrolling to specific About page segments (e.g., sections, certifications, markets, core product lines) without using localStorage.
Expose core product lines as navigable items that link to equipment.html with product-line-related URL parameters based on the dynamic slug values from getAboutPageContent.
user_workflows: View general company information: User navigates to about.html → on page initialization, the application calls getSiteHeaderContext and getAboutPageContent → header counts and totals are displayed in the site header → the page title and introductory text from getAboutPageContent are rendered → all sections (history, expertise, solutions, etc.) are listed using their dynamic headings and bodies → certifications, markets served, and core product lines are displayed as separate structured blocks.

Navigate to equipment from a core product line: User scrolls to the core product lines block → user clicks on a specific product line item rendered from getAboutPageContent.core_product_lines → click handler reads that item’s slug value → browser navigates to equipment.html with a URL parameter (for example, equipment.html?product_line_slug={slug}) so the equipment page can use the slug for context-specific behavior.

Navigate to services and maintenance plans: User scrolls to a section or call-to-action area that includes a link to services → user clicks the link → browser navigates to services.html (optionally with a contextual parameter like services.html?source=about to signal entry source).

Contact the company from the About page: User locates a contact-related call-to-action in the About content → user clicks the contact link → browser navigates to contact.html with an optional URL parameter (e.g., contact.html?topic=about) so the contact page can preselect a subject or context if desired.

Use header icons informed by About page header context: User arrives on about.html → getSiteHeaderContext returns counts and totals which are shown on header icons (quote list, comparison, reading list, service inquiry) → user clicks a header icon (e.g., quote list icon) → browser navigates to the corresponding page (e.g., quote_list.html) possibly including a referrer parameter such as quote_list.html?from=about to preserve navigation context.

Open About page focusing on a specific section via URL: User follows a link from another page that points to about.html with a URL parameter (e.g., about.html?section=certifications or about.html?product_line_slug={slug}) → on initialization, the About page reads the URL parameters → once getAboutPageContent data is loaded, the page scrolls to and optionally highlights the requested section or product line, based on the parameter value.
interactions: Initial data load: On page load (or when the route becomes active), the page triggers getSiteHeaderContext and getAboutPageContent; rendering of header and About content is bound to the resolved data.

Header icon clicks: User clicks quote-list, comparison, reading-list, or service-inquiry icons in the header; each click triggers navigation to the respective dedicated page with an optional URL parameter specifying that the user came from about.html.

Section navigation via in-page anchors: If the About page exposes an internal navigation (e.g., a small list of links to sections like sections, certifications, markets served, core product lines), clicking one of these anchors updates the URL (e.g., about.html?section=markets) using pushState or a full reload and scrolls to the relevant section.

Core product line item click: User clicks on a core product line entry; the click handler reads the clicked item’s slug from getAboutPageContent.core_product_lines and navigates the browser to equipment.html with a query parameter binding that slug (e.g., product_line_slug={slug}).

Services link click: User clicks a link or button within the About content intended to lead to service offerings; the event handler navigates to services.html, optionally appending a context parameter such as source=about.

Contact link click: User clicks a contact link in the About content; the handler navigates to contact.html, optionally appending a query parameter like topic=about or source=about depending on implementation.

URL parameter change handling: When the URL for about.html changes (for example, section or product_line_slug parameters are updated), the About page state manager re-evaluates the parameters and scrolls/highlights the requested section or product line without reusing localStorage.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page state is governed exclusively by URL parameters and API responses. On initialization, the page reads the current location search parameters (e.g., section, product_line_slug, source) and stores them in local component or application state. After getAboutPageContent resolves, the page interprets these parameters as follows: (1) section: if present and equal to a known structural identifier ('sections', 'certifications', 'markets', 'product_lines'), the page scrolls to the corresponding block and may visually mark it as active; (2) product_line_slug: if present, the core-product-lines component searches getAboutPageContent.core_product_lines for an item whose slug matches the parameter; if found, it scrolls that item into view and can mark it as focused; if not found, no special behavior occurs; (3) source: if present (e.g., source=nav or source=footer), the page may adjust analytics or minor UI state but does not affect core rendering. Header values such as quote_list_item_count and quote_list_total_value are not stored in URL parameters; they are read directly from getSiteHeaderContext on each load. No persistent client-side storage (such as localStorage) is used; any inter-page context needed by other pages (e.g., which product line was selected) is passed through query parameters on navigation (for example, equipment.html?product_line_slug={slug}).

components.functionality: Calls getSiteHeaderContext to retrieve counts and totals for quote list, comparison, reading list, and service inquiry, and binds these values to header icons or indicators that appear consistently across the site while on about.html.
event_handlers: onInit: Trigger getSiteHeaderContext with no parameters when about.html loads or becomes active, and update header state with the returned counts and totals.
onClickQuoteListIcon: Navigate to quote_list.html with an optional query parameter from=about to indicate the referrer.
onClickComparisonIcon: Navigate to comparison.html with an optional query parameter from=about.
onClickReadingListIcon: Navigate to reading_list.html with an optional query parameter from=about.
onClickServiceInquiryIcon: Navigate to service_inquiry.html with an optional query parameter from=about.

components.functionality: Central component responsible for invoking getAboutPageContent, storing the returned structure (title, hero_text, sections, certifications, markets_served, core_product_lines), and providing this data to child components for rendering.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getAboutPageContent when the About page is first loaded; when the promise resolves, propagate the data to all dependent child components.
onRefreshRequested: Optional handler that can re-trigger getAboutPageContent (for example, if a manual refresh control is implemented) and update all bound content.

components.functionality: Displays the About page main heading and synchronizes the browser document title using the dynamic title returned by getAboutPageContent.
event_handlers: onDataAvailable: When getAboutPageContent returns, set the visible page heading text to the title and update the document.title property accordingly.

components.functionality: Renders the introductory text for the About page using the hero_text field from getAboutPageContent, without enforcing a visual hero layout.
event_handlers: onDataAvailable: When getAboutPageContent.hero_text is available, render it as an introductory paragraph or block at the top of the main content area.

components.functionality: Displays each section object from getAboutPageContent.sections as a heading and associated body text, and exposes anchors or IDs that can be targeted by URL-derived scrolling logic.
event_handlers: onDataAvailable: For each section item returned (with heading and body), create a discrete content section and register an internal anchor or DOM identifier.
onUrlStateChange: When URL parameters change (for example, section=sections), evaluate whether the About sections block should be scrolled into view or highlighted.

onInternalNavClick: If an internal navigation menu is provided for sections, clicking a section link updates the URL parameter (e.g., about.html?section=sections) and scrolls to the corresponding section list block.

components.functionality: Renders the certifications array from getAboutPageContent, listing each certification with its dynamic name and description, and exposes an anchor target so section-focused URL parameters can scroll here.
event_handlers: onDataAvailable: Render each certification entry with its returned fields; if the array is empty or undefined, hide or omit the certifications block.

onUrlStateChange: If the section URL parameter equals a value mapped to the certifications block (e.g., section=certifications), scroll this block into view and optionally apply a temporary highlight.

components.functionality: Displays the markets_served array from getAboutPageContent as a list of industries or market segments that the company serves, and can be used as a scroll target based on URL state.
event_handlers: onDataAvailable: Render each entry from markets_served as a list item or tag; hide the block if no markets are provided.
onUrlStateChange: If the section URL parameter equals a value mapped to the markets block (e.g., section=markets), scroll this block into view and optionally highlight it.

components.functionality: Lists core product lines from getAboutPageContent.core_product_lines, and for each line provides a navigation action to equipment.html using the dynamic slug value as a URL parameter for contextual equipment filtering.
event_handlers: onDataAvailable: For each product line object (name, slug, description) received, render a selectable item or link; hide or omit the block if the array is empty or undefined.

onProductLineClick: When a user clicks a product line, read its slug and navigate the browser to equipment.html with a query parameter bound to that slug (e.g., equipment.html?product_line_slug={slug}); optionally also include from=about in the query string.
onUrlStateChange: If the URL contains product_line_slug, find the corresponding entry in core_product_lines whose slug matches; if found, scroll to and visually emphasize that entry within the list.

components.functionality: Provides concise navigation links from the About page to services.html and contact.html, optionally using URL parameters to convey context about the origin of the navigation.
event_handlers: onClickServicesLink: Navigate to services.html, optionally appending a query parameter like source=about to allow the services page to adapt its context.
onClickContactLink: Navigate to contact.html, optionally appending a query parameter like topic=about or source=about for context.

onClickEquipmentOverviewLink: Optionally navigate to equipment.html without a specific product_line_slug but with a source=about parameter if the About content includes a generic equipment overview link.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and reacting to URL parameters for about.html, coordinating scrolling and focus behavior for other About components without using localStorage.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse the initial URL query string (e.g., section, product_line_slug, source) and store these values in in-memory state; defer scroll actions until getAboutPageContent has finished loading.

onAboutContentLoaded: After getAboutPageContent data is available, use any stored section parameter to decide which block (sections, certifications, markets, core product lines) to scroll to; use any stored product_line_slug to request that core-product-lines-list focus the matching item.
onPopState: When the browser back/forward navigation changes the query parameters, re-parse them and repeat the same scroll and focus logic as on initialization.
onInternalNavUpdate: When internal navigation inside the About page updates the URL (e.g., user clicks internal links that call history.pushState), re-run the URL parsing and scroll/focus behavior.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width grid, approximately 2-column layout within a centered 900–980px container. Not a modern 12-column system, but conceptually a main-content column (~70–75%) and a left sidebar (~25–30%) with occasional full-width header and navigation rows.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the classic boxed, fixed‑width corporate layout from the mockup. Outside of these components, the site logo/banner occupies a full‑width band at the very top, followed immediately by a full‑width horizontal navigation bar. Directly beneath the navigation sits a full‑width, low‑height context/status bar specific to the About page. Below that, the main content area is split into two columns within the centered white container: a narrow left sidebar and a wider right content column. The left sidebar stacks navigation‑oriented modules (core product lines and related cross‑links), while the right column stacks the actual About content: page title, intro text, section list, and content lists (certifications and markets served), one below the other. Invisible functional components (content loader and URL state manager) are associated with the main content band but do not disrupt the visual stacking.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the classic boxed, fixed‑width corporate layout from the mockup. Outside of these components, the site logo/banner occupies a full‑width band at the very top, followed immediately by a full‑width horizontal navigation bar. Directly beneath the navigation sits a full‑width, low‑height context/status bar specific to the About page. Below that, the main content area is split into two columns within the centered white container: a narrow left sidebar and a wider right content column. The left sidebar stacks navigation‑oriented modules (core product lines and related cross‑links), while the right column stacks the actual About content: page title, intro text, section list, and content lists (certifications and markets served), one below the other. Invisible functional components (content loader and URL state manager) are associated with the main content band but do not disrupt the visual stacking.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Within a centered 900–980px white page container, the top logo/banner strip occupies roughly 15–20% of the visible page height, and the global navigation another ~5–8%. The About‑specific header context bar consumes a thin horizontal strip (~32–40px) directly under the nav. The remaining vertical space is the main two‑column band: the left sidebar takes about 25–30% of the container width (roughly 240–280px), and the right main column takes the remaining 70–75%. Vertically, the sidebar modules extend downward to roughly match the height of the main About content. Internal padding within each module is modest (8–12px), and vertical gaps between stacked sections stay tight (8–16px), maintaining the compact feeling of the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is relatively dense. Paragraphs in the main column sit close together with minimal vertical spacing (about 8px between paragraphs, 12–16px between major sections). Lists of certifications and markets are compact rows with limited line spacing, prioritizing information quantity over airy aesthetics. The sidebar navigation lists have tight vertical stacking: links are placed with about 4–8px vertical spacing and 8–12px horizontal padding, reflecting the utilitarian, information‑heavy character. Skeuomorphic touches such as subtle beveled section headers or soft gradients on bars are used, but they do not introduce large empty areas.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors a traditional F‑pattern. Users first notice the company logo and banner imagery at the top left of the page, scan across to the right along the top band, then scan the horizontal navigation bar just below it. The eye drops to the About header context bar, which reinforces current location. From there, attention shifts down and slightly right to the prominent About page title at the top of the main column. As users read downward through the intro text and sections, their peripheral vision catches the left sidebar modules. Occasional colored accents (blue section headers, red highlights for active links or important status text) serve as secondary attractors that pull attention momentarily into the sidebar or to specific subsections before returning to the vertical reading flow in the main column.

layout.overall_description: The About page sits inside a centered, boxed white container over a subtle patterned background, giving a conservative corporate feel. At the top, the company logo and a banner image share a full‑width strip, followed immediately by a solid, beveled navigation bar spanning the same width. Just beneath this, a narrow context/status bar for the About section runs edge‑to‑edge within the container, visually separating the global navigation from the page body. Below that, the layout resolves into two clear columns: on the left, a narrower sidebar containing stacked, bordered boxes holding navigation lists for core product lines and cross‑links to related sections; on the right, a wider main column where the About page title sits prominently at the top, followed by dense introductory text and a sequence of sections describing the company, certifications, and markets served. Each major About subsection begins with a modestly emphasized blue heading line, followed by tightly spaced paragraphs or lists. The sidebar remains visually present but secondary, using muted blues and occasional red highlights for key links, while the main column’s text content forms a continuous vertical narrative. The overall impression is of a compact, information‑rich industrial website, with clear structure but minimal decorative whitespace, optimized for serious reading and quick navigation rather than marketing flair.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The site-header-context-bar appears as a thin, full-width horizontal strip positioned directly beneath the global navigation bar and fully inside the centered page container. It spans 100% of the container width (roughly 900–980px) and has a modest height of about 32–40px. The bar has a subtle beveled or gradient background in a light blue or grey tone, visually separating it from both the navigation above and the content below. Inside this bar, text such as the current page label (e.g., "About the Company") or dynamic status (e.g., loaded region or profile segment) is left-aligned with approximately 12px of horizontal padding from the left edge and about 8px of vertical padding top and bottom. Any secondary indicators, such as small icons or a breadcrumb, appear to the right of the primary label but remain within the same compact height.
