There is a slim gap of about 8–12px between the bottom edge of this bar and the start of the two-column content band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the page; it scrolls away with the content and always sits directly below the global navigation band at the top of the About page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-loader is logically anchored to the top of the main two-column content area, just below the site-header-context-bar, but its visual footprint is minimal or non-existent when content is loaded. When used to show loading state, it manifests as a compact, full-width message strip or spinner area spanning the width of the main column (only the right column, not the sidebar). This temporary strip sits at the top of the main content column, occupying roughly 40–56px in height, with 8–12px padding around the spinner or text. It pushes the about-page-title downward while visible and collapses completely when loading completes. The visual treatment is understated—a light background with a subtle border, matching the utilitarian theme, so it reads as an informational status rather than a dominant hero element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-fixed and conditional: it appears at the top of the main content column during loading, occupying normal document flow space, and collapses to zero height (no visible trace) once content is ready.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-title is placed at the top of the right main content column, just below either the about-content-loader (when visible) or directly below the site-header-context-bar’s vertical gap when loading is complete. It aligns flush with the left edge of the main column, roughly 16–24px inset from the container edge once the column division is applied. The title runs horizontally across almost the full width of the main column (about 70–75% of the total container width), with a modest top margin of 12–16px from the element above and about 8–12px of bottom margin before the intro text. The typographic treatment is bold and blue, echoing the mockup’s main content headings. Under the title, there may be a thin blue underline or separator line spanning about 60–80% of the main column width, further anchoring it visually as the starting point of the page’s content. There is no extra decorative whitespace around the title beyond the minimal spacing necessary to distinguish it from body text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; it defines the top of the scrollable About content and moves with normal page scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-intro-text appears immediately beneath the about-page-title inside the right main column. It occupies the full width of the main column, with text lines running from the left column boundary to a comfortable right margin (roughly 16–24px from the main column edge). The first paragraph sits about 8–12px below the title or its underline. Multiple paragraphs are stacked vertically with tight spacing: roughly 8px between paragraphs, preserving the information-dense feel. There is no large indentation before the first line; alignment is left, with justified or near-justified text to create a solid text block reminiscent of older corporate websites. The entire intro block has a bottom margin of about 12–16px before the next major section (about-section-list) begins, keeping the vertical rhythm tight but still visually segmented.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block content within the main column, flowing naturally after the page title and scrolling normally with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-list is positioned directly below the intro text in the right main column, separated by about 12–16px of vertical space. It occupies the same width as the intro—essentially the full width of the main content column. Each section within the list begins with a compact blue subsection heading (smaller and slightly less bold than the main page title) aligned to the left, possibly accompanied by a small icon or bullet on its left, following the mockup’s pattern of icon-and-heading pairs. Directly beneath each subsection heading, one or more paragraphs of descriptive text are stacked with minimal paragraph spacing (around 8px), forming dense blocks of content. Between subsections in this list, there is a slightly larger gap (12–16px) to signal a new topic, but the overall impression remains compact. The entire about-section-list block extends vertically down the main column and ends with a slightly larger bottom margin (~16px) before transitioning into the certifications list, mirroring the repeating reference-entry pattern in the mockup but adapted for About content rather than images.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and vertically flowing within the main column; it does not float or overlap and grows in height based on the number of sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-certifications-list is placed immediately below the about-section-list within the right main content column, separated by about 16px of vertical space to signal a distinct subsection. It spans the full width of the main column. At the top of this block, a blue subsection heading such as "Certifications" appears, left-aligned and similar in style to the section list headings. Below the heading, individual certifications are presented as a dense vertical list—either as short paragraphs or single-line rows. Each item is separated by a small vertical gap of about 4–8px, with no excessive padding on any side, to emphasize that this is a compact reference list. If icons or small badges are used, they are placed to the left of each item within a narrow horizontal space (about 16–24px), keeping the overall row height low. The block has modest internal padding (about 8–12px) and may sit inside a faintly bordered or slightly shaded box to echo the mockup’s card-like sections. A bottom margin of 12–16px separates this block from the markets served list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list content; it expands vertically based on the number of certifications and scrolls within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-markets-served-list is positioned directly beneath the about-certifications-list in the right main column, with approximately 12–16px of vertical separation. It also spans the full width of the main column. At the top of this block, a left-aligned blue heading such as "Markets Served" introduces the list. Below, markets are listed in tight rows or brief descriptions, stacked vertically in an information-dense fashion. Each market entry has roughly 4–8px of vertical spacing from the next, and if grouped by region or industry, group headings are slightly bolder but still compact. The left margin aligns cleanly with the other content in the main column, creating a consistent text column. The entire markets-served block may mirror the visual treatment of the certifications block—a subtle border or background tint and 8–12px of inner padding—so that the two lists read as related reference modules at the bottom of the About content. A final margin of about 16–24px at the bottom gives a small buffer before the page transitions into any later content or the start of the footer band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static reference list that grows vertically according to content and scrolls as part of the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The core-product-lines-list occupies the upper portion of the left sidebar column, directly beneath the site-header-context-bar and aligned with the top of the main content column. In the two-column band, the sidebar starts about 12–16px below the context bar, and this list is the first module in that column. It sits within a bordered, white or very light-tinted box that spans nearly the full width of the sidebar (about 90–95% of the sidebar width, with 8–12px padding inside). At the top of the module, a small, bold title such as "Core Product Lines" appears in blue or dark grey, aligned to the left with minimal top margin. Below it, individual product line links are stacked vertically with tight spacing: each link row has about 4–6px of vertical padding and 8–12px of horizontal padding, creating a compact but clearly clickable list. Active or hovered items may show a red text color or a subtle background, matching the red highlight behavior seen in the mockup’s navigation. The bottom of this module is separated from the next sidebar module (about-cross-links) by roughly 12–16px of vertical space, maintaining the stacked-box pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left sidebar; it scrolls with the page and maintains its position as the topmost sidebar navigation block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-cross-links module is placed directly beneath the core-product-lines-list within the left sidebar, separated by about 12–16px of vertical space. It occupies nearly the full width of the sidebar column and is similarly housed in a bordered or lightly shaded box with 8–12px of internal padding. A small heading such as "Related Links" or "More About Us" appears at the top left of the module, using a modest blue or dark grey style consistent with the core product lines heading. Below, a series of navigation links (e.g., to equipment, services, contact, or related About subpages) are stacked vertically in a dense but legible fashion. Each link has about 4–8px vertical spacing and 8–12px horizontal padding, with minimal line height between multi-line labels. Red highlights are used sparingly, perhaps for the link corresponding to the current About subsection or for the most important cross-link, aligning with the mockup’s use of red as a secondary attractor. This module may extend downward to roughly match the height of the primary main-column content, though it is allowed to be shorter without forcing additional whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left sidebar; it stacks immediately under the core-product-lines-list and scrolls in unison with the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-url-state-manager is not represented by any visible UI element; it is conceptually attached to the entire About content region, monitoring and updating URL-based navigation state for the page. In layout terms, it has no height, width, or visible presence and does not alter the flow or spacing of the header, sidebar, or main content. Its influence is perceived only through behavior: as users interact with links in the core-product-lines-list, about-cross-links, or internal About sections, the URL may update and content within the main column may change, but the visual structure and spatial relationships remain stable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely logical/behavioral; it occupies no visual space, does not scroll, and does not affect layout beyond triggering content updates and navigation state changes.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Displays dynamically loaded privacy policy content and provides ways for users to review data practices and reach out with privacy questions.

core_features: Load and display the current privacy policy title, last updated date, sections, and privacy contact email using getPrivacyPolicyContent.
Show header context counts (quote list items, comparison items, reading list items, service inquiry items, and quote total) using getSiteHeaderContext.
Allow users to navigate directly to specific privacy policy sections via URL parameters.
Provide interaction paths to contact the company regarding privacy questions, either via the privacy contact email or navigation to the contact page.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Viewing the privacy policy
1. User opens privacy.html (optionally with a section URL parameter).
2. Page calls getSiteHeaderContext to obtain current counts for quote list, comparison, reading list, and service inquiry.
3. Page calls getPrivacyPolicyContent to retrieve the privacy policy title, last_updated value, sections array, and privacy_contact_email.
4. The page header context component displays counts from getSiteHeaderContext.
5. The policy content manager component displays the policy title, last_updated, and each section (using heading and body fields).
6. If a section URL parameter is present, the corresponding section is highlighted and scrolled into view; the section TOC reflects the active section.
7. If no section URL parameter is present, the first section in the sections array is treated as the default active section.

Workflow: Jumping to a specific privacy section from within the page
1. User clicks a section entry in the table of contents (TOC).
2. The click handler determines the associated section identifier (derived from the section.heading).
3. The handler updates the browser URL query parameter (e.g., privacy.html?section=<derivedSectionId>) without reloading the page if possible.
4. The content manager updates the active section state based on the new section parameter.
5. The view scrolls to the selected section and highlights it in both the TOC and section list.

Workflow: Deep-linking to a specific privacy section from outside the page
1. User follows a link to privacy.html with a section query parameter (e.g., from email or another page).
2. On load, the privacy policy content manager parses the section parameter from the URL.
3. After getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves, the manager derives identifiers for each section from their headings and matches the URL parameter to the appropriate section.
4. If a matching section is found, the page scrolls to and highlights that section; if not, the default behavior is used (first section as active).

Workflow: Contacting the company about privacy via email
1. User sees the privacy_contact_email value rendered in the privacy contact component.
2. User clicks the displayed email address.
3. The click handler triggers a mail action (e.g., using a mailto link based on the privacy_contact_email returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent).
4. Optionally, the component can also expose an alternate action that navigates to contact.html with URL parameters including a reason indicating a privacy-related inquiry and a ref pointing back to privacy.html.

Workflow: Navigating to the contact page for privacy questions
1. User clicks a "Contact us about your data" link provided in the privacy contact component.
2. The click handler constructs a target URL to contact.html with query parameters such as reason=privacy and ref=privacy.html (and optionally including the currently active section id for additional context).
3. Browser navigates to the constructed contact.html URL.
4. The contact page can then interpret the reason and ref parameters to pre-fill or annotate the contact form (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow: Header context usage from the privacy page
1. User views the privacy page while having existing items in quote lists, comparisons, reading lists, or service inquiries.
2. On load, the header context component calls getSiteHeaderContext.
3. The component displays the returned counts and totals in header icons or summary text.
4. When the user clicks those header elements (e.g., quote list icon), they are navigated to the corresponding page (e.g., quote_list.html) using URL-based navigation with appropriate parameters (e.g., no additional parameters or with ref=privacy.html).
interactions: On page load: Trigger parallel calls to getSiteHeaderContext and getPrivacyPolicyContent. Once data is resolved, render header context metrics and privacy policy content. Handle loading and error states by showing appropriate generic indicators while avoiding hardcoded policy content.

Section TOC item click: When a user clicks a section entry in the TOC, prevent full page reload if possible, update the URL's section query parameter to a derived identifier, update active section state, and scroll to the relevant section.

Hash or section parameter change (via browser navigation): When the user uses the browser's back/forward buttons and the URL section parameter changes, re-compute the active section based on the new parameter and update highlighting and scroll position.

Click on privacy contact email: When the user clicks the email address exposed from privacy_contact_email, initiate an email action (e.g., open the default email client) using that email value; do not hardcode any email.

Click on "Contact us about your data" link: Navigate to contact.html with query parameters indicating a privacy-related reason, preserving context such as the currently active privacy section and a reference back to this page.

Header icon clicks (quote, comparison, reading list, service inquiry): When users click header icons showing counts from getSiteHeaderContext, navigate to their respective pages using URL-based routes and include a ref parameter referencing privacy.html so the user can return easily.
page_functionality.state_logic:
This page uses URL query parameters to manage navigational and interaction state, without relying on localStorage.

1. section parameter:
- Name: section
- Location: privacy.html?section=<sectionId>
- Purpose: Indicates the currently active/highlighted section of the privacy policy.
- Derivation: sectionId is derived at runtime from the section.heading returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent (for example by lowercasing, trimming, and replacing non-alphanumeric characters; the exact transformation is implementation-specific but must be computed dynamically).
- Behavior on load: If section is present, after getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves, the page attempts to match section to a derived identifier for one of the sections. If matched, that section becomes active and the view scrolls to it. If no match is found, the page falls back to using the first section as active.
- Behavior on change: When section changes via internal navigation or browser back/forward, the active section state is recomputed and the view updates accordingly.

2. ref parameter for outgoing navigation:
- When navigating from privacy.html to other pages (e.g., contact.html, quote_list.html), the page may append ref=privacy.html or ref=privacy.html%3Fsection%3D<sectionId> to indicate return context. This ref value is used by other pages and does not affect functionality on privacy.html itself.

3. reason parameter for contact navigation:
- When linking to contact.html for privacy-related questions, the page may include reason=privacy in the URL (e.g., contact.html?reason=privacy&ref=privacy.html). This informs the contact page of the inquiry type. The privacy page does not process this parameter itself.

4. No persistent client storage:
- All state necessary for navigation and deep-linking (e.g., active section) is derived directly from the URL and API responses. No localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies are used by this page's logic for internal state management.

5. Header context state:
- Header counts and totals are not encoded in the URL. They are fetched on each page load via getSiteHeaderContext and are treated as transient UI state without persistence in URL or localStorage.

components.functionality: Fetches and displays summary counts and totals for the user's quote list, product comparison, reading list, and service inquiry in the header area of the privacy policy page.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when the page loads. Invokes getSiteHeaderContext with no parameters, then binds quote_list_item_count, quote_list_total_value, quote_list_currency, comparison_item_count, reading_list_item_count, and service_inquiry_item_count to header context UI elements.
onQuoteIconClick: Navigates the user to the quote list page (e.g., quote_list.html) including a ref parameter referencing privacy.html or privacy.html with the current section parameter.
onComparisonIconClick: Navigates the user to the comparison page (e.g., compare.html) with ref=privacy.html or including the current section parameter.
onReadingListIconClick: Navigates the user to a reading list page (e.g., reading_list.html) with ref=privacy.html or including the current section parameter.
onServiceInquiryIconClick: Navigates the user to the service inquiry page (e.g., service_inquiry.html) with ref=privacy.html or including the current section parameter.

components.functionality: Central controller that loads the privacy policy content, manages loading and error states, exposes policy data to child components, and interprets URL parameters for section-level navigation.
event_handlers: onInit: Upon page load, reads the section query parameter from the URL (if present), invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent to retrieve title, last_updated, sections, and privacy_contact_email, and then computes a derived identifier for each section based on its heading. It determines the active section using either the URL parameter or default logic (first section). It passes the policy data and activeSectionId as props/state to child components.

onSectionParamChange: Listens for changes in the section query parameter (via browser navigation events). When detected, recomputes activeSectionId based on the new parameter, keeping the already-loaded policy content in memory, and not re-calling getPrivacyPolicyContent unless necessary (e.g., page reload).

onSectionChangeRequest: Receives requests from child components (e.g., TOC or in-content links) to switch to a particular section. Updates the URL's section parameter accordingly (using the derived identifier) and updates the activeSectionId state so that other components reflect the change and scroll behavior can be applied.

onReloadRequest: Provides an optional mechanism to manually re-trigger getPrivacyPolicyContent if a refresh is needed (e.g., user-triggered refresh button), while respecting the same logic for section parameter handling.

components.functionality: Displays the privacy policy title and last updated information using the data returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataChange: When privacy policy data is received or updated by privacy-policy-loader, this handler updates the displayed title and last_updated fields, ensuring that no static text is used in place of dynamic content.

components.functionality: Renders a table of contents listing all privacy policy sections and allows users to jump between sections while keeping the URL parameter and active state synchronized.
event_handlers: onInit: Receives the sections array and derived section identifiers from privacy-policy-loader and renders a list of clickable items representing each section.heading.

onSectionItemClick: When a TOC item is clicked, calls privacy-policy-loader.onSectionChangeRequest with the associated sectionId. This updates the URL's section parameter and triggers active section changes and scrolling.
onActiveSectionChange: Updates visual indication (e.g., highlight) of the active section in the TOC when activeSectionId changes due to URL parameter changes or other interactions.

components.functionality: Displays the full privacy policy content by iterating through the sections array and rendering each section.heading and section.body. Handles scrolling and highlighting of the active section.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataChange: When sections are provided or updated by privacy-policy-loader, renders each section dynamically based on heading and body fields, without introducing any hardcoded section text.
onActiveSectionChange: When activeSectionId changes, scrolls the corresponding section into view and highlights it, ensuring the user can see which part of the policy is currently active.

onInContentLinkClick: If the body content contains references to other sections (e.g., via anchors or structured links), this handler relays a section change request to privacy-policy-loader.onSectionChangeRequest without hardcoding target identifiers.

components.functionality: Provides mechanisms for users to initiate privacy-related inquiries, using the privacy_contact_email from the policy content and links to the contact page.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataChange: When privacy_contact_email is retrieved from getPrivacyPolicyContent, binds it to the displayed email link dynamically. No email address is hardcoded in the component.
onEmailClick: When the user clicks the displayed privacy_contact_email, triggers an email client action (for example using a dynamically constructed mailto link based on the returned email).

onContactPageLinkClick: Navigates the user to contact.html, appending query parameters such as reason=privacy, ref=privacy.html, and optionally section=<activeSectionId> obtained from privacy-policy-loader so that the contact page can tailor the form to a privacy-related inquiry.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width grid, approximately 2-column layout within a centered 900–980px container. Not a modern 12-column system, but conceptually a main-content column (~70–75%) and a left sidebar (~25–30%) with occasional full-width header and navigation rows.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit inside the existing fixed-width, centered page container, in the main (right) column of the two-column content band. The global layout remains: full-width logo/banner strip at the top, full-width navigation bar below it, then the two-column band with a narrow left site-wide sidebar and a wider right main-content column. Within that main column, the privacy‑related components stack linearly from top to bottom in this order: header-context-bar, privacy-header-block, privacy-toc, privacy-section-list, and finally the privacy-contact-block. The privacy-policy-loader appears transiently, centered within the main content column as an overlay or inline row while data is loading, without changing the linear ordering of the other components.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit inside the existing fixed-width, centered page container, in the main (right) column of the two-column content band. The global layout remains: full-width logo/banner strip at the top, full-width navigation bar below it, then the two-column band with a narrow left site-wide sidebar and a wider right main-content column. Within that main column, the privacy‑related components stack linearly from top to bottom in this order: header-context-bar, privacy-header-block, privacy-toc, privacy-section-list, and finally the privacy-contact-block. The privacy-policy-loader appears transiently, centered within the main content column as an overlay or inline row while data is loading, without changing the linear ordering of the other components.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header and navigation occupy the top 20–25% of the viewport as per the mockup. The remaining height is the content band. Horizontally, about 25–30% of the interior width is the global left sidebar (not part of these components), and about 70–75% is the main privacy content column that contains all six components. Within that main column, roughly 5–8% of its vertical space is used by the header-context-bar, 5–8% by the privacy-header-block, 10–15% by the privacy-toc, 65–70% by the privacy-section-list (the long policy body), and 8–12% by the privacy-contact-block at the bottom. Internal padding for each block is compact (8–12px), and the vertical gaps between these modules follow the 8–16px step from the spacing system.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page preserves the mockup’s information-dense, utilitarian feel. Paragraphs within the privacy sections are long with tight vertical spacing (around 8px between paragraphs). The header-context-bar and privacy-header-block are compact with minimal breathing room around text. The privacy-toc packs section links closely together in a bordered box with small padding. The privacy-section-list follows the reference pattern from the mockup: each section starts with a blue heading bar followed by one or more dense text blocks with minimal spacing. The privacy-contact-block has slightly more padding to differentiate it as an action-oriented area but still uses the conservative 8–12px internal padding, not large white-space breaks.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows the same F-pattern as in the mockup. Users first scan the top-left logo and company name, move right across the banner and navigation, then drop into the main content column. Within the privacy content, the eye first catches the horizontal header-context-bar at the top of the main column, then moves down to the larger, bold title in the privacy-header-block. From there, attention shifts slightly left and downward to the structured list of links in the privacy-toc, and then continues vertically through the dense privacy-section-list. Red highlights in links or key phrases in the TOC and contact block act as secondary attractors, while the blue section headings in the section list create a consistent vertical rhythm guiding the user down the page toward the privacy-contact-block at the bottom.

layout.overall_description: Within the familiar boxed corporate shell, the Privacy Policy page appears in the right-hand main column beneath the full-width logo/banner and navigation. A narrow global left sidebar remains visible, but the user’s attention is pulled to a modest horizontal context bar at the very top of the main column, aligned with the top of the content band. Directly beneath it, a compact but prominent privacy header block introduces the page with a strong blue title and a small update date line. Below that, a light, bordered table-of-contents box spans the width of the main column, packed with internal links to policy sections. Under the TOC, the bulk of the screen is occupied by tightly stacked policy sections: each begins with a full-width blue horizontal heading strip and an icon on the left, followed by dense paragraphs of text that extend almost to the bottom of the viewport. At the very end of the column, a slightly more padded contact block stretches across the main column, visually anchored to the bottom of the privacy content band. The entire composition feels compact and text-heavy, with modest vertical gaps and consistent left alignment, preserving the conservative, utilitarian character of the original mockup while organizing the privacy information clearly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-context-bar sits at the top of the main content column, directly below the site-wide navigation bar and aligned to the right of the global left sidebar. It appears as a low-profile horizontal band spanning the full width of the main column (approximately 70–75% of the overall page width). The bar has a subtle blue or gray-tinted background with a slight beveled edge to echo the skeuomorphic navigation style. Its height is modest—about 32–40px, with 8px padding on the top and bottom and 12px left padding. The primary text (e.g., ‘You are viewing: Privacy Policy’ or breadcrumb-style context) is left-aligned, with any secondary elements (such as a small icon or status tag) positioned toward the right, but still within the same band. The vertical gap between the navigation bar and the header-context-bar is minimal (around 8px), and there is a slightly larger spacing (12–16px) between this bar and the privacy-header-block below, maintaining the tightly stacked, banded structure from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the main content column; scrolls away with the page and does not stick or float. Its width and position are fixed relative to the boxed page container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-loader is positioned within the main content column, logically associated with the area occupied by the privacy-section-list. When active, it appears centered horizontally in the main column, slightly below the privacy-header-block and overlapping or temporarily replacing the initial part of the privacy-section-list. Visually, it presents as either a semi-opaque white overlay covering the section-list area (with a compact, 200–260px wide loading panel centered both horizontally and within the visible portion of that area), or as a narrow inline strip (about 32–40px tall) directly under the privacy-header-block and above the privacy-toc. In either case, it respects the fixed-width column: the loader never exceeds the main column’s width and maintains 12–16px of vertical separation from adjacent blocks. When inactive, it collapses completely so that no additional vertical space is consumed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces part of the privacy-section-list’s area while data is loading. It is positioned relative to the main column (not the whole viewport), and scrolls normally with the page; no sticky or fixed behavior is used.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-header-block sits immediately below the header-context-bar, with a vertical separation of roughly 12px. It spans the full width of the main content column and uses a card-like white background against the slightly patterned page backdrop. Internally it has 12px padding on all sides. At the top left of this block is the main title ‘Privacy Policy’ rendered in a strong corporate blue, using a slightly larger font size than section headings and styled similarly to the main article headers in the mockup. Below the title, aligned to the left and separated by 4–8px, is a smaller line for metadata such as ‘Last updated: [date]’, optionally accompanied by a small icon. No large imagery is included; the emphasis is on text. The block’s overall height is compact—typically 64–96px depending on content—but feels dense, not spacious. A 12–16px vertical gap separates the bottom of this block from the top of the privacy-toc, reinforcing the page’s stacked band structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky block within the main column. It maintains its full-width alignment as the user scrolls and does not change size or position dynamically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-toc sits directly beneath the privacy-header-block, separated by about 12–16px. It appears as a framed module spanning the full width of the main content column, visually similar to a sidebar box but placed inside the main article area. The TOC has a light background (white or very pale blue) with a thin border that echoes other boxed elements on the site and 8–12px of internal padding. A small, bold subheading like ‘On this page’ may appear at the top left of the box, followed by a vertical list of section links. These links are left-aligned, stacked closely with minimal vertical gap (~4–6px), and may be grouped into two narrow columns inside the box if the list is long, respecting the dense, information-heavy look. Occasional red highlights (e.g., for the current section) add visual accents without disrupting the conservative aesthetic. The TOC’s height will grow with content but is kept visually compact, typically occupying about 10–15% of the main content column’s vertical area on initial view. Below it, a 12–16px gap leads into the privacy-section-list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that scrolls with the content. It does not stick to the viewport while scrolling (in line with the non-modern, non-dynamic navigation style of the mockup).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-section-list occupies the majority of the main column beneath the privacy-toc, with a vertical offset of about 12–16px between them. It spans the full width of the main content column, presenting a series of stacked policy sections that mirror the reference-entry pattern from the mockup. Each section begins with a full-width blue heading bar or strong heading line: on the left, a small icon (such as a document or shield) sits next to the section title in white or dark-blue text, aligning with the start of the paragraph text below. This heading bar is about 32–40px tall with 8px vertical padding. Directly beneath each heading bar are one or more dense paragraphs of policy text, left-aligned, stretching nearly across the column with 8px spacing between paragraphs and only 12px padding at left/right margins. Sections are separated vertically by modest gaps (12–16px) so that the entire list reads as a continuous, information-heavy document, not as isolated panels. No large illustrations are included; if any small icons or labels appear inline, they are kept small and aligned with the text baseline. This block usually extends beyond the initial viewport height, forming the long scrollable core of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flowing content within the main column. It scrolls normally and expands vertically depending on the amount of dynamic policy content, without any fixed or sticky elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-contact-block is positioned at the bottom of the main content column, immediately following the last section in the privacy-section-list, with a 16px vertical gap to distinguish it as a concluding module. It spans the full width of the main column and appears as a slightly emphasized card, using either a very light gray or pale blue background to subtly stand out from the pure-white content area, while still matching the conservative corporate palette. Internal padding is slightly more generous than other blocks—around 12px on all sides—yet still feels compact. Inside, a small blue heading such as ‘Questions about your privacy?’ or ‘Contact our data protection team’ is left-aligned at the top, followed by one or two short paragraphs of explanatory text and a dense set of contact options (email address, phone, possibly a small inline link to a contact form). These contact details are tightly stacked, with 4–8px vertical spacing, and may use red highlights for key items like the primary email address to echo other red accents on the site. The block visually anchors the end of the privacy content and aligns with the top edge of the footer area that follows the content band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block at the end of the main column. It scrolls with the rest of the page and does not attach to the bottom of the viewport or behave as a sticky footer.

name: Terms of Use
title: Terms of Use
description: Displays the website’s Terms of Use content, including sections related to tools, calculators, technical documentation, intellectual property, and change notifications, and shows header context counts for quote list, comparisons, reading list, and service inquiries.

core_features: Load and display the structured Terms of Use content (title, last updated date, sections) via getTermsOfUseContent.
Show current header context counts (quote list items and total, comparison items, reading list items, service inquiry items) via getSiteHeaderContext.
Provide in-page navigation to jump between Terms of Use sections using anchors derived from the returned section headings.
Support deep-linking to a specific Terms of Use section using a URL parameter (e.g., terms.html?section=<section-slug>) and automatically focus/highlight that section on load.
Allow users to copy or navigate to direct links for specific sections by updating the URL parameter and browser history when a section link is clicked.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Reading the Terms of Use
1. User opens terms.html (typically via footer link).
2. Page calls getSiteHeaderContext and renders the header icons and counts.
3. Page calls getTermsOfUseContent and renders the title, last updated date, and full list of sections in order.
4. User scrolls through sections or uses the in-page navigation component to jump to a specific section.
5. If user wants a direct link to a section, they click the section in the in-page navigation; the URL is updated with the corresponding section parameter and can then be shared.

Workflow: Opening a deep-linked Terms section
1. User navigates to terms.html with a section URL parameter (e.g., terms.html?section=<some-slug>), typically from a shared link or another page.
2. Page loads and calls getSiteHeaderContext to render header context and getTermsOfUseContent to load sections.
3. After sections are loaded, the page derives slugs from section headings and locates the section whose slug matches the section parameter.
4. The matching section is scrolled into view and visually highlighted according to functional rules of the section viewer component.
5. The user may then navigate to other sections via the in-page navigation or clear the section parameter (e.g., by clicking a “All sections” link if implemented) to view the document without a focused section.

Workflow: Navigating from Terms to other functional pages
1. User is on terms.html and sees header icons with counts (quote list, comparison, reading list, service inquiry).
2. User clicks one of the header icons.
3. Based on the clicked icon, the page navigates to the corresponding page URL (e.g., quote list, comparison page, reading list page, or service inquiry page), using URL parameters where needed (e.g., quote_list.html, comparison.html, reading_list.html, service_inquiry.html).
4. The new page initializes its own state and loads its data; when the user navigates back to terms.html, getSiteHeaderContext is called again to refresh counts.
interactions: On initial page load, the page invokes getSiteHeaderContext with no parameters and binds its response to the header context component (quote list item count and total, comparison item count, reading list item count, service inquiry item count).
On initial page load, the page invokes getTermsOfUseContent with no parameters and binds the returned title, last_updated, and sections array to the content components.

When the user clicks a section entry in the in-page navigation component, the page updates the section URL parameter (using history.pushState or similar), scrolls to the corresponding section element, and highlights it.
When the user clicks a section’s internal anchor link (e.g., copy link or heading anchor), the page ensures the URL section parameter reflects that section’s slug, enabling sharable deep links.

When the URL section parameter changes (due to navigation actions or browser back/forward), the page re-resolves the matching section from the current sections array and scrolls/focuses it if present.
When the user clicks the quote list header icon, the page navigates to the quote list page URL using standard navigation (e.g., window.location = 'quote_list.html').
When the user clicks the comparison header icon, the page navigates to the product comparison page URL.
When the user clicks the reading list header icon, the page navigates to the reading list page URL.
When the user clicks the service inquiry header icon, the page navigates to the service inquiry page URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Terms of Use page is primarily controlled by URL state and API responses. It does not use localStorage.

URL parameters:
- section: Optional. A slug identifier for a specific Terms of Use section. On load, after getTermsOfUseContent returns sections, the page derives a slug for each section based on its heading (e.g., by normalizing the heading string) and compares those slugs to the section parameter. If a match is found, the corresponding section is scrolled into view and functionally marked as active (highlighted). If no match is found, all sections are displayed with no active highlight.

State behavior:
- On first load of terms.html without parameters, both getSiteHeaderContext and getTermsOfUseContent are called. All sections are rendered in order, and the header context shows the current counts. No section is auto-focused.
- On first load of terms.html with section=<slug>, both interfaces are called. After sections are bound, the in-page nav / section viewer components locate the section with the matching slug, scroll to it, and set it as the active section.
- When a user clicks an item in the in-page navigation, the component updates the URL query parameter section to the selected section’s slug and triggers a scroll into view for that section. The active section state is derived solely from the current section parameter and the headings returned from getTermsOfUseContent.
- Browser back/forward navigation is handled by listening to URL changes (e.g., popstate). When the section parameter changes due to navigation history actions, the page recomputes which section is active and scrolls accordingly.
- Header counts are not directly modified by interactions on this page; any changes occur when the user navigates to other pages that affect quote lists, comparisons, reading lists, or service inquiries. Returning to terms.html will result in a new call to getSiteHeaderContext, ensuring the header context is always based on the latest server-side state.

components.functionality: Fetches aggregated header context (quote list item count and total value, comparison item count, reading list item count, service inquiry item count) using getSiteHeaderContext and exposes it to header-related components on the page.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: calls getSiteHeaderContext and stores the returned object in a shared headerContext state.
onHeaderContextRefresh: optional manual or lifecycle-triggered re-fetch of getSiteHeaderContext (e.g., when returning to this page).

components.functionality: Displays global navigation and header icons for quote list, product comparison, reading list, and service inquiry using the data provided by site-header-context-provider.
event_handlers: onQuoteIconClick: navigates to the quote list page URL (e.g., quote_list.html).
onComparisonIconClick: navigates to the product comparison page URL (e.g., comparison.html).
onReadingListIconClick: navigates to the reading list page URL (e.g., reading_list.html).
onServiceInquiryIconClick: navigates to the service inquiry page URL (e.g., service_inquiry.html).

components.functionality: Loads the Terms of Use document structure (title, last updated date, and sections with headings and bodies) using getTermsOfUseContent and provides it to terms-related components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: calls getTermsOfUseContent and stores title, last_updated, and sections in local state.
onReloadRequested: optional re-fetch of getTermsOfUseContent if needed (for example, if user triggers a manual refresh action).

components.functionality: Displays the Terms of Use title and last updated information using the data from terms-content-provider.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: reads title and last_updated from the provider’s state and renders them in the page heading area.

components.functionality: Renders an in-page table of contents listing each Terms of Use section based on the headings from getTermsOfUseContent; allows users to click to navigate to and focus a specific section and updates the URL section parameter accordingly.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: generates a slug for each section heading and builds the TOC entries using the sections array.

onTocItemClick: given a selected section, updates the section URL parameter to that section’s slug (using history.pushState or similar) and emits an event (e.g., onActiveSectionChange) with the selected slug.
onUrlSectionParamChange: when notified of a change to the section query parameter (including on initial load), updates the active entry in the TOC to match the current parameter.

components.functionality: Displays the full Terms of Use sections sequentially, each with an anchor ID derived from its heading; listens to the current section slug to scroll the matching section into view and visually mark it as active.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: renders all sections in order, generates a slug for each heading, and assigns it as a DOM id or data attribute for deep linking.

onActiveSectionChange: receives a section slug (e.g., from URLSectionStateManager or terms-section-toc) and, if a section with that slug exists, scrolls to that section and applies active styling logic.
onHeadingAnchorClick: when a user clicks a section’s heading anchor, updates the section URL parameter to the corresponding slug (without reloading the page) so that the URL can be shared.

components.functionality: Manages synchronization between the URL section parameter and the active section state used by terms-section-toc and terms-section-viewer.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: reads the initial section query parameter and emits an initial active section slug (if present) to subscribers (e.g., terms-section-viewer and terms-section-toc).
onPopState: listens for browser back/forward actions and updates the active section slug based on the current section parameter, notifying subscribers.

onSetActiveSectionRequest: when terms-section-toc or terms-section-viewer requests a change to the active section, updates the URL section parameter (using history.pushState) and broadcasts the new slug.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width grid, approximately 2-column layout within a centered 900–980px container. Not a modern 12-column system, but conceptually a main-content column (~70–75%) and a left sidebar (~25–30%) with occasional full-width header and navigation rows.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the classic boxed layout: a full-width patterned background containing a centered, fixed-width (≈940–960px) white page container. Inside: a stacked header band for logo/banner and navigation, then a two-column content band where the left column holds the in-page terms table of contents and the right column holds the terms title and section content viewer. Data and state components are non-visual wrappers around these visible structures.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the classic boxed layout: a full-width patterned background containing a centered, fixed-width (≈940–960px) white page container. Inside: a stacked header band for logo/banner and navigation, then a two-column content band where the left column holds the in-page terms table of contents and the right column holds the terms title and section content viewer. Data and state components are non-visual wrappers around these visible structures.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate roughly the top 15–20% of the initial viewport height to the site header bar (logo/banner + navigation + header context counts). Immediately below, a horizontal rule or subtle shadow marks the start of the main content band, which consumes the remaining 80–85% of the viewport. Within this band, reserve about 25–30% of the content width for the left sidebar (terms-section-toc) and about 70–75% for the main column (terms-title-block + terms-section-viewer). Vertical spacing between modules follows the compact system: 8–12px between related elements (title and first section, section heading and its paragraphs) and 12–16px between larger blocks (between sections).
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain an information-dense, utilitarian feel: compact line heights, long paragraphs with minimal vertical separation, and tightly stacked links in the sidebar. The table of contents items should be close together with modest 4–8px vertical spacing. The main content viewer uses clear section headers but keeps paragraphs visually tight (small top and bottom margins). Padding inside any boxed areas (sidebar module, main content card) is modest (≈8–12px on all sides).
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve an F-pattern. The eye hits the logo/company name at the top left of the header, scans right across the header navigation and header context counts, then drops down to the start of the content band. From there, the gaze naturally passes over the left-column table of contents but quickly anchors on the bold blue page title at the top of the right column, then moves down through the terms sections in the main column. Within each section, the blue section headings and any subtle icons act as anchors, followed by dense text. The left sidebar TOC links and any red-highlighted active item serve as secondary attractors during vertical scrolling.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a centered, fixed-width white container (around 940–960px wide) on top of a patterned, slightly textured full-width background. At the very top of this container, a relatively tall header band spans the full width: logo and company name on the left, any supporting banner graphics stretching to the right, and beneath them a compact horizontal navigation row with a subdued gradient or beveled look. On the far right of this navigation row, small, tightly spaced indicators show counts for quotes, comparisons, reading list, and service inquiries, aligned to form a dense utility cluster. Below the header, a subtle dividing line or shadow introduces the main content band. This band is split into two columns: a narrower left sidebar and a wider right main column. The left sidebar begins with a small boxed area containing the Terms of Use table of contents: a compact heading and a vertical list of section links with minimal space between them, packed in a card-like panel. To its right, aligned at the same top level, the main column starts with the Terms of Use page title in bold blue, spanning the full width of this column, followed immediately by the terms content viewer. The viewer presents each section as a horizontally full-width piece: there is a blue-tinted heading row (possibly with a small icon at the left), followed by tightly set paragraphs of legal and technical text, then a modest gap before the next section begins. Sections stack vertically down the page, giving a sense of continuous, dense information. Data loading and URL state management happen invisibly in the background and do not affect the visual structure. Everything scrolls together as a single, non-sticky document, closely mirroring an old-generation corporate reference page focused on utility over spacious aesthetics.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component does not render any visible UI. It sits logically at the very top of the page hierarchy, wrapping or preceding the site-header-bar. It has no visual footprint, occupies no explicit space in the fixed-width container, and does not introduce any margins or padding. Its presence is only structural, providing the data required to populate the header bar and its context counts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height and zero-width; it lives outside the visual flow and does not affect layout or scrolling behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The site header bar is the topmost visible band inside the centered, boxed page container. It spans 100% of the container width (≈940–960px) and occupies roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height when first loaded. The header band itself can be visually divided into two horizontal zones: the upper logo/banner zone and the lower navigation/utilities zone. The upper zone takes about two-thirds of the header height and contains the company logo and name aligned to the left, with any industrial imagery or banner graphics filling the remaining horizontal space to the right. A subtle gradient or textured background may run behind this area. Directly below, running full width, a navigation bar occupies about one-third of the header height. This bar has a slightly beveled or gradient effect and houses the main nav items arranged horizontally from left to right, each with tight horizontal spacing (≈8–12px padding per side) and minimal vertical padding (≈4–6px). The active menu item uses a red highlight consistent with the overall design DNA. On the far right of this navigation row, in the same horizontal line, compact context indicators appear: small icons or labels for quote list, comparisons, reading list, and service inquiries, each with a numeric count. These are grouped tightly with modest gaps (≈8px) between them, forming a dense utility cluster. The entire header band sits flush with the top of the page container and the content below starts immediately after a thin bottom border or shadow line. The header scrolls away with the page; it is not sticky or fixed on scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the document flow, full-width inside the fixed container, scrolling normally with the page. Height is fixed relative to its contents, not viewport-proportional, but visually sized to feel like about 15–20% of the initial viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is a non-visual data layer wrapping the terms page content. It sits conceptually just below the site-header-bar and around the terms-title-block, terms-section-toc, and terms-section-viewer, but it does not render any visible element or occupy any explicit space. From a layout perspective, it is transparent: the two-column content band starts immediately below the header as if this provider were not there.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-size wrapper with no impact on layout or scroll position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms title block appears at the top of the right-hand main content column within the two-column content band. The content band itself begins directly below the site header bar, separated by a modest vertical gap (≈12–16px) or a thin dividing line. Inside this band, the title block is aligned to the top of the main column, horizontally positioned so that its left edge aligns with the vertical boundary between the sidebar and the main area. It spans the full width of the main column (approximately 70–75% of the container width). Vertical spacing above the title is modest: around 8–12px from the top boundary of the content band. The page title text (e.g., “Terms of Use”) is set in a bold, relatively large font, using the characteristic blue color of the site’s reference headings. Beneath the title, a small subtitle or short description (if present) is set in normal weight, closely spaced (≈4–8px below the main title) to keep the dense feel. The entire title block occupies a low vertical profile—just enough to contain one or two lines of text—before the terms-section-viewer content begins, with about 8–12px of gap between the title block and the first section heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the main content column, part of the normal flow. It does not stick or float; as the user scrolls, the title moves out of view along with the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms section table of contents forms the left sidebar column of the main content band. Starting directly below the site-header-bar and aligned with the top of the terms-title-block, it occupies approximately 25–30% of the total content width. The TOC is placed inside a visually distinct but modestly styled box: a white or very light background, a thin grey border, and perhaps a faint inner gradient or bevel to match the skeuomorphic style. The box has small internal padding (≈8–12px on all sides). At the top of this box, a small heading (e.g., “On this page” or “Terms Sections”) appears in a slightly stronger blue text, with about 4–8px space below it. Below that, the TOC entries are presented as a vertical list of links, each representing a section of the terms. The links are tightly stacked with minimal vertical separation (≈4–6px between items), creating a compact column of navigational text. Active or currently viewed sections may be highlighted with a red accent (e.g., red text or a small red indicator) consistent with the site’s use of red for active states. The TOC box extends downward as needed, typically covering most or all of the visible height of the main content band. It remains aligned to the left edge of the page container with a small gap (≈12–16px) between its right edge and the start of the main content column, reinforcing the two-column structure. The entire TOC scrolls with the page, staying in its relative position next to the section content; it is not fixed or sticky.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block forming the left column of a two-column layout. It scrolls normally with the rest of the content. Width is fixed relative to the overall container (≈25–30%), and height grows with its contents.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms section viewer is the dominant area in the right-hand main column of the two-column band. It sits immediately below the terms-title-block, sharing the same column width (≈70–75% of the container) and left alignment. There is a modest vertical gap (≈8–12px) between the bottom of the title block and the top of the first section. Each section within the viewer follows the reference pattern from the mockup: at the top of a section, a horizontal heading strip spans the full width of the main column. This strip uses a blue text heading, optionally sitting on a slightly tinted or gradient blue bar, with a small icon or symbol aligned to the left of the heading text. The heading strip has minimal vertical padding (≈4–8px) to keep it compact. Immediately below each heading, the section’s legal and technical content is presented as one or more dense paragraphs. Paragraphs use a relatively tight line height and only small vertical margins (≈4–8px between paragraphs), so the text appears compact and information-heavy. Where lists or bullet points appear, they are indented slightly but keep tight spacing between items. After the last paragraph of a section, there is a modest vertical gap (≈12–16px) before the following section’s heading strip begins, creating a clear but not spacious separation. The overall viewer content flows vertically down the page, likely extending beyond the initial viewport and requiring scroll. The right edge of this column aligns with the right edge of the page container, reinforcing the boxed layout. No horizontal scrolling is introduced. Any internal anchors or highlighting triggered by the TOC are reflected within this area, but there is no floating behavior—the content simply scrolls linearly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Main scrollable content area in the right column. It participates in normal document flow, with height determined by the amount of terms content. Anchored navigation from the TOC moves the scroll position within this viewer but does not change its layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component manages synchronization between the URL (e.g., hash or query parameters) and the currently active section in the terms-section-viewer and terms-section-toc. It does not create any visible elements, borders, or spacing. It sits logically alongside or around the TOC and viewer, but from a layout standpoint it is transparent: its presence does not change the size, position, or spacing of any visible component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-space component that only affects navigation state. It does not influence scrolling, positioning, or sizing of visible UI elements.
